protocol_hierarchical.3.1: Remove sealed bag containing the slide from -80°C storage and allow to reach room temperature for 5 minutes. NOTE: If the cold slide is immediately exposed to the laboratory atmosphere, the tissue will become coated with condensation, and the spatial integrity of the drug may be compromised.
protocol_hierarchical.3.2: Turn on the microscope and laser (laser requires 5 - 10 minutes warm up before cutting can commence). Load flat-cap 0.20 mL PCR tubes into the holder.
protocol_hierarchical.3.3: Remove slide from the bag and take an optical image of the tissue section on the PET slide using a flatbed scanner.
protocol_hierarchical.3.4: Place the slide into the slide holder (tissue side facing down) and assign separate collection tubes to specific granuloma regions of interest using the microscope software. Typically, these will be 'uninvolved lung,' 'cellular granuloma,' and 'caseum' (necrotic center), but may vary depending upon the specific pathology of the granuloma/biopsy.

3.5.title: Focus on the tissue using the 5X microscope objective. This magnification should provide a good overview of the tissue containing both cellular and necrotic granuloma areas. In the software select the tube designated 'caseum' to move it into position under the tissue. (arbitrary units). However, these will differ depending upon the microscope used and potential declining power due to the age of the laser. 7. Select the 'free-draw' tool and, using either a mouse or touchscreen pen, outline the desired region for dissection. The surface area of the region will be displayed in the software. Keep selected regions under 500,000 mu m2(0.5m m2) to facilitate easier dissection. Repeat the dissection until 3 million mu m2(3m m2) have been collected in total in the tube cap.

protocol_hierarchical.3.5.1: On occasion, the dissected region may remain stuck to the surrounding membrane (for example due to static attraction) and not fall into the collection cap. Remove these regions from the cumulative surface area total by selecting and removing manually within the software.
protocol_hierarchical.3.6: Select the cap for 'cellular lesion' and collect 3 million A m2 of tissue using the same process as outlined in step 3.7.
protocol_hierarchical.3.7: Select the cap for 'uninvolved lung' and collect 3 million A m2 of tissue using the same process as outlined in step 3.7. Note that uninvolved lung tissue contains many bronchioles and alveolar spaces. Pay careful attention to exclude these from the defined tissue regions for dissection.
protocol_hierarchical.3.8: Remove the cap holder and carefully unclip, seal and label each tube. Protect the dissected tissues from surrounding air disturbances (such as from air flow disruption from an opening door). Analyze the dissected tissues immediately, or store at -80°C and thaw prior to processing and LCMS analysis.
protocol_hierarchical.4.1: Prepare extraction solution of 1:1 acetonitrile/methanol containing Ethambutol d-10 internal standard. When selecting an internal standard, use a stable labeled form of the analyte drug (such as deuterium-labeled EMB used in this demonstration) with sufficient mass shift to avoid isotope cross talk between the analyte drug and standard (usually a minimum of 4 daltons). of the study sample tissue sections is combined directly with an amount of the test compound that would be present at a given concentration. 2. Calculate the targeted tissue volume based on the surface area and thickness of the tissue section and determine the necessary dilution factor for the homogenate using the volume of homogenate that will be added to the standard and QC samples. Calculations are illustrated below for a 3 million um2 ( 3mm2) target dissected area with a 25um thickness and 2uL volume of homogenate. Target Tissue Volume = Target Dissection Area × Slice Thickness Target Tissue Volu m e=3,000,000 mum2×25 mum×frac1 muL1,000,000,000 mum3 = 0.075 muL Homogenate Dilution Factor = Homogenate Volume  Target Volume Homogenate Dilution Facto r=2u L0.075u L=26.67
protocol_hierarchical.4.2: Assuming a tissue density of 1 /mL , prepare the homogenate stock by weighing 50mg of control tissue and adding PBS buffer to dilute (using the 26.67 homogenate dilution factor calculated in step 4.2, the diluent is 1.283mL ). Homogenize by bead beating lung tissue and PBS buffer for 5 minutes at 1750 rpm on a bead homogenizer. (26.67-1)×50 mg×fracg1000mg×frac1 mLg=1.283mL
protocol_hierarchical.4.3: Dilute 1mg/mL drug stocks concentration in 1:1 acetonitrile/water to create standard curve spiking solutions. Determine spiking standard concentrations based on the spike volume and the target tissue volume. The illustrated example is for a 100n g/m L standard using a 10 µL spike volume.
protocol_hierarchical.4.4: Remove the tubes containing the microdissected tissues from -80°C storage and allow to reach room temperature for 5 minutes.
protocol_hierarchical.4.5: Add 6.Add 10 μL of 1:1 acetonitrile/water solution and 2 μL of PBS buffer to the tubes containing microdissected tissue. of 1:1 acetonitrile/water solution and of PBS buffer to the tubes containing microdissected tissue.
protocol_hierarchical.4.6: For standard curve and quality control tubes, add 10uL of spiking solution to 2uL of control lung homogenate.
protocol_hierarchical.4.7: Add 50uL of extraction solution to each tube.
protocol_hierarchical.4.8: Vortex each tube for 5 minutes, sonicate for 5 minutes and centrifuge at 5000 RPM for 5 minutes to form a pellet of film and tissue in each tube.
protocol_hierarchical.4.9: Transfer 50uL of supernatant to a 96-well deep-well plate and dilute with an additional 50uL of deionized water in each well.
protocol_hierarchical.4.10: Perform LC/MS/MS analysis using optimized instrument parameters for Ethambutol and Ethambutol-d10 internal standard (as previously described in detail12).
protocol_hierarchical.4.11: Use a dilution factor to correct for the exact amount of tissue dissected for each sample. Dilution Factor = Target Area  Actual Area
protocol_hierarchical.5.1: Create a homogenate in control lung tissue by combining 1 part lung, 2 parts PBS, and 3 - 4 steel beads. Beat lung tissue and PBS buffer for 5 minutes at 1750 rpm using a bead homogenizer.
protocol_hierarchical.5.2: Spike the homogenate by adding 10uL of 1mg/mL Ethambutol DMSO stock into 990 µL homogenate to create a final concentration of 10,000n g/m L (10m g/m L) and vortex for 1 minute.
protocol_hierarchical.5.3: Create a frozen homogenate block by pouring the homogenate into a cryomold and rapidly freezing on dry ice for 5 minutes.
protocol_hierarchical.5.4: Prepare 25um thick sections from the homogenate block as described in steps 2.1 - 2.5.
protocol_hierarchical.5.5: Dissect the target tissue area as specified in steps 3.2-3.10 .
protocol_hierarchical.5.6: Add 10uL 1:1 acetonitrile/water and 2uL of PBS buffer to the tubes containing microdissected tissue.
protocol_hierarchical.5.7: Add 50uL of extraction solution to each tube. Follow steps 4.9-4.12 to create a standard curve and determine the drug concentration in the tissue homogenate block.
protocol_hierarchical.5.8: Calculate extraction efficiency using the formula below: Measured Drug Concentration in Homogenate Extraction Efficiency (%)= X100 Spiked Drug Concentration in Homogenate

problem: Need for accurate spatial quantification of drug concentrations within specific tissue compartments of pulmonary tuberculosis lesions to evaluate drug penetration and efficacy.

query: I want to achieve Second generation biofuels are derived from inedible lignocellulosic biomass of food and non-food crops.. What are the recommended experimental workflows for that goal?

ground_truth_answer.title: Saccharification Protocol for Small-scale Lignocellulosic Biomass Samples to Test Processing of Cellulose into Glucose
ground_truth_answer.abstract: Second generation biofuels are derived from inedible lignocellulosic biomass of food and non-food crops. Lignocellulosic biomass is mainly composed of cell walls that contain a large proportion of cellulosic and hemicellulosic polysaccharides. An interesting route to generate  biofuels  and  bio-based  materials  is  via  enzymatic  hydrolysis  of  cell  wall polysaccharides into fermentable sugars, a process called saccharification. The released sugars can then be fermented to fuels, e.g., by use of yeast.
ground_truth_answer.protocol_text: A.  Preparing the enzyme mixture1.  The enzyme mixture used in this protocol is a mix of cellulase and  -glucosidase (BG). These enzymes first need to be cleared from their stabilizing salts. This is done with a desalting Econo-Pac 10 DG column, fitted with Bio-gel® P-6DG gel, and can be used for both cellulase and BG.2.  First remove, by pouring off, the NaN3-solution that prevents growth of fungi on the column.3.  Bring ^20 m l of acetic acid buffer solution (p H4.8) on the column and let gravity work.4.  Mix 1 ml of enzyme (cellulase or BG) with _2ml acetic acid buffer solution (p H4.8) in a Corning® 15ml centrifuge tube. Bring this mix on the column and let it run through the column. Remove the 3ml that comes off the column.5.  Add 4ml acetic acid buffer solution (p H4.8) on the column and collect the 4ml that comes off the column in a Corning® 15 ml centrifuge tube. This is the desalted enzyme.6.  Clean the column by running 100ml milliQ-water through the column. The washed column can be used again for another desalting.7.  Close the column at the bottom and fill the column with 0.02% NaN3. Close the column with a cap on the top and store the column at room temperature.8.  When cellulase and BG are both desalted, dilute the desalted BG 350-fold with acetic acid buffer solution (p H4.8) .9.  Mix the desalted cellulase and the desalted, diluted BG in a 5:3 ratio.10. Dilute this mix 10-fold with acetic acid buffer solution (p H4.8) . This is the enzyme mixture that will be used in the saccharification assay. It is recommended that the enzyme mixture is used within two months after preparation.B.  Preparing GOD-POD solution (Bergmeyer, 1974) and calibration curveThe  glucose  oxidase  (GOD)-peroxidase  (POD)  method  measures  indirectly  the concentration of glucose (Figure 1). In a first step GOD converts glucose monomers into gluconic acid with the production of hydrogen peroxide. The oxidizing power of hydrogen peroxide is used by POD to oxidize a dye,2,2’-azino-bis(3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulphonic acid) (ABTS). Finally, this oxidized form of ABTS will absorb at a wavelength of 405 nm.()# Figure 1. Illustration of the reaction mechanism used to measure glucose1.  Prepare 100ml of GOD-POD solution as described in recipes. The GOD-POD solution contains 50~mg ABTS, 44.81 mg glucose oxidase and 173 ul of 4% peroxidase. The final volume is adjusted to 100ml by adding acetic acid buffer solution (p H4.5) .2.  Make a dilution series of different glucose concentrations (from 20~uM till 200~uM) . Proceed as described in section H and plot as in Figure 2.()Figure 2. An example of a calibration curveglucose (mg)C.  Measuring enzyme activityFor publication of saccharification results, it is recommended to report the enzyme activity. As such, the saccharification assay can be repeated with exactly the same conditions. In addition, the number of samples that can be analyzed in one single assay is limited due to practical reasons. To compare saccharification results obtained in different saccharification assays, it is necessary that these assays are performed with the same enzyme activity.1.  Fill three safe-lock tubes with 4.5 mg of Whatman filter paper. Also include a blank, i.e. a safe-lock tube with no filter paper.2.  Add to each safe-lock tube (with or without filter paper) 450ul acetic acid buffer solution (p H4.8) and 50ul of the enzyme mixture.3.  Incubate in a thermoblock set at 50~^°C for exactly 1 h while shaking at 12.5H z .4.  Boil the samples and blank for 5 min at 99^°C without shaking.5.  Centrifuge at 23,477× g for 5 min.6.  Dilute the supernatants 20 times with acetic acid buffer solution (pH 4.8).7.  Measure  the  glucose  release  in  the  diluted  supernatant  using  glucose oxidase-peroxidase (GOD-POD) (see section H) and calculate the activity of the enzyme mixture expressed in filter paper units (F P U)/m I according to the formula described in Xiao et al. (2004) and as explained in Figure 3.()Figure 3. An example of the calculation of the enzyme activity# D.  Acid pretreatment (see Note 1)1.  Weigh 10mg aliquots of biomass. The biomass can be chopped into little pieces, e.g., Arabidopsis stem pieces of ^2 m m long, or the biomass can be grinded, e.g., poplar stems.2.  Add 1 ml of 1 M HCl and incubate at 80^°C for ^2h while shaking at 12.5H z .3.  Centrifuge for 5 min at 23,477× g and remove the supernatant.4.  Wash the pellet three times by adding each time 1 ml of milliQ-water, centrifuging for 5 min at 23,477× g and removing the supernatant. Note 1: There are many different pretreatments possible. Broad categories are alkali or acid or hot water. Pretreatment can also be skipped. The best type of pretreatment is case by case dependent. An alkali pretreatment has the characteristic to breakdown part of lignin and ester bonds, whereas acid pretreatments will mainly breakdown glycosidic bonds in hemicellulose and amorphous cellulose.E.  Alkali pretreatment1.  Weigh 10mg aliquots of biomass. The biomass can be chopped into little pieces, e.g., Arabidopsis stem pieces of ^2 m m long, or the biomass can be grinded, e.g., poplar stems.2.  Add 1 ml of 62.5 mM NaOH, as described in Van Acker et al. (2014) and incubate at 90~^°C for 3h while shaking at 12.5H z .3.  Centrifuge for 5 min at 23,477× g and remove the supernatant. 4.  Wash the pellet three times by adding each time 1 ml of milliQ-water, centrifuging for 5 min at 23,477× g and removing the supernatant.# F.  Severe washing stepThis washing step removes residual chemicals used during pretreatment, but also free sugars and other solutes that are not part of the cell wall. This step should thus also be included for samples that will be saccharified without any pretreatment.1.  Add 1~ml of 70% ethanol and incubate at 55^°C overnight.2.  Centrifuge for 5 min at 23,477× g and remove the supernatant.3.  Wash the pellet three times by adding each time 1~ml of 70% ethanol, centrifuging for 5 min at 23,477× g and removing the supernatant.4.  Finally wash the pellet with 1 ml acetone, centrifuge for 5 min at 23,477xg and remove the supernatant.5.  Dry the pellet using a vacuum concentrator.6.  Weigh the left-over biomass.G.  Saccharification (Note 2)1.  Dissolve every dried (pretreated or not) sample in 1~ml acetic acid buffer solution (pH4.8) and incubate at 50^°C while shaking ^12.5 H z) ).2.  After 5 min of incubation, add 100ul of enzyme mix to each sample.3.  At several time points, e.g., 2 h, 6 h, ^24h , 48 h after adding the enzyme mixture, take a 20~ul aliquot of the supernatant after spinning down the samples in a benchtop microcentrifuge.4.  Dilute this 20ul -aliquot in an appropriate way (i.e., in a range of 10 till 40-fold dilution) with acetic acid buffer solution (p H4.8) and boil the diluted solution for 5 min at 99^°C to stop the enzymatic reactions.5.  Measure the glucose concentration according to step H. Note 2: To work very precisely, it is recommended to process only 2 samples every minute. This means adding enzyme to the first two samples in the first minute, adding enzyme to the next two samples in the second minute, etc. After exactly incubating for a certain time, e.g., 2 h, the first two samples are centrifuged and an aliquot is taken in the first minute. Samples are boiled to stop the reaction. In the second minute, the two next samples are centrifuged, an aliquot is taken and boiled to stop the reaction, etc.H.  Measuring released glucose1.  For each sample (resulting from step G), standard glucose concentration (see step B) or enzyme activity measurement (see step C), transfer 50~ul to a separate well of a 96-well flat bottom microplate. All measurements are performed in triplicate.2.  Fill at least 3 wells with blanks, i.e. 50 µl acetic acid buffer (pH 4.8). Also add to these blanks 150ul GOD-POD-solution.3.  Add to each well 150ul GOD-POD-solution.4.  Close the microplate with a sealing film and incubate the microtiter plate in an oven at 37^°C for exactly 30 min.5.  Gently remove the sealing film and read the absorbance at 405 nm using a plate spectrophotometer.6.  Use  the  GOD-POD  calibration  curve  (see  step  B)  to  calculate  the  glucose concentration in the samples (see Notes 3, 4 and 5). Note 3: For the calculations, take also into account the dilution factor and the starting volumes at the different time points.Note 4: Figure 4 shows an example of saccharification results.Note 5: It is common to normalize the released glucose to the theoretical amount of glucose that would be recovered if cellulose is completely saccharified. As such, the cellulose-to-glucose conversion can be calculated. To determine the cellulose content in small samples, the protocol as described by Foster and colleagues4 can be followed. An anhydroglucose unit of cellulose (162.14 g/mol) gives rise to glucose (180.155 g/mol) upon saccharification. Therefore, the theoretical complete conversion of 1 mg cellulose will give rise to 1.11 mg of glucose.()saccharification time (h)Figure 4. An example of saccharification results of WT (blue diamonds and lines) and lignin-deficient Arabidopsis thaliana (red squares and lines)# Recipes1.  0.1 M acetic acid buffer solution (p H4.5) Prepare the acetic acid buffer pH 4.5 as follows: 32.5 ml 1 M glacial acetic acid 2.38 sodium acetate trihydrate 967.5 ml milliQ-water2.  0.1 M acetic acid buffer solution (pH 4.8) Prepare the acetic acid buffer pH 4.8 as follows: 24.1 ml 1 M glacial acetic acid 3.52 g sodium acetate trihydrate 975.9ml milliQ-water3.  GOD-POD solution (see Note 6) Prepare the GOD-POD solution as follows: 50mg angle=1 tablet) ABTS 44.81 mg glucose oxidase 173ul4% peroxidase (see Note 7) Add to reach a total volume of 100ml with 0.1 M acetic acid buffer (pH 4.5) Note 6: First dissolve one ABTS tablet in a volumetric flask of 100 ml in approximately 40 ml of 0.1M acetic acid buffer (p H4.5) . Once it is dissolved and glucose oxidase and peroxidase are added, adjust the volume to the mark of 100 ml with 0.1 M acetic acid buffer (p H4.5) . Note 7: 4% peroxidase = 20 mg peroxidase dissolved in 500 µl of 0.1 M acetic acid buffer (p H4.5) .

protocol_hierarchical.1.1.1.1: Remove the NaN3-solution from the desalting Econo-Pac 10 DG column.
protocol_hierarchical.1.1.1.2: Bring 20ml of acetic acid buffer solution (pH 4.8) onto the column and let gravity work.
protocol_hierarchical.1.1.1.3: Mix 1ml of enzyme (cellulase or BG) with 2ml acetic acid buffer solution (pH 4.8) and bring this mix onto the column.
protocol_hierarchical.1.1.1.4: Remove the 3ml that comes off the column.
protocol_hierarchical.1.1.1.5: Add 4ml acetic acid buffer solution (pH 4.8) onto the column and collect the 4ml that comes off the column.
protocol_hierarchical.1.1.2.1: Clean the column by running 100ml milliQ-water through the column.
protocol_hierarchical.1.1.2.2: Close the column at the bottom and fill the column with 0.02% NaN3.

protocol_hierarchical.1.1.3.1: Dilute the desalted BG 350-fold with acetic acid buffer solution (pH 4.8).
protocol_hierarchical.1.1.3.2: Mix the desalted cellulase and the desalted, diluted BG in a 5:3 ratio.
protocol_hierarchical.1.1.3.3: Dilute this mix 10-fold with acetic acid buffer solution (pH 4.8).

protocol_hierarchical.1.7.1.1: Dissolve every dried (pretreated or not) sample in 1ml acetic acid buffer solution (pH 4.8) and incubate at 50°C while shaking at 12.5Hz.

query: I need to solve this problem: how to effectively apply the 'Dissociation and Culture of Adult Mouse Satellite Glial Cells' protocol for Cell Biology & Culture research. Please help me design an experiment.

ground_truth_answer.title: Dissociation and Culture of Adult Mouse Satellite Glial Cells
ground_truth_answer.abstract: Satellite glial cells (SGCs) are a type of glial cell population that originates from neural crest cells. They ultimately migrate to surround the cell bodies of neurons in the ganglia of the peripheral nervous system. Under physiological conditions, SGCs perform homeostatic functions by modifying the microenvironment around nearby neurons and provide nutrients, structure, and protection. In recent years, they have gained considerable attention due to their involvement in peripheral nerve regeneration and pain. Although methods for culturing neonatal or rat SGCs have long existed, a well-characterized method for dissociating and culturing adult SGCs from mouse tissues has been lacking until recently. This has impeded further studies of their function and the testing of new therapeutics. This protocol provides a detailed description of how to obtain primary cultures of adult SGCs from mouse dorsal root ganglia in approximately two weeks with over 90% cell purity. We also demonstrate cell purity of these cultures using quantitative real-time RT-PCR and their functional integrity using calcium imaging.
ground_truth_answer.protocol_text: # A. Initial preparations1.  Prior to beginning any procedures, disinfect all surgical tools and working areas with 70% ethanol.2.  Next, prepare a culture dish by filling it with 4~mL of HBSS dissociation solution (see Recipes) for the initial collection and cleaning of DRG tissues. Prepare two microtubes with 1~mL of HBSS dissociation solution each, where the cleaned tissues will be transferred. Place the culture dish and microtubes on ice at 4^°C .3.  The papain and collagenase solutions (see Recipes) can also be prepared at this time and kept at 4~^°C . Warm these solutions and DMEM culture medium (see Recipes) to 37^°C just before use.4.  Terminally anesthetize a mouse by placing the animal in a jar/chamber for anesthesia. Add a gauze with 1 mL of isoflurane to the jar, and the mouse will be anesthetized within a minute. To ensure complete anesthesia, check for a lack of response to a pinch of the rear footpad.5.  Next, use scissors to decapitate the mice in a sink for the purpose of euthanasia and blood removal. This will facilitate the tissue dissection process.# B.  Tissue dissection1.  Move the mouse to a designed tissue dissection area and liberally spray the back fur with 70% ethanol.2.  To expose the back muscles, make a large transverse cut in the middle of the back skin using standard scissors. Then, pull the skin in opposite directions to remove all of the back skin.3.  To remove the back muscles, make two long cuts close to the left and right sides of the spinal column. Then, use forceps to pull away the muscles starting from the rostral end.4.  To expose the spinal cord and DRGs, perform a laminectomy by removing the top of the vertebral canal. Use spring scissors to cut the bones on both sides of the vertebral canal in a 45^° angle to avoid damaging or losing the DRGs. Continue cutting from side to side while lifting the top of the vertebral canal, starting from the rostral end and working your way down to the caudal end of the spinal column.5.  Next, use forceps to slowly remove the spinal cord in a rostral to caudal direction from the column and expose the DRGs. Discard the spinal cord. The DRGs are located in the dorso-lateral position of the vertebral canal and can be recognized by their round shape and hyaline appearance, which is different from the white color of the attached nerve fibers (Supplementary Figure 1).6.  Using the stereomicroscope, grasp the dorsal root with #5 forceps, carefully pull it a few millimeters, and cut the spinal nerve (immediately distal to the ganglion) with spring scissors. Next, cut the dorsal root and place the collected DRGs in the culture dish on ice. Typically, an experienced experimenter can collect approximately 40 ganglia per mouse.7.  If needed, move the culture dish under the stereomicroscope and use a pair of spring scissors and #5 forceps to remove any extra nerves, roots, and connective tissues from the DRGs. Then, transfer the cleaned DRGs equally into two microtubes.# C. Cell dissociation1.  Centrifuge the microtubes containing the cleaned DRGs at 200×g for 1m i n and remove the HBSS dissociation solution.2. Perform the first enzymatic dissociation by adding 1.5mL of papain solution into the microtubes. Gently flick the microtubes to resuspend the DRGs and incubate them for 20min in a 37 ^°C water bath. Note: Do not resuspend with a pipette or vigorously shake to maintain the integrity of the cells.3. Centrifuge the microtubes at 200× g for 1 min and remove the papain solution.4.  Wash the DRGs with 1~mL of HBSS dissociation solution, centrifuge ( 200× g for 1 min ), and remove the solution.5. Perform the second enzymatic dissociation by adding 1.5mL of collagenase solution into the microtubes. Gently flick the microtubes to resuspend the DRGs and incubate them for 20 min in a 37~^°C still water bath. Note: Do not resuspend with the pipette or vigorously shake to maintain the integrity of the cells.6. Centrifuge the microtubes at 200× g for 1 min and remove all the collagenase solution. Note: From this point forward, the opening of tubes/plates that contain any tissue, cells, media, or reagents should be done in a laminar flow cell culture hood.7.  Resuspend the DRGs in 1~mL of DMEM culture medium. Centrifuge at 200× g for 1 min and carefully remove all culture solution without disturbing the tissue.8. Resuspend the DRGs in 0.5mL of DMEM culture medium. Perform mechanical dissociation by trituration using a P1000 pipette until the solution becomes cloudy (approximately 15–20 times up and down). During trituration, the tissues should pass through the tip with a little friction initially and progress to passing easily. Be gentle and avoid introducing air bubbles at this step; this is critical.9.  Add 1~mL of DMEM culture medium to each microtube. Next, transfer the contents of all microtubes into one 50mL conical tube. Adjust the volume to approximately 5mL with DMEM culture medium and gently mix the contents by flicking the tube.10. Filter the mixture using a 40~um cell strainer followed by a 10~um cell strainer. Collect the filtrate each time in a 50mL tube. If necessary, adjust the volume of the filtrate to 5mL with DMEM culture medium and resuspend the cells by gently flicking the tube.# D. Cell plating and growth1.  Transfer the resuspended cells into a 12-well microplate by pipetting 400~uL of the filtrate into each well containing sterilized cover glasses. Then, incubate the plate in a CO_2 cell culture incubator ( 37~^°C , 5% CO_2, ). Note: During this incubation (24 h), due to the lack of coating of the cover glasses and use of DMEM lowglucose media, SGC cells will attach to the bottom of the dish while the DRG neurons and debris will remain in suspension.2. One day after plating the cells, replace the DMEM culture medium with 400~uL of fresh DMEM culture medium to remove cell debris. Always pre-warm the culture medium to 37 ^°C before use.3.  SGCs proliferate and develop a spindle-shaped morphology (Figure 1) during the next 10–14 days. To maintain SGCs cultures, replace the DMEM culture medium every 2–3 days.

protocol_hierarchical.1.1: Disinfect all surgical tools and working areas with 70% ethanol.
protocol_hierarchical.1.2: Prepare a culture dish with 4mL of HBSS dissociation solution and two microtubes with 1mL of HBSS dissociation solution each, and place them on ice at 4°C.
protocol_hierarchical.1.3: Prepare papain and collagenase solutions and keep them at 4°C. Warm these solutions and DMEM culture medium to 37°C just before use.
protocol_hierarchical.1.4: Terminally anesthetize a mouse using isoflurane and ensure complete anesthesia by checking for a lack of response to a pinch of the rear footpad.
protocol_hierarchical.1.5: Decapitate the mice to facilitate tissue dissection.
protocol_hierarchical.2.1: Move the mouse to a tissue dissection area and liberally spray the back fur with 70% ethanol.
protocol_hierarchical.2.2: Make a large transverse cut in the middle of the back skin and pull the skin in opposite directions to remove all of the back skin.
protocol_hierarchical.2.3: Remove the back muscles by making two long cuts close to the left and right sides of the spinal column and pulling away the muscles.
protocol_hierarchical.2.4: Perform a laminectomy to expose the spinal cord and DRGs by removing the top of the vertebral canal.
protocol_hierarchical.2.5: Remove the spinal cord and expose the DRGs. Discard the spinal cord.
protocol_hierarchical.2.6: Grasp the dorsal root with forceps, carefully pull it, and cut the spinal nerve. Cut the dorsal root and place the collected DRGs in the culture dish on ice.
protocol_hierarchical.2.7: Remove any extra nerves, roots, and connective tissues from the DRGs under a stereomicroscope and transfer the cleaned DRGs into microtubes.
protocol_hierarchical.3.1: Centrifuge the microtubes containing the cleaned DRGs and remove the HBSS dissociation solution.
protocol_hierarchical.3.2.1: Add papain solution to the microtubes and incubate for 20 minutes in a 37°C water bath.
protocol_hierarchical.3.2.2: Gently flick the microtubes to resuspend the DRGs. Note: Do not resuspend with a pipette or vigorously shake to maintain cell integrity.
protocol_hierarchical.3.3: Centrifuge the microtubes and remove the papain solution.
protocol_hierarchical.3.4: Wash the DRGs with HBSS dissociation solution and centrifuge.
protocol_hierarchical.3.5.1: Add collagenase solution to the microtubes and incubate for 20 minutes in a 37°C still water bath.
protocol_hierarchical.3.5.2: Gently flick the microtubes to resuspend the DRGs. Note: Do not resuspend with a pipette or vigorously shake to maintain cell integrity.
protocol_hierarchical.3.6: Centrifuge the microtubes and remove the collagenase solution.
protocol_hierarchical.3.7: Resuspend the DRGs in DMEM culture medium and centrifuge.
protocol_hierarchical.3.8.1: Resuspend the DRGs in DMEM culture medium and perform trituration using a P1000 pipette until the solution becomes cloudy.
protocol_hierarchical.3.8.2: Be gentle and avoid introducing air bubbles during trituration.
protocol_hierarchical.3.9: Add DMEM culture medium to each microtube and transfer the contents into a 50mL conical tube.
protocol_hierarchical.3.10: Filter the mixture using cell strainers and collect the filtrate.
protocol_hierarchical.4.1: Transfer the resuspended cells into a 12-well microplate and incubate in a CO2 cell culture incubator.
protocol_hierarchical.4.2: Replace the DMEM culture medium with fresh medium to remove cell debris.
protocol_hierarchical.4.3: Maintain SGC cultures by replacing the DMEM culture medium every 2-3 days and monitoring cell proliferation and morphology.

ground_truth_answer.title: Testing the Effect of UV Radiation on the Survival of Burkholderia glumae
ground_truth_answer.abstract: Burkholderia glumae (B. glumae) is becoming a serious threat in the major rice producing areas of the world. It was reported that Burkholderia spp., including B. glumae, are adapted to a wide range of ecological niches. Different bacterial strains show different levels of UV tolerance which may be due to the presence of different protection mechanisms. Previously we reported that pigment producing strains of B. glumae are more tolerant to UV radiation than non-pigmented strains. Here, we describe the protocol of UV tolerance assay for B. glumae in different exposure times. Using this protocol, we can calculate the survival rate of B. glumae strains, as well as other bacterial species, in exposure to UV radiation.
ground_truth_answer.protocol_text: Note: For handling bacterial cultures, aseptic techniques must be used and each step should be carried out in a sterile laminar flow hood when practicable.# Day 11.  Streak the bacterial cells from glycerol stock on an LB agar plate supplemented with nitrofurantoin (100u/ml) , an antibiotic to which B. glumae is naturally resistant. 500× stock solution of nitrofurantoin (50 ml/ml) should be prepared by dissolving in dimethylformamide (DMF).2.  Incubate the LB plate at 37~^°C (optimal temperature for bacterial growth) overnight (~ 18 h).# Day 23.  Streak the overnight-grown bacterial cells from a single colony on a CPG agar plate, using a metal inoculation needle or a sterile toothpick. Incubate the inoculated CPG agar plate at 30^°C (optimal temperature for the production of pigment, which is a main determinant for bacterial tolerance to UV) for 48 h (Figure 1).()Figure 1. Strains of Burkholderia glumae grown on CPG agar plates. The upper-left and bottom plates: Pigment-producing strains of B. glumae. The upper-right plate: A pigment-deficient strain of B. glumae. Photo was taken after 48 h of incubation at 30^°C .# bio-protocol# Day 44.  Resuspend the bacterial colonies grown on CPG agar in sterile water in a sterile microcentrifuge tube by flicking with hands.5.  Adjust the bacterial concentration to  O D_600=0.1 (0.5x10^7C F U/m l) , using a spectrophotometer.6.  Dilute the bacterial suspension with 1:10 ratio in sterile d H_2O .7.  Spread 100ul of the diluted B. . glumae cells on a CPG plate.8.  Expose B. glumae cells to UV light from a G15T8 germicidal fluorescent bulb, 15 W in a laminar hood at the distance of 70 cm with 253.7 nm wave length for various durations (Figure 2). The lid should be off during the UV exposure.9.  To determine the number of CFU accurately, serial dilutions up to 1:1,000 should be made and spread on CPG agar plates10. Incubate the inoculated plates at 30~^°C for 48 h .()Figure 2. An image showing the UV irradiation procedureDay 6 11. Count the surviving bacterial colonies (Figure 3).()Figure 3. An image of bacterial colonies that survived UV exposure. The upper two rows: pigment-producing strains of Burkholderia glumae. The bottom row: A pigment-deficient strain of B . glumae. From left to right: Each vertical row represents the CPG agar plates exposed to UV for 0, 10, 20, 40, and 60 sec, respectively.12. Convert the data to percentage of survival in different time of exposure to UV radiation. Survival rate (%)= # colonies from UV-exposed cells/# colonies from non UV-exposed cells x 100# Recipes1.  LB Agar plates (1L) 25 of LB broth 18 of agar Add dH2O to 1 L Sterilize by autoclaving on liquid cycle2.  CPG agar plates (1 L) 1.5 of Casamino acid 10 of Peptone 5 g of Glucose 18 g of agar Add dH2O to 1 L Sterilize by autoclaving on liquid cycle

protocol_hierarchical.1.1: Streak the bacterial cells from glycerol stock on an LB agar plate supplemented with nitrofurantoin (100u/ml).
protocol_hierarchical.1.2: Prepare a 500× stock solution of nitrofurantoin (50 ml/ml) by dissolving in dimethylformamide (DMF).
protocol_hierarchical.1.3: Incubate the LB plate at 37°C overnight (~ 18 h).
protocol_hierarchical.2.1: Streak the overnight-grown bacterial cells from a single colony on a CPG agar plate.
protocol_hierarchical.2.2: Incubate the inoculated CPG agar plate at 30°C for 48 h.

3.title: Resuspension and Adjustment of Bacterial Concentration

protocol_hierarchical.3.1: Resuspend the bacterial colonies grown on CPG agar in sterile water.
protocol_hierarchical.3.2: Adjust the bacterial concentration to OD_600=0.1 (0.5x10^7 CFU/ml) using a spectrophotometer.

4.title: Dilution and Plating of Bacterial Cells

protocol_hierarchical.4.1: Dilute the bacterial suspension with a 1:10 ratio in sterile dH2O.
protocol_hierarchical.4.2: Spread 100ul of the diluted B. glumae cells on a CPG plate.
protocol_hierarchical.5.1: Expose B. glumae cells to UV light from a G15T8 germicidal fluorescent bulb for various durations.
protocol_hierarchical.5.2: Incubate the inoculated plates at 30°C for 48 h.
protocol_hierarchical.6.1: Make serial dilutions up to 1:1,000 and spread on CPG agar plates.
protocol_hierarchical.6.2: Count the surviving bacterial colonies.
protocol_hierarchical.7.1: Convert the data to percentage of survival in different times of exposure to UV radiation.
protocol_hierarchical.7.2: Calculate the survival rate (%) using the formula: # colonies from UV-exposed cells/# colonies from non-UV-exposed cells x 100
protocol_hierarchical.8.1.4: Sterilize by autoclaving on liquid cycle.
protocol_hierarchical.8.2.6: Sterilize by autoclaving on liquid cycle.
