problem: Need to determine the non-inferiority and safety of olive-oil-based three-chamber bags compared to soybean-oil-based compounded bags for parenteral nutrition in hospitalized adult patients.

query: Please generate detailed steps for the experiment 'Video Article CRISPR-Cas9-based Genome Engineering to Generate Jurkat Reporter Models for HIV-1 Infection with Selected Proviral Integration Sites', focusing on the Molecular Biology Techniques technique.

ground_truth_answer.title: Video Article CRISPR-Cas9-based Genome Engineering to Generate Jurkat Reporter Models for HIV-1 Infection with Selected Proviral Integration Sites
ground_truth_answer.abstract: Human immunodeficiency virus (HIV) integrates its proviral DNA non-randomly into the host cell genome at recurrent sites and genomic hotspots. Here we present a detailed protocol for the generation of novel in vitro models for HIV infection with chosen genomic integration sites using CRISPR-Cas9-based genome engineering technology. With this method, a reporter sequence of choice can be integrated into a targeted, chosen genomic locus, reflecting clinically relevant integration sites.In the protocol, the design of an HIV-derived reporter and choosing of a target site and gRNA sequence are described. A targeting vector with homology arms is constructed and transfected into Jurkat T cells. The reporter sequence is targeted to the selected genomic site by homologous recombination facilitated by a Cas9-mediated double-strand break at the target site. Single-cell clones are generated and screened for targeting events by flow cytometry and PCR. Selected clones are then expanded, and correct targeting is verified by PCR, sequencing, and Southern blotting. Potential off-target events of CRISPR-Cas9-mediated genome engineering are analyzed.By using this protocol, novel cell culture systems that model HIV infection at clinically relevant integration sites can be generated. Although the generation of single-cell clones and verification of correct reporter sequence integration is time-consuming, the resulting clonal lines are powerful tools to functionally analyze proviral integration site choice.
ground_truth_answer.protocol_text:
# 1. Targeting Strategy for Genome Engineering and Targeting Vector (tv) Design
# 1. Choice of targeted locus, gRNA, and targeting vector design
1. oCf hHoIoVs ef otuhnedg ienn opamtiice lnotsc uins  tdoif fbeer etnatr gsteutedide bs2a,3s,e4,d5, 6o,7n,8 t haes  ian dgiuviiddeulianl es.c Iine nstiilfiicco  qeuxetrstaicotn t. hUe sgee pnuobmliisc hseedq liusetsn coef  roef cthurer ednets iirnteedg graetnioonm siictes locus to be targeted (sequence of the complete gene or at least 5 kb of genomic sequence) using UCSC genome browser (http:// genome.edu.ucsc.edu).
2. Choose guide RNAs (gRNAs) of 20 nt for targeting of the chosen genomic locus using the E-CRISP webtool (http://www.e-crisp.org). 1. Select “Homo sapiens GRCh38” as the organism. Input 2,000 bp of the genomic sequence covering the desired genomic locus extracted in step 1.1.1. 2. Start a gRNA search using medium application settings (any PAM, any 5' base, off-targets tolerate mismatches, and introns/ CPG islands are excluded). A list with possible gRNA designs will appear, ranking from highest to lower scores for specificity and efficiency. 3. Select a gRNA that preferably shows a high score for specificity and efficiency and is as close as possible to the desired genomic locus to be targeted. NOTE: A compromise between proximity to the desired genomic locus and design of specific and efficient gRNA has to be found.
3. Blast the chosen gRNA sequence against the reference genome using the NCBI blast browser (https://blast.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov) to check for uniqueness of the gRNA binding site. 1. Select “human” as the genome. Input the gRNA sequence as the query sequence. Select “highly similar sequences” (megablast) as the program. Ensure that the gRNA sequence is unique. If not, chose a different gRNA from step 1.1.2.3 and blast again.
4. Once gRNA sequence is chosen, select in silico 1,000 bp upstream and downstream of gRNA sequence from genomic sequence extracted in step 1.1.1 as 5’ and 3’ HA accordingly. NOTE: The gRNAs should be homologous to the chosen genomic integration site locus and located adjacent to a protospacer adjacent motif (PAM; e.g., NGG for SpCas9) (Figure 2a). The tv contains the reporter sequence that is 5° and acute3 flanked by HAs. HAs cover 1000 bp upstream and downstream of the gRNA sequence11. The full gRNA sequence should not be included in the HA. An overlap of up to 5 nt is acceptable (Figure 2a).
# 2. Generation of gRNA and targeting vectors # NOTE:  For vector schemes, see Figure 2b. To generate a vector for expression of SpCas9 and gRNA, use the pX330-U6-Chimeric_BB-cBh-hSpCas9 as the backbone from which both SpCas9 and the single guide RNA (sgRNA) can be simultaneously expressed. To clone the gRNA sequence into the backbone, use the BbsI restriction sites12. 2. To generate the tv, choose a high-copy plasmid as backbone (e.g., pMK or cDNA3.1). 1. First, assemble the reporter (in this protocol: LTR_tdTomato_BGH-PA) into the construct backbone by Gibson Assembly clong13 using a commercial assembly cloning kit, and introduce 5° and acute3 flanking restriction sites (e.g., 5’ PacI and 3’ SmaI) for subsequent restriction digestion cloning of the HAs. 2. Amplify 1000 bp of the HA fragments chosen in step 1.1.4 from genomic DNA (gDNA) of the cell type to be targeted (in this protocol: Jurkat cells) using a DNA polymerase with proofreading activity (see Tables 1 and 2 for PCR ingredients and cycling conditions). Then, introduce reporter flanking restriction sites on the 5’ and acute3 ends of each HA (PacI on 5’ HA on both ends, SmaI on 3°H A on both ends). 3. Sequentially clone HAs into construct backbone already containing the reporter (generated in step 1.2.2.1) by restriction enzyme cloning14,15. First, clone in 5° HA using PacI restriction sites, then clone in acute3 HA using SmaI restriction sites. NOTE: If tv backbone contains an additional fluorescent reporter, unwanted backbone integration can be assessed by flow cytometry (see steps 3.2.2 and 3.2.3). If tv backbone contains no fluorescent reporter, backbone integration must be assessed using PCR (see step 3.2.8).
# 2. CRISPR-Cas9-Based Targeting of Jurkat Cells
# 1. Transfection of Jurkat cells
1. 24h prior to transfection, plate 1.25×106 Jurkat T cells in 2.5mL of RPMI 1640 supplemented with 10% (v/v) fetal calf serum (FCS) and 4 mM L-glutamine referred to as “RPMI w.o. antibiotics (AB)” per well of a 6-well cell culture plate. For a single targeting experiment, prepare one complete 6-well plate (i.e., 6 wells each with 2.5mL of cell suspension).
2. On the following day, co-transfect the cells with circular tv and pX330-U6-Chimeric_BB-cBh-hSpCas9/gRNA using a transfection reagent specific for Jurkat cells. 1. Add 2u of circular tv and 2u of pX330 -U6-Chimeric_BB-cBh-hSpCas9/gRNA per well to 250uL of commercial RPMI medium with reduced serum concentration optimized for transfection (RPMI with 50% reduction in serum) in a reaction tube and mix well. 2. Add 12uL of transfection reagent slowly to the DNA/medium without touching the wall of the tube and swirl. Let the mixture incubate for 15 min and add dropwise to one well of cells. Incubate the cells at 37°C and 5% C O_2 . NOTE: The preparation of transfection reaction can be scaled up. No medium change is required after transfection.
# 2. Enrichment of transfected cells by fluorescence-activated cell sorting (FACS)
1. 72h post-transfection, pool the transfected cells, count them, and prepare for enrichment by FACS. Collect the cells in a50m L conical tube, centrifuge at 300× g and room temperature (RT) for 4 min, wash the cells once in PBS, centrifuge again, suspend the pellet in an appropriate amount of FACS buffer (PBS supplemented with 1%F C S+1 m M EDTA) at 1 x 10 cells/mL, and finally transfer into a FACS tube.
2. Subject the cells to FACS and sort those that express the fluorescent reporter of the tv (e.g., tdTomato in this protocol). Collect the cells in RPMI 1640 supplemented with 10% FCS, 4 mM L-glutamine, and 50U/mL penicillin and streptomycin (referred to as “RPMI w/ AB”).
3. After FACS sorting, wash the cells once by adding 20mL of RPMI w/ AB to sorted cells and centrifuge at 300× g for 4 min at RT. Resuspend the cell pellet in an appropriate amount of RPMI w/ AB and plate the cells in one well of a cell culture plate with the appropriate volume according to cell number post-FACS. NOTE: Culture sort the cells in a small volume (e.g., 24-well), as considerable levels of cell death have been observed in the first week post-targeting (up to 80-90% ).
4. Expand the mixed targeted cell population up to a density of 1×106 cells/mL in a 75cm2 cell culture flask. This will take around 10–14 days post-FACS sorting.
# 3. Confirmation of targeting events by flow cytometry in the mixed targeted cell population
1. After 10–14 days of expansion (when cells have reached a density of 1×106 cells/mL in a 75c m2 cell culture flask), plate two wells with 1×106 cells of the mixed targeted cell population in 1 mL of RPMI w/ AB in a 12-well cell culture plate.
2. Induce the HIV LTR of the reporter (generation of tv is described in step 1.2.2.1.) in one of the wells by adding 50ng/mL phorbol 12- myristate 13-acetate (PMA) and 1 uM Ionomycin (referred to as PMA-Iono). Use cells in the second well as the non-induced control. Culture both the induced and the non-induced cells for 24h .
3. Take 0.5mL of cell suspension of non-induced and induced cells (each), wash them once in PBS, and suspend each in 200uL of FACS buffer.
4. Analyze 100,000 cells by flow cytometry. Gate the viable single-cells based on size in forward and sideward scatter, and analyze fluorescent reporter gene expression. NOTE: At this step (10–14 days post-FACS sorting), transient expression of fluorescent reporter by transfection should no longer be detectable. Fluorescent expression at this time point indicates genomic integration of reporter sequence.
# 4. Detection of targeting events by PCR on genomic DNA of the mixed targeted cell population
1. Prepare gDNA from 2mL of cell suspension of the mixed targeted cell population from step 2.2.4. Use a gDNA extraction kit according to the manufacturer’s protocol. Then prepare the gDNA of non-targeted cells as a control.
2. Perform int. junction PCRs (primers P1/P2 and P3/P4 for 5' and z int. junction, respectively) and non-targeted allele PCR (primers P1/P4) using a high-fidelity DNA polymerase (see Tables 3 and 4 for PCR ingredients and cycling conditions). Analyze 5uL of PCR products on a 1.5% agarose/TAE gel. NOTE: If the mixed targeted population contains cells that have undergone genome engineering, a specific PCR product should be observed that is not detectable in gDNA of non-targeted cells (negative control). For the non-targeted allele PCR, one should observe a product of the same size for both the targeted and non-targeted cells (positive control for genomic P1 and P4 primers). If no bands are observed, consider altering PCR cycling conditions by increasing the number of cycles or altering the PCR buffer (for example through addition of DMSO or increased amounts of  M g2+ ), or by changing the polymerase.
# 3. Generation of Clonal Lines and Screening for Correct Targeting
# 1. Generation of single-cell clones through dilution plating
1. Prepare Jurkat-conditioned medium in advance: take off RPMI w/ AB medium from healthy, untreated Jurkat T cells grown to 1×106 cells/mL, centrifuge for 5 min at 300× g, and filter the supernatant using a 0.22um syringe filter unit. NOTE: Keep the conditioned medium at 4°C for short-term storage or at -20°C for storage longer than 1 week. Prepare 20 to 30mL of conditioned medium prior to dilution plating.
2. Count the targeted cells from step 2.2.4 after 10–14 days of expansion and dilute them in RPMI w/ AB to a concentration of 1×105 cells/mL. Take 100uL of 1×105 cells/mL solution and dilute with 9.9mL of medium to achieve a concentration of 1,000 cells/mL. Take 1 mL of 1,000 cells/mL solution and dilute with m L of medium to achieve a concentration of 100 cells/mL.
3. Plate 96-well plates containing 1 cell per well and 2 cells per well. For 1 cell per well, take 1 mL of 100 cells/mL solution and mix gently with 5mL of conditioned medium and 4mL of fresh medium in a sterile reagent reservoir.
4. For 2 cells per well, take 2mL of 100 cells/mL solution and mix gently with 5mL of conditioned medium and 3m L of fresh medium. Plate 96-well round-bottom plates with 100uL of the respective cell dilution per well using a multichannel pipet. NOTE: 5 to 10 96-well plates per targeting construct are sufficient to obtain clones for screening.
5. Stack the 96-well plates, cover each stack with a 6-well plate containing 3 mL of PBS in each well, and incubate the plates at 37°C in a humidified cell culture incubator with 5% C O_2 for 3 weeks. NOTE:  Do not change the cell culture medium during this time. Do not open the incubator more than once or twice a week. The best results are observed in incubators with open water reservoir.
6. After 3 weeks of incubation, visually confirm the presence of grown colonies using light microscopy (4X magnification) and mark the wells with grown colonies so they are visible as points on the bottom of the wells.
7. Prepare one 96-well round-bottom plate with 100uL of RPMI w/ AB per well. Gently resuspend cells of a marked well by pipetting. Transfer 100uL of cell suspension into one well of the new 96-well plate already containing 100uL of RPMI w/ AB, then mix gently by pipetting. Transfer 100uL of this cell suspension into a second empty 96-well round-bottom plate to duplicate the plate.
8. Continue with all marked wells with grown colonies. Fill all the blank wells with 200uL of RPMI w/ AB medium. Incubate the plates at 37° and 5% C O_2 NOTE:  One of these plates will serve for expansion of single-cell clones (“stock plate”) and the other as a “duplicate plate” for screening.
# 2. Screening of single-cell clones by flow cytometry and PCR
1. Let the duplicate plate incubate for 24 to 48h and duplicate the plate again. To do this, add 100uL of RPMI w/ AB to every well, mix gently by pipetting, and transfer 100uL to a new 96-well round-bottom plate using a multichannel pipet. Use one plate for flow cytometry screening and the other for PCR-based screening.
2. For flow cytometry screening, stimulate the cells with PMA-Iono. Prepare a mastermix of 0.1uL of Ionomocin (1 mM stock), 0.1 µL of PMA moonug/uL stock), and 4.8uL of RPMI w/ AB per number of wells, then add 5uL of mastermix per well. NOTE: Induction is necessary to successfully identify clones, where the LTR might be transcriptionally silent and therefore the fluorescent reporter is not expressed.
3. Let cells incubate for 24h and prepare cells for flow cytometry as described in step 2.3.3. Gate any viable single-cells based on size in forward and sideward scatter and analyze fluorescent reporter gene expression by flow cytometry (for example results, see Figure 3c). If tv backbone contains a second fluorescent reporter with promoter (e.g., GFP), screen any clones for backbone reporter expression also (see step 1.2.2 and the following note for explanation). NOTE:  Backbone reporter expression indicates unwanted integration of backbone sequences.
4. Once the clones in the second duplicate plate have grown sufficiently (usually 24 to 48h after duplication of the 96-well plate), prepare cell lysates containing gDNA for PCR screening. Centrifuge the plate for 10m i n at 300× g at RT. Carefully take off the supernatant without disturbing the cell pellet. NOTE: All steps for the preparation of lysates and PCR reactions can be performed with multichannel pipettes.
5. Wash cells with 100uL of PBS by gentle pipetting and centrifuging the plate for 5 min at 300× g at RT. Take off the PBS and add 200uL of lysis buffer 200 mM NaCl, 100m M Tris-HCl pH 8-8.5, 5mM EDTA, 0.1% SDS; then add 250-1,000 ug/mL of proteinase K (lyophilized powder, weigh in freshly) per well. Mix gently by pipetting, and transfer the suspension to a new PCR plate.
6. Seal the plate with paraffin film and incubate for 1h at 55°C in a thermocycler. Centrifuge at maximum speed for 10 min (3,000× g) to spin down cell debris, and transfer the supernatant to a new PCR plate. NOTE: Cell lysates in plates can be stored at this stage at 4°C until further use.
7. Prepare a 96-well PCR plate with 110uL of d H_2O and add 10uL of cell lysate (1:12 dilution). Cell lysates might be viscous and difficult to pipet. Use at least 20-uL pipet tips.
8. Inactivate proteinase K by incubation for 10 min at 99°C in a thermocycler. Subsequently use the inactivated and diluted cell lysates for PCR screening.
9. Design primers for screening PCR (P5 and P6) based on the chosen reporter sequence to amplify 500–800 bp of the reporter sequence. For positive control PCR, use primers P7 and P8 that amplify 630 bp of a wild-type, non-targeted genomic locus (NUP188 gene) (Figure 3c and Table 5). Design a third primer pair that amplifies 500–600 bp of the tv backbone as a control for unspecific integration of tv backbone sequences (backbone PCR).
10. For screening, control, and backbone PCR, use a commercial PCR mastermix (see Tables 6 and 7 for PCR ingredients and cycling conditions). Use 2uL of the diluted and inactivated lysate prepared in step 3.2.8 as a template and run PCR for 38 to 40 cycles of PCR amplification in 96-well format.
11. Analyze 5uL of PCR products on a 1.5% agarose/TAE gel. NOTE: For control PCR, a specific band of 630 bp should be observed for every sample, confirming that the quality of cell lysates is adequate for PCR. A specific band in screening PCR (500–800 bp depending on primer design) indicates integration of the reporter sequence. A specific band for backbone PCR (500–600 bp, depending on primer design) indicates unwanted integration of tv backbone sequences (for example results, see Figure 3c).
12. Combine the results of flow cytometry (step 3.2.3) and PCR-based screening (step 3.2.12). Select clones which show correct sizes of PCR products in screening PCR and positive control PCR and expression of fluorescent reporter after induction with PMA-Iono in flow cytometry. Exclude clones that show any PCR product in backbone PCR or expression of tv backbone-encoded fluorescent protein, indicating unspecific integration of tv backbone sequence.
13. Gradually expand selected clones from the 96-well stock plate to bigger well formats (48/24/12/6-well) until achieving a T75 cell culture flask format by adding fresh medium every 2 to 3 days. Maintain a cell density between 1×105 and 1×106 cells/mL.
14. Make sure to prepare cell stocks of clones during expansion: count the cells, centrifuge at 300× for 5 min at RT, discard the supernatant, and suspend the cells gently in F C S+10% DMSO at 5×106 cells/mL. Aliquot in cryogenic vials and use a cryo-freezing container to freeze the cells to 80°C at 1 °C/m i n . For long term storage, transfer them to liquid N_2 . NOTE: It is advisable to retain a T75 cell culture flask (i.e., around 1×107 cells) during the expansion in preparation of gDNA for verification of targeting by Southern blotting (see section 3.4).
# 3. Verification of integration sites by PCR/sequencing in selected clones DNA sequence level.
1. Prepare gDNA of the selected clones and Jurkat wild-type cells using a commercial gDNA extraction kit.
2. Use primer pairs binding the 5' end of the reporter and upstream of 5HA for 5° int. junction (primers P1 and P2) and the z end of the reporter and downstream of 3H A for z int. junction (primers P3 and P4) as described in step 2.4. Use primers P1 and P4 to amplify the targeted integration site on the allele without reporter integrant (Figure 4a).
3. Prepare PCR reactions with 100–200 ng of gDNA as a template and perform PCR using a polymerase with proofreading activity (see Tables 1 and 2 for PCR ingredients and cycling conditions). NOTE: If no bands are observed, consider altering the PCR cycling conditions by increasing the number of cycles or altering the PCR buffer (for example, by adding DMSO or increased amounts of M g2+. ), or by changing the polymerase.
4. Analyze 5uL of PCR products on a 1.5% agarose/TAE gel. If correct band sizes are observed, purify the remaining PCR product using a commercial kit and subject it to Sanger sequencing. Verify sequences of 5’ int. junction, z int. junction, and targeted site of the allele without reporter integrant by aligning them with expected sequences. NOTE: The homologous allele where the reporter has not integrated will likely show Cas9-mediated changes at the target site, such as nucleotide insertions or deletions (Figure 4a).
5. For clones that show correct int. junction sequences after alignment, perform a PCR amplifying the whole targeted reporter and subject it to Sanger sequencing to verify the correct sequence of the integrant.
# 4. Southern blot analysis for verification of targeting in selected clones Develop a strategy for appropriate gDNA digestion and probe the design prior to starting the experiment. 1. Select a restriction enzyme for gDNA restriction that generates appropriate fragments of 2 to 10 kb length at the targeted site. Certain restriction enzymes, such as Asp718, BamHI, BglI, BglII, EcoRV, HindIII, NcoI, PstI, PvuII, ScaI, StuI, and SstI have been successfully used for digesting high molecular weight gDNA. 2. Design two different Southern probes: a reporter-specific probe and genomic probe. The reporter-specific probe hybridizes to a sequence within the reporter (i.e., tdTomato-specific probe). The genomic probe hybridizes to a genomic region close to (but not overlapping) one HA. 3. Choose the genomic probe so that the fragment generated by gDNA digestion that will be detected by genomic probe binding differs in length (more than 2k b ) from the targeted and non-targeted alleles (Figure 4b). A probe length of 400 to 1,000 bp is recommended.
4. Design PCR primers to amplify the two required probes. Amplify the reporter-specific probe from tv template using a high-fidelity DNA polymerase (see Tables 3 and 4 for PCR ingredients and cycling conditions).
5. Amplify the genomic probe from wild-type Jurkat gDNA prepared with a commercial gDNA extraction kit using a DNA polymerase with proofreading activity (see Tables 1 and 2 for PCR ingredients and cycling conditions). Purify the PCR products on an agarose/TAE gel and extract the fragments using a commercial gel extraction kit according to the manufacturer’s instructions. Extract high molecular weight gDNA from 1 x 10 cells of the wild-type Jurkat cells and selected clones from step 3.2.14.
1. Pellet the cells by centrifugation at 300× g for 5 min at RT, wash it once with PBS, and suspend the pellet in 4m L of lysis buffer 200 mM NaCL, 100m M Tris-HCl pH 8, 5 mM EDTA, 0.1% SDS; then add 250-1,000ug/mL proteinase K (lyophilized powder, weigh in freshly). Incubate o/n at 55°C , shaking at 350 rpm in a tabletop thermomixer.
2. Add 4mL of isopropanol and mix by inversion 10 to 20 times. The gDNA should become visible as white precipitate. Spool the precipitated gDNA onto the fine tip of a glass pipette, wash by emerging in 750uL of 70% EtOH, and let dry at RT (5 to 10m i n) .
3. Shed the precipitate into a 1.5mL reaction tube containing 500uL of 1× TE buffer (10 mM Tris-HCl pH 8.0, 1 mM EDTA) and leave to dissolve o/n at 4°C , shaking at 350 rpm. Any pipetting of gDNA from this stage should be done with wide-bore tips to avoid shearing. NOTE: Preparation of high molecular weight gDNA is essential for Southern blot analysis, and commercially available gDNA preparation kits are not suitable.
3. Digest (two times) 15u of gDNA of the selected clones and wild-type Jurkat cells with selected restriction enzyme (see step 3.4.1.1) in a 60uL reaction with 6uL of enzyme (20 Units /uL. ): first, add DNA, digestion buffer, and d d H_2O , incubate o/n at 37°C , then add enzyme and incubate o/n at enzyme-specific digestion temperature. 15u g of digested gDNA is required per Southern probe.
4. Use 7uL of the 60uL restriction digest for analytical gel electrophoresis on a 1% agarose/TAE gel. A smear indicates complete digestion and good DNA quality for Southern blot analysis.
5. Precipitate the remaining restriction digest by adding 1:10 3 M sodium acetate and 2 volumes 100% EtOH, then incubate for 1h at -80°C and centrifuge for 30m i n at 15,600× g at 4°C .
6. Discard the supernatant and wash the pellet with 70% EtOH. Centrifuge for 15 min at 15,600× g at 4°C , discard the supernatant, let the pellet dry briefly at RT, and dissolve in 20uL of d d H_2O .
7. Run 1% agarose/TAE blotting gel, loading 20 uL of digested gDNA per lane. Run the gel for 2h at 60 V, 400m A . NOTE: The percentage of agarose gel and running time/voltage may be adjusted according to expected fragment size for Southern blot detection calculated in step 3.4.1.1. The following steps of Southern blot analysis are described in detail in a supplementary protocol (steps 1 to 18). These steps comprise of: washing of the blotting gel, blotting onto a nylon membrane, radioactive probe generation, probe hybridization, and development of autoradiograph film. Compare the obtained banding pattern after autoradiograph development in step 18 (supplementary protocol) with the expected pattern according to Southern strategy (for example results, see Figure 4b).
# 5. Analysis of off-target events Use CCTop16 (http://crispr.cos.uni-heidelberg.de) to generate a list of the ten highest-ranked in silico predicted off-target sequences.
1. Input the gRNA sequence including PAM as used for targeting as the query sequence. Select “NGG” as PAM and “Human genome” as the reference for off-target prediction.
2. Set maximal total mismatches to 4 and target site length to the length of the gRNA without PAM. The output file will provide a ranked list of genomic off-target sites for respective gRNA.
2. In silico extract the genomic sequence 500 bp upstream and downstream of each of the ten highest-ranked off-target hits using UCSC Genome Browser (http://genome.edu.ucsc.edu) and the position of the off-target hit from CCTop results list.
3. For each off the target sites to be analysed, design a PCR primer pair that amplifies a fragment of 600 to 700 bp in length including the predicted off-target site.
4. Extract gDNA from the selected clones and Jurkat wild-type cells using a commercial gDNA extraction kit. For every off-target site, perform a PCR using a DNA polymerase with proofreading activity (see Tables 1 and 2 for PCR ingredients and cycling conditions) on wild-type and the respective clone-derived gDNA.
5. Analyze 5uL of PCR products on a 1.5% agarose/TAE gel. If correct band sizes are observed, purify the remaining PCR product using a commercial PCR purification kit and subject it to Sanger sequencing. Compare sequences of the off-target sites in Jurkat cells and the targeted clones.

NOTE: If tv backbone contains an additional fluorescent reporter, unwanted backbone integration can be assessed by flow cytometry (see steps 3.2.2 and 3.2.3). If tv backbone contains no fluorescent reporter, backbone integration must be assessed using PCR (see step 3.2.8)

protocol_hierarchical.5.1: 24h prior to transfection, plate 1.25×106 Jurkat T cells in 2.5mL of RPMI 1640 supplemented with 10% (v/v) fetal calf serum (FCS) and 4 mM L-glutamine referred to as “RPMI w.o. antibiotics (AB)” per well of a 6-well cell culture plate. For a single targeting experiment, prepare one complete 6-well plate (i.e., 6 wells each with 2.5mL of cell suspension).
protocol_hierarchical.5.2: On the following day, co-transfect the cells with circular tv and pX330-U6-Chimeric_BB-cBh-hSpCas9/gRNA using a transfection reagent specific for Jurkat cells. 1. Add 2u of circular tv and 2u of pX330 -U6-Chimeric_BB-cBh-hSpCas9/gRNA per well to 250uL of commercial RPMI medium with reduced serum concentration optimized for transfection (RPMI with 50% reduction in serum) in a reaction tube and mix well. 2. Add 12uL of transfection reagent slowly to the DNA/medium without touching the wall of the tube and swirl. Let the mixture incubate for 15 min and add dropwise to one well of cells. Incubate the cells at 37°C and 5% C O_2 . NOTE: The preparation of transfection reaction can be scaled up. No medium change is required after transfection.

6.title: Enrichment of transfected cells by fluorescence-activated cell sorting (FACS)

protocol_hierarchical.6.1: 72h post-transfection, pool the transfected cells, count them, and prepare for enrichment by FACS. Collect the cells in a50m L conical tube, centrifuge at 300× g and room temperature (RT) for 4 min, wash the cells once in PBS, centrifuge again, suspend the pellet in an appropriate amount of FACS buffer (PBS supplemented with 1%F C S+1 m M EDTA) at 1 x 10 cells/mL, and finally transfer into a FACS tube.
protocol_hierarchical.6.2: Subject the cells to FACS and sort those that express the fluorescent reporter of the tv (e.g., tdTomato in this protocol). Collect the cells in RPMI 1640 supplemented with 10% FCS, 4 mM L-glutamine, and 50U/mL penicillin and streptomycin (referred to as “RPMI w/ AB”).
protocol_hierarchical.6.3: After FACS sorting, wash the cells once by adding 20mL of RPMI w/ AB to sorted cells and centrifuge at 300× g for 4 min at RT. Resuspend the cell pellet in an appropriate amount of RPMI w/ AB and plate the cells in one well of a cell culture plate with the appropriate volume according to cell number post-FACS. NOTE: Culture sort the cells in a small volume (e.g., 24-well), as considerable levels of cell death have been observed in the first week post-targeting (up to 80-90% ).
protocol_hierarchical.6.4: Expand the mixed targeted cell population up to a density of 1×106 cells/mL in a 75cm2 cell culture flask. This will take around 10–14 days post-FACS sorting.

7.title: Confirmation of targeting events by flow cytometry in the mixed targeted cell population

protocol_hierarchical.7.1: After 10–14 days of expansion (when cells have reached a density of 1×106 cells/mL in a 75c m2 cell culture flask), plate two wells with 1×106 cells of the mixed targeted cell population in 1 mL of RPMI w/ AB in a 12-well cell culture plate.
protocol_hierarchical.7.2: Induce the HIV LTR of the reporter (generation of tv is described in step 1.2.2.1.) in one of the wells by adding 50ng/mL phorbol 12- myristate 13-acetate (PMA) and 1 uM Ionomycin (referred to as PMA-Iono). Use cells in the second well as the non-induced control. Culture both the induced and the non-induced cells for 24h .
protocol_hierarchical.7.3: Take 0.5mL of cell suspension of non-induced and induced cells (each), wash them once in PBS, and suspend each in 200uL of FACS buffer.
protocol_hierarchical.7.4: Analyze 100,000 cells by flow cytometry. Gate the viable single-cells based on size in forward and sideward scatter, and analyze fluorescent reporter gene expression. NOTE: At this step (10–14 days post-FACS sorting), transient expression of fluorescent reporter by transfection should no longer be detectable. Fluorescent expression at this time point indicates genomic integration of reporter sequence.

8.title: Detection of targeting events by PCR on genomic DNA of the mixed targeted cell population

protocol_hierarchical.8.1: Prepare gDNA from 2mL of cell suspension of the mixed targeted cell population from step 2.2.4. Use a gDNA extraction kit according to the manufacturer’s protocol. Then prepare the gDNA of non-targeted cells as a control.
protocol_hierarchical.8.2: Perform int. junction PCRs (primers P1/P2 and P3/P4 for 5' and z int. junction, respectively) and non-targeted allele PCR (primers P1/P4) using a high-fidelity DNA polymerase (see Tables 3 and 4 for PCR ingredients and cycling conditions). Analyze 5uL of PCR products on a 1.5% agarose/TAE gel. NOTE: If the mixed targeted population contains cells that have undergone genome engineering, a specific PCR product should be observed that is not detectable in gDNA of non-targeted cells (negative control). For the non-targeted allele PCR, one should observe a product of the same size for both the targeted and non-targeted cells (positive control for genomic P1 and P4 primers). If no bands are observed, consider altering PCR cycling conditions by increasing the number of cycles or altering the PCR buffer (for example through addition of DMSO or increased amounts of  M g2+ ), or by changing the polymerase.

9.title: Generation of Clonal Lines and Screening for Correct Targetin

10.title: Generation of single-cell clones through dilution plating

protocol_hierarchical.10.1: Prepare Jurkat-conditioned medium in advance: take off RPMI w/ AB medium from healthy, untreated Jurkat T cells grown to 1×106 cells/mL, centrifuge for 5 min at 300× g, and filter the supernatant using a 0.22um syringe filter unit. NOTE: Keep the conditioned medium at 4°C for short-term storage or at -20°C for storage longer than 1 week. Prepare 20 to 30mL of conditioned medium prior to dilution plating.
protocol_hierarchical.10.2: Count the targeted cells from step 2.2.4 after 10–14 days of expansion and dilute them in RPMI w/ AB to a concentration of 1×105 cells/mL. Take 100uL of 1×105 cells/mL solution and dilute with 9.9mL of medium to achieve a concentration of 1,000 cells/mL. Take 1 mL of 1,000 cells/mL solution and dilute with m L of medium to achieve a concentration of 100 cells/mL.
protocol_hierarchical.10.3: Plate 96-well plates containing 1 cell per well and 2 cells per well. For 1 cell per well, take 1 mL of 100 cells/mL solution and mix gently with 5mL of conditioned medium and 4mL of fresh medium in a sterile reagent reservoir.
protocol_hierarchical.10.4: For 2 cells per well, take 2mL of 100 cells/mL solution and mix gently with 5mL of conditioned medium and 3m L of fresh medium. Plate 96-well round-bottom plates with 100uL of the respective cell dilution per well using a multichannel pipet. NOTE: 5 to 10 96-well plates per targeting construct are sufficient to obtain clones for screening.
protocol_hierarchical.10.5: Stack the 96-well plates, cover each stack with a 6-well plate containing 3 mL of PBS in each well, and incubate the plates at 37°C in a humidified cell culture incubator with 5% C O_2 for 3 weeks. NOTE:  Do not change the cell culture medium during this time. Do not open the incubator more than once or twice a week. The best results are observed in incubators with open water reservoir.
protocol_hierarchical.10.6: After 3 weeks of incubation, visually confirm the presence of grown colonies using light microscopy (4X magnification) and mark the wells with grown colonies so they are visible as points on the bottom of the wells.
protocol_hierarchical.10.7: Prepare one 96-well round-bottom plate with 100uL of RPMI w/ AB per well. Gently resuspend cells of a marked well by pipetting. Transfer 100uL of cell suspension into one well of the new 96-well plate already containing 100uL of RPMI w/ AB, then mix gently by pipetting. Transfer 100uL of this cell suspension into a second empty 96-well round-bottom plate to duplicate the plate.
protocol_hierarchical.10.8: Continue with all marked wells with grown colonies. Fill all the blank wells with 200uL of RPMI w/ AB medium. Incubate the plates at 37° and 5% C O_2 NOTE:  One of these plates will serve for expansion of single-cell clones (“stock plate”) and the other as a “duplicate plate” for screening.

11.title: Screening of single-cell clones by flow cytometry and PCR

protocol_hierarchical.11.1: Let the duplicate plate incubate for 24 to 48h and duplicate the plate again. To do this, add 100uL of RPMI w/ AB to every well, mix gently by pipetting, and transfer 100uL to a new 96-well round-bottom plate using a multichannel pipet. Use one plate for flow cytometry screening and the other for PCR-based screening.
protocol_hierarchical.11.2: For flow cytometry screening, stimulate the cells with PMA-Iono. Prepare a mastermix of 0.1uL of Ionomocin (1 mM stock), 0.1 µL of PMA moonug/uL stock), and 4.8uL of RPMI w/ AB per number of wells, then add 5uL of mastermix per well. NOTE: Induction is necessary to successfully identify clones, where the LTR might be transcriptionally silent and therefore the fluorescent reporter is not expressed.
protocol_hierarchical.11.3: Let cells incubate for 24h and prepare cells for flow cytometry as described in step 2.3.3. Gate any viable single-cells based on size in forward and sideward scatter and analyze fluorescent reporter gene expression by flow cytometry (for example results, see Figure 3c). If tv backbone contains a second fluorescent reporter with promoter (e.g., GFP), screen any clones for backbone reporter expression also (see step 1.2.2 and the following note for explanation). NOTE:  Backbone reporter expression indicates unwanted integration of backbone sequences.
protocol_hierarchical.11.4: Once the clones in the second duplicate plate have grown sufficiently (usually 24 to 48h after duplication of the 96-well plate), prepare cell lysates containing gDNA for PCR screening. Centrifuge the plate for 10m i n at 300× g at RT. Carefully take off the supernatant without disturbing the cell pellet. NOTE: All steps for the preparation of lysates and PCR reactions can be performed with multichannel pipettes.
protocol_hierarchical.11.5: Wash cells with 100uL of PBS by gentle pipetting and centrifuging the plate for 5 min at 300× g at RT. Take off the PBS and add 200uL of lysis buffer 200 mM NaCl, 100m M Tris-HCl pH 8-8.5, 5mM EDTA, 0.1% SDS; then add 250-1,000 ug/mL of proteinase K (lyophilized powder, weigh in freshly) per well. Mix gently by pipetting, and transfer the suspension to a new PCR plate.
protocol_hierarchical.11.6: Seal the plate with paraffin film and incubate for 1h at 55°C in a thermocycler. Centrifuge at maximum speed for 10 min (3,000× g) to spin down cell debris, and transfer the supernatant to a new PCR plate. NOTE: Cell lysates in plates can be stored at this stage at 4°C until further use.
protocol_hierarchical.11.7: Prepare a 96-well PCR plate with 110uL of d H_2O and add 10uL of cell lysate (1:12 dilution). Cell lysates might be viscous and difficult to pipet. Use at least 20-uL pipet tips.
protocol_hierarchical.11.8: Inactivate proteinase K by incubation for 10 min at 99°C in a thermocycler. Subsequently use the inactivated and diluted cell lysates for PCR screening.
protocol_hierarchical.11.9: Design primers for screening PCR (P5 and P6) based on the chosen reporter sequence to amplify 500–800 bp of the reporter sequence. For positive control PCR, use primers P7 and P8 that amplify 630 bp of a wild-type, non-targeted genomic locus (NUP188 gene) (Figure 3c and Table 5). Design a third primer pair that amplifies 500–600 bp of the tv backbone as a control for unspecific integration of tv backbone sequences (backbone PCR).
protocol_hierarchical.11.10: For screening, control, and backbone PCR, use a commercial PCR mastermix (see Tables 6 and 7 for PCR ingredients and cycling conditions). Use 2uL of the diluted and inactivated lysate prepared in step 3.2.8 as a template and run PCR for 38 to 40 cycles of PCR amplification in 96-well format.
protocol_hierarchical.11.11: Analyze 5uL of PCR products on a 1.5% agarose/TAE gel. NOTE: For control PCR, a specific band of 630 bp should be observed for every sample, confirming that the quality of cell lysates is adequate for PCR. A specific band in screening PCR (500–800 bp depending on primer design) indicates integration of the reporter sequence. A specific band for backbone PCR (500–600 bp, depending on primer design) indicates unwanted integration of tv backbone sequences (for example results, see Figure 3c).
protocol_hierarchical.11.12: Combine the results of flow cytometry (step 3.2.3) and PCR-based screening (step 3.2.12). Select clones which show correct sizes of PCR products in screening PCR and positive control PCR and expression of fluorescent reporter after induction with PMA-Iono in flow cytometry. Exclude clones that show any PCR product in backbone PCR or expression of tv backbone-encoded fluorescent protein, indicating unspecific integration of tv backbone sequence.
protocol_hierarchical.11.13: Gradually expand selected clones from the 96-well stock plate to bigger well formats (48/24/12/6-well) until achieving a T75 cell culture flask format by adding fresh medium every 2 to 3 days. Maintain a cell density between 1×105 and 1×106 cells/mL.
protocol_hierarchical.11.14: Make sure to prepare cell stocks of clones during expansion: count the cells, centrifuge at 300× for 5 min at RT, discard the supernatant, and suspend the cells gently in F C S+10% DMSO at 5×106 cells/mL. Aliquot in cryogenic vials and use a cryo-freezing container to freeze the cells to 80°C at 1 °C/m i n . For long term storage, transfer them to liquid N_2 . NOTE: It is advisable to retain a T75 cell culture flask (i.e., around 1×107 cells) during the expansion in preparation of gDNA for verification of targeting by Southern blotting (see section 3.4).

12.title: Verification of integration sites by PCR/sequencing in selected clones DNA sequence level.

protocol_hierarchical.12.1: Prepare gDNA of the selected clones and Jurkat wild-type cells using a commercial gDNA extraction kit.
protocol_hierarchical.12.2: Use primer pairs binding the 5' end of the reporter and upstream of 5HA for 5° int. junction (primers P1 and P2) and the z end of the reporter and downstream of 3H A for z int. junction (primers P3 and P4) as described in step 2.4. Use primers P1 and P4 to amplify the targeted integration site on the allele without reporter integrant (Figure 4a).
protocol_hierarchical.12.3: Prepare PCR reactions with 100–200 ng of gDNA as a template and perform PCR using a polymerase with proofreading activity (see Tables 1 and 2 for PCR ingredients and cycling conditions). NOTE: If no bands are observed, consider altering the PCR cycling conditions by increasing the number of cycles or altering the PCR buffer (for example, by adding DMSO or increased amounts of M g2+. ), or by changing the polymerase.
protocol_hierarchical.12.4: Analyze 5uL of PCR products on a 1.5% agarose/TAE gel. If correct band sizes are observed, purify the remaining PCR product using a commercial kit and subject it to Sanger sequencing. Verify sequences of 5’ int. junction, z int. junction, and targeted site of the allele without reporter integrant by aligning them with expected sequences. NOTE: The homologous allele where the reporter has not integrated will likely show Cas9-mediated changes at the target site, such as nucleotide insertions or deletions (Figure 4a).
protocol_hierarchical.12.5: For clones that show correct int. junction sequences after alignment, perform a PCR amplifying the whole targeted reporter and subject it to Sanger sequencing to verify the correct sequence of the integrant.

13.title: Southern blot analysis for verification of targeting in selected clones Develop a strategy for appropriate gDNA digestion and probe the design prior to starting the experiment. 1. Select a restriction enzyme for gDNA restriction that generates appropriate fragments of 2 to 10 kb length at the targeted site. Certain restriction enzymes, such as Asp718, BamHI, BglI, BglII, EcoRV, HindIII, NcoI, PstI, PvuII, ScaI, StuI, and SstI have been successfully used for digesting high molecular weight gDNA. 2. Design two different Southern probes: a reporter-specific probe and genomic probe. The reporter-specific probe hybridizes to a sequence within the reporter (i.e., tdTomato-specific probe). The genomic probe hybridizes to a genomic region close to (but not overlapping) one HA. 3. Choose the genomic probe so that the fragment generated by gDNA digestion that will be detected by genomic probe binding differs in length (more than 2k b ) from the targeted and non-targeted alleles (Figure 4b). A probe length of 400 to 1,000 bp is recommended.

protocol_hierarchical.13.1: Use 7uL of the 60uL restriction digest for analytical gel electrophoresis on a 1% agarose/TAE gel. A smear indicates complete digestion and good DNA quality for Southern blot analysis.
protocol_hierarchical.13.2: Precipitate the remaining restriction digest by adding 1:10 3 M sodium acetate and 2 volumes 100% EtOH, then incubate for 1h at -80°C and centrifuge for 30m i n at 15,600× g at 4°C .
protocol_hierarchical.13.3: Digest (two times) 15u of gDNA of the selected clones and wild-type Jurkat cells with selected restriction enzyme (see step 3.4.1.1) in a 60uL reaction with 6uL of enzyme (20 Units /uL. ): first, add DNA, digestion buffer, and d d H_2O , incubate o/n at 37°C , then add enzyme and incubate o/n at enzyme-specific digestion temperature. 15u g of digested gDNA is required per Southern probe.
protocol_hierarchical.13.4: Discard the supernatant and wash the pellet with 70% EtOH. Centrifuge for 15 min at 15,600× g at 4°C , discard the supernatant, let the pellet dry briefly at RT, and dissolve in 20uL of d d H_2O .
protocol_hierarchical.13.5: Run 1% agarose/TAE blotting gel, loading 20 uL of digested gDNA per lane. Run the gel for 2h at 60 V, 400m A . NOTE: The percentage of agarose gel and running time/voltage may be adjusted according to expected fragment size for Southern blot detection calculated in step 3.4.1.1. The following steps of Southern blot analysis are described in detail in a supplementary protocol (steps 1 to 18). These steps comprise of: washing of the blotting gel, blotting onto a nylon membrane, radioactive probe generation, probe hybridization, and development of autoradiograph film. Compare the obtained banding pattern after autoradiograph development in step 18 (supplementary protocol) with the expected pattern according to Southern strategy (for example results, see Figure 4b).

14.title: Analysis of off-target events Use CCTop16 (http://crispr.cos.uni-heidelberg.de) to generate a list of the ten highest-ranked in silico predicted off-target sequences.

protocol_hierarchical.14.1: Input the gRNA sequence including PAM as used for targeting as the query sequence. Select “NGG” as PAM and “Human genome” as the reference for off-target prediction.

14.2.title: Set maximal total mismatches to 4 and target site length to the length of the gRNA without PAM. The output file will provide a ranked list of genomic off-target sites for respective gRNA.

protocol_hierarchical.14.2.1: In silico extract the genomic sequence 500 bp upstream and downstream of each of the ten highest-ranked off-target hits using UCSC Genome Browser (http://genome.edu.ucsc.edu) and the position of the off-target hit from CCTop results list.
protocol_hierarchical.14.2.2: For each off the target sites to be analysed, design a PCR primer pair that amplifies a fragment of 600 to 700 bp in length including the predicted off-target site.
protocol_hierarchical.14.2.3: Extract gDNA from the selected clones and Jurkat wild-type cells using a commercial gDNA extraction kit. For every off-target site, perform a PCR using a DNA polymerase with proofreading activity (see Tables 1 and 2 for PCR ingredients and cycling conditions) on wild-type and the respective clone-derived gDNA.
protocol_hierarchical.14.2.4: Analyze 5uL of PCR products on a 1.5% agarose/TAE gel. If correct band sizes are observed, purify the remaining PCR product using a commercial PCR purification kit and subject it to Sanger sequencing. Compare sequences of the off-target sites in Jurkat cells and the targeted clones.

problem: Need to generate in vitro models for HIV-1 infection that accurately reflect clinically relevant proviral integration sites using CRISPR-Cas9-based genome engineering.

query: Please review the attached document containing our protocol for 'Video Article Orthogonal Protein Purification Facilitated by a Small Bispecific Affinity Tag'. I need you to check it for errors and generate a machine-readable version.

ground_truth_answer.title: Video Article Orthogonal Protein Purification Facilitated by a Small Bispecific Affinity Tag
ground_truth_answer.abstract: Due to the high costs associated with purification of recombinant proteins the protocols need to be rationalized. For high-throughput efforts there is a demand for general methods that do not require target protein specific optimization . To achieve this, purification tags that genetically can be fused to the gene of interest are commonly used . The most widely used affinity handle is the hexa-histidine tag, which is suitable for purification under both native and denaturing conditions . The metabolic burden for producing the tag is low, but it does not provide as high specificity as competing affinity chromatography based strategies .Here, a bispecific purification tag with two different binding sites on a 46 amino acid, small protein domain has been developed. The albuminbinding domain is derived from Streptococcal protein G and has a strong inherent affinity to human serum albumin (HSA). Eleven surfaceexposed amino acids, not involved in albumin-binding4 , were genetically randomized to produce a combinatorial library. The protein library with the novel randomly arranged binding surface (Figure 1) was expressed on phage particles to facilitate selection of binders by phage display technology. Through several rounds of biopanning against a dimeric Z-domain derived from Staphylococcal protein A5 , a small, bispecific molecule with affinity for both HSA and the novel target was identified6 .The novel protein domain, referred to as ABDz1, was evaluated as a purification tag for a selection of target proteins with different molecular weight, solubility and isoelectric point. Three target proteins were expressed in Escherishia coli with the novel tag fused to their N-termini and thereafter affinity purified. Initial purification on either a column with immobilized HSA or Z-domain resulted in relatively pure products. Twostep affinity purification with the bispecific tag resulted in substantial improvement of protein purity. Chromatographic media with the Z-domain immobilized, for example MabSelect SuRe, are readily available for purification of antibodies and HSA can easily be chemically coupled to media to provide the second matrix.This method is especially advantageous when there is a high demand on purity of the recovered target protein. The bifunctionality of the tag allows two different chromatographic steps to be used while the metabolic burden on the expression host is limited due to the small size of the tag. It provides a competitive alternative to so called combinatorial tagging where multiple tags are used in combination1,7.
ground_truth_answer.protocol_text:
# 1. Cloning of the target gene/ABDz1-tagged fusion construct
1. Prepare PCR fragments of the ABDz1 gene flanked by suitable restriction sites for N-terminal ligation of the target gene in the expression plasmid (a plasmid containing ABDz1 is freely available through a material transfer agreement).
2. Cleave purified expression vector and PCR fragments with chosen restriction enzymes in a suitable reaction buffer. Purify the products before ligation.
3. Ligate the restricted ABDz1 fragment into the expression vector containing the gene of interest and transform the ligation product to E_ . coli (in the original method the RR1ΔM15 strain was used). Spread transformed cells on agar plates supplemented with suitable antibiotics for selection.
4. PCR screen a few colonies and sequence verify the resulting expression cassette by DNA sequencing.
5. Prepare plasmid from an overnight culture of a sequence verified colony and transform to the preferred expression strain (in the original method E. coli Rosetta (DE3), hosting a pRARE plasmid to improve the production of proteins encoded by human genes, were used).
# 2. Protein expression
1. Inoculate a single bacterial colony into 10ml of tryptic soy broth supplemented with 5/l yeast extract and appropriate antibiotics. Incubate at 150 rpm at 37°C overnight.
2. Inoculate 1 ml of overnight culture in 100ml fresh medium and induce protein expression (originally 1 mM isopropyl-  -D-thiogalactoside when a lac operon was utilized) when the cells reach the logarithmic growth phase.
3. Incubate at 150 rpm at 25°C overnight and harvest cells by centrifugation.
# 3. Orthogonal affinity purification
1. Resuspend the pellet in 25ml running buffer (25 mM Tris-HCl, 200 mM NaCl, 1 mM EDTA, 0.05% (w/v) Tween 20, pH 8.0) and disrupt the cells by sonication at 60% amplitude and -1.0/1.0 pulses for 3 min. Centrifuge the sample and filter the target protein containing supernatant (0.45um) before further purification.
2. Equilibrate either a 1 ml HSA Sepharose column or an NHS-activated column, previously coupled with HSA according to the supplier's recommendations, with 10 column volumes (CV) of running buffer at 1 ml/min on a suitable protein purification system. Load the bacterial lysate at 0.5ml/min and then reset the flow rate to 1 m/m i n .
3. Wash the column with 5 CV of running buffer followed by 5 CV of washing buffer (5 mM NH4Ac, pH 5.5).
4. Elute the sample with elution buffer (0.5 M HAc, pH 2.5) at 1 ml/min and collect fractions, monitor absorption at 280n m to select fractions from the eluted peak for further purification.
5. Pool the fractions with the highest protein concentration, dilute two times in SuRe running buffer ( 20m M phosphate, 150 mM NaCl, pH 7.2) and make sure the pH is around neutral. Add 1 M Tris-HCl pH 8 to increase pH if necessary.
6. Load the sample at 0.5 ml/min on a 1 ml HiTrap MabSelect SuRe column that has been equilibrated with 10 CV of SuRe running buffer at 1 ml/min. Reset the flow rate to 1 m l/m i n after loading.
7. Wash the column with 5 CV of SuRe running buffer (step 5) and elute the proteins with 0.2 M HAc, pH 2.7. For sensitive target proteins the eluate can be neutralized directly upon collection by addition of Tris-HCl. If the chromatographic steps are reversed the eluate from the MabSelect SuRe column can be diluted in running buffer (step 3.1) and the pH increased to around 8 by addition of 1 M Tris-HCl. In general, narrower peaks are observed when the SuRe matrix is employed in the second step.
# 4. Evaluation of purity by sodium dodecyl sulphate polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis (SDS-PAGE)
1. Load the purified fractions on a reducing SDS-PAGE and, if desired, analyze the molecular weight of the purified product by mass spectrometry. It is important to fully reduce the sample since a free cysteine in ABDz1 may cause dimerization of the product under nonreducing conditions.
# 5. Representative Results: As a proof of principle, orthogonal affinity purification facilitated by the ABDz1 tag was evaluated for three human target proteins representing different solubility classes, molecular weights and isoelectric points (Table 1). The ABDz1 gene was genetically fused to the target genes and the constructs were expressed in E. coli, Figure 2 shows a flow chart for all steps in the method consecutively. Following purification by the orthogonal protocol, samples collected at different points were analyzed by SDS-PAGE (Figure 3). The results clearly show the utility of a dual tag and two highly specific purification steps. Even though reasonable purity is achieved after the initial purification step as seen from the gels, the successive step yields a very pure product well suited for highly demanding applications. a. Molecular weight of ABDz1 tag alone is 6.3k D a , isoelectric point 6.7. b. Target protein represents a part of the Uniprot protein. c. http://www.uniprot.org. d. Solubility class of protein fragment with N-terminal His6ABP10 . # Table 1. Target proteins and fusion products with ABDz1 tag evaluated in a proof of principle study. Three unique human proteins with different molecular weight, solubility and isoelectric point were chosen for expression and purification by the orthogonal affinity approach.

1.title: Cloning of the target gene/ABDz1-tagged fusion construct

protocol_hierarchical.1.1: Prepare PCR fragments of the ABDz1 gene flanked by suitable restriction sites for N-terminal ligation of the target gene in the expression plasmid (a plasmid containing ABDz1 is freely available through a material transfer agreement).
protocol_hierarchical.1.2: Cleave purified expression vector and PCR fragments with chosen restriction enzymes in a suitable reaction buffer. Purify the products before ligation.
protocol_hierarchical.1.3: Ligate the restricted ABDz1 fragment into the expression vector containing the gene of interest and transform the ligation product to E_ . coli (in the original method the RR1ΔM15 strain was used). Spread transformed cells on agar plates supplemented with suitable antibiotics for selection.
protocol_hierarchical.1.4: PCR screen a few colonies and sequence verify the resulting expression cassette by DNA sequencing.
protocol_hierarchical.1.5: Prepare plasmid from an overnight culture of a sequence verified colony and transform to the preferred expression strain (in the original method E. coli Rosetta (DE3), hosting a pRARE plasmid to improve the production of proteins encoded by human genes, were used).
protocol_hierarchical.2.1: Inoculate a single bacterial colony into 10ml of tryptic soy broth supplemented with 5/l yeast extract and appropriate antibiotics. Incubate at 150 rpm at 37°C overnight.
protocol_hierarchical.2.2: Inoculate 1 ml of overnight culture in 100ml fresh medium and induce protein expression (originally 1 mM isopropyl-  -D-thiogalactoside when a lac operon was utilized) when the cells reach the logarithmic growth phase.
protocol_hierarchical.2.3: Incubate at 150 rpm at 25°C overnight and harvest cells by centrifugation.
protocol_hierarchical.3.1: Resuspend the pellet in 25ml running buffer (25 mM Tris-HCl, 200 mM NaCl, 1 mM EDTA, 0.05% (w/v) Tween 20, pH 8.0) and disrupt the cells by sonication at 60% amplitude and -1.0/1.0 pulses for 3 min. Centrifuge the sample and filter the target protein containing supernatant (0.45um) before further purification.
protocol_hierarchical.3.2: Equilibrate either a 1 ml HSA Sepharose column or an NHS-activated column, previously coupled with HSA according to the supplier's recommendations, with 10 column volumes (CV) of running buffer at 1 ml/min on a suitable protein purification system. Load the bacterial lysate at 0.5ml/min and then reset the flow rate to 1 m/m i n .
protocol_hierarchical.3.3: Wash the column with 5 CV of running buffer followed by 5 CV of washing buffer (5 mM NH4Ac, pH 5.5).
protocol_hierarchical.3.4: Elute the sample with elution buffer (0.5 M HAc, pH 2.5) at 1 ml/min and collect fractions, monitor absorption at 280n m to select fractions from the eluted peak for further purification.
protocol_hierarchical.3.5: Pool the fractions with the highest protein concentration, dilute two times in SuRe running buffer ( 20m M phosphate, 150 mM NaCl, pH 7.2) and make sure the pH is around neutral. Add 1 M Tris-HCl pH 8 to increase pH if necessary.
protocol_hierarchical.3.6: Load the sample at 0.5 ml/min on a 1 ml HiTrap MabSelect SuRe column that has been equilibrated with 10 CV of SuRe running buffer at 1 ml/min. Reset the flow rate to 1 m l/m i n after loading.
protocol_hierarchical.3.7: Wash the column with 5 CV of SuRe running buffer (step 5) and elute the proteins with 0.2 M HAc, pH 2.7. For sensitive target proteins the eluate can be neutralized directly upon collection by addition of Tris-HCl. If the chromatographic steps are reversed the eluate from the MabSelect SuRe column can be diluted in running buffer (step 3.1) and the pH increased to around 8 by addition of 1 M Tris-HCl. In general, narrower peaks are observed when the SuRe matrix is employed in the second step.

4.title: Evaluation of purity by sodium dodecyl sulphate polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis (SDS-PAGE)

protocol_hierarchical.4.1: Load the purified fractions on a reducing SDS-PAGE and, if desired, analyze the molecular weight of the purified product by mass spectrometry. It is important to fully reduce the sample since a free cysteine in ABDz1 may cause dimerization of the product under nonreducing conditions.

5.title: Representative Results: As a proof of principle, orthogonal affinity purification facilitated by the ABDz1 tag was evaluated for three human target proteins representing different solubility classes, molecular weights and isoelectric points (Table 1). The ABDz1 gene was genetically fused to the target genes and the constructs were expressed in E. coli, Figure 2 shows a flow chart for all steps in the method consecutively. Following purification by the orthogonal protocol, samples collected at different points were analyzed by SDS-PAGE (Figure 3). The results clearly show the utility of a dual tag and two highly specific purification steps. Even though reasonable purity is achieved after the initial purification step as seen from the gels, the successive step yields a very pure product well suited for highly demanding applications. a. Molecular weight of ABDz1 tag alone is 6.3k D a , isoelectric point 6.7. b. Target protein represents a part of the Uniprot protein. c. http://www.uniprot.org. d. Solubility class of protein fragment with N-terminal His6ABP10 . # Table 1. Target proteins and fusion products with ABDz1 tag evaluated in a proof of principle study. Three unique human proteins with different molecular weight, solubility and isoelectric point were chosen for expression and purification by the orthogonal affinity approach

1.title: Cloning of the target gene/ABDz1-tagged fusion construct

attached_document_content.1.1: Prepare PCR fragments of the ABDz1 gene flanked by suitable restriction sites for N-terminal ligation of the target gene in the expression plasmid (a plasmid containing ABDz1 is freely available through a material transfer agreement).
attached_document_content.1.2: Cleave purified expression vector and PCR fragments with chosen restriction enzymes in a suitable reaction buffer. Purify the products before ligation.
attached_document_content.1.3: Ligate the restricted ABDz1 fragment into the expression vector containing the gene of interest and transform the ligation product to E_ . coli (in the original method the RR1ΔM15 strain was used). Spread transformed cells on agar plates supplemented with suitable antibiotics for selection.
attached_document_content.1.4: PCR screen a few colonies and sequence verify the resulting expression cassette by DNA sequencing.
attached_document_content.1.5: Prepare plasmid from an overnight culture of a sequence verified colony and transform to the preferred expression strain (in the original method E. coli Rosetta (DE3), hosting a pRARE plasmid to improve the production of proteins encoded by human genes, were used).
attached_document_content.2.1: Inoculate a single bacterial colony into 10ml of tryptic soy broth supplemented with 5/l yeast extract and appropriate antibiotics. Incubate at 150 rpm at 37°C overnight.
attached_document_content.2.2: Inoculate 1 ml of overnight culture in 100ml fresh medium and induce protein expression (originally 1 mM isopropyl-  -D-thiogalactoside when a lac operon was utilized) when the cells reach the logarithmic growth phase.
attached_document_content.2.3: Incubate at 150 rpm at 25°C overnight and harvest cells by centrifugation.
attached_document_content.3.1: Resuspend the pellet in 25ml running buffer (25 mM Tris-HCl, 200 mM NaCl, 1 mM EDTA, 0.05% (w/v) Tween 20, pH 8.0) and disrupt the cells by sonication at 60% amplitude and -1.0/1.0 pulses for 3 min. Centrifuge the sample and filter the target protein containing supernatant (0.45um) before further purification.
attached_document_content.3.2: Equilibrate either a 1 ml HSA Sepharose column or an NHS-activated column, previously coupled with HSA according to the supplier's recommendations, with 10 column volumes (CV) of running buffer at 1 ml/min on a suitable protein purification system. Load the bacterial lysate at 0.5ml/min and then reset the flow rate to 1 m/m i n .
attached_document_content.3.3: Wash the column with 5 CV of running buffer followed by 5 CV of washing buffer (5 mM NH4Ac, pH 5.5).
attached_document_content.3.4: Elute the sample with elution buffer (0.5 M HAc, pH 2.5) at 1 ml/min and collect fractions, monitor absorption at 280n m to select fractions from the eluted peak for further purification.
attached_document_content.3.5: Pool the fractions with the highest protein concentration, dilute two times in SuRe running buffer ( 20m M phosphate, 150 mM NaCl, pH 7.2) and make sure the pH is around neutral. Add 1 M Tris-HCl pH 8 to increase pH if necessary.
attached_document_content.3.6: Load the sample at 0.5 ml/min on a 1 ml HiTrap MabSelect SuRe column that has been equilibrated with 10 CV of SuRe running buffer at 1 ml/min. Reset the flow rate to 1 m l/m i n after loading.
attached_document_content.3.7: Wash the column with 5 CV of SuRe running buffer (step 5) and elute the proteins with 0.2 M HAc, pH 2.7. For sensitive target proteins the eluate can be neutralized directly upon collection by addition of Tris-HCl. If the chromatographic steps are reversed the eluate from the MabSelect SuRe column can be diluted in running buffer (step 3.1) and the pH increased to around 8 by addition of 1 M Tris-HCl. In general, narrower peaks are observed when the SuRe matrix is employed in the second step.

It is important to fully reduce the sample since a free cysteine in ABDz1 may cause dimerization of the product under nonreducing conditions.
