query: I want to achieve 3D organotypic cultures of epithelial cells on a matrix embedded with mesenchymal cells are widely used to study epithelial cell differentiation and invasion.. What are the recommended experimental workflows for that goal?

ground_truth_answer.title: Video Article Tissue Engineering of Tumor Stromal Microenvironment with Application to Cancer Cell Invasion
ground_truth_answer.abstract: 3D organotypic cultures of epithelial cells on a matrix embedded with mesenchymal cells are widely used to study epithelial cell differentiation and invasion. Rat tail type I collagen and/or matrix derived from Engelbreth-Holm-Swarm mouse sarcoma cells have been traditionally employed as the substrates to model the matrix or stromal microenvironment into which mesenchymal cells (usually fibroblasts) are populated. Although experiments using such matrices are very informative, it can be argued that due to an overriding presence of a single protein (such as in type I Collagen) or a high content of basement membrane components and growth factors (such as in matrix derived from mouse sarcoma cells), these substrates do not best reflect the contribution to matrix composition made by the stromal cells themselves. To study native matrices produced by primary dermal fibroblasts isolated from patients with a tumor prone, genetic blistering disorder (recessive dystrophic epidermolysis bullosa), we have adapted an existing native matrix protocol to study tumor cell invasion. Fibroblasts are induced to produce their own matrix over a prolonged period in culture. This native matrix is then detached from the culture dish and epithelial cells are seeded onto it before the entire coculture is raised to the air-liquid interface. Cellular differentiation and/or invasion can then be assessed over time. This technique provides the ability to assess epithelial-mesenchymal cell interactions in a 3D setting without the need for a synthetic or foreign matrix with the only disadvantage being the prolonged period of time required to produce the native matrix. Here we describe the application of this technique to assess the ability of a single molecule expressed by fibroblasts, type VII collagen, to inhibit tumor cell invasion.
ground_truth_answer.protocol_text:
# 1. Preparation of Media and Reagents
1. Preparation of 200× of L-ascorbic acid 2-phosphate stock 1. Dissolve 29m g of L-ascorbic acid 2-phosphate per 5ml of Dulbecco’s Modified Eagle’s Medium (DMEM) solution and filter through 0.22um membrane filter. Store as 0.25ml sterile aliquots in -20°C . 2. Add 0.25ml aliquot of 200× L-ascorbic acid 2-phosphate stock to every 50ml of fibroblast media (DMEM with 1% L-glutamine and 10% fetal bovine serum) on the day required, for a final concentration of 0.1 m M of L-ascorbic acid 2-phosphate.
2. Preparation of keratinocyte growth media 1. Isolation and culture of primary SCC keratinocytes have been described previously21. Preparation of keratinocyte growth media is described therein as well. 2. Briefly, prepare the media as follows: 300ml of DMEM and 100m of Ham’s F-12 supplemented with 10% FBS 0.4m g/m l hydrocortisone 5mg/ml insulin 10n g/m l EGF 5mg/ml transferrin 8.4n g/m l cholera toxin 13n g/m l liothyronine 1x penicillin-streptomycin solution
# 2. In Vitro Construction of Fibroblast-derived Native Matrix in L-Ascorbic Acid 2-Phosphate Supplemented Media
1. Seed 200,000 fibroblasts per well in 6-well plates (20,000 cells/cm2) in fibroblast media supplemented with of L-ascorbic acid 2-phosphate. Refeed every 2-3 days with 2.5m of media. (Note: Refeeding frequency and volume may be adjusted to suit the individual needs of different cells. See “Discussions”).
2. A thick layer of cells embedded in extracellular matrix will form at the end of 6 weeks, visible to the naked eye (Figure 1). To release this layer from the tissue culture plate, gently scrape the circumference of matrix with a 1 ml micropipette tip and then push the edges of the matrix towards center of the well. This layer of cells and matrix (native matrix) should be easily detachable from the plate surface, and now be floating in the media. Spread the native matrix out in the media to avoid it folding up onto itself.
3. Let the native matrix float and remodel for 5 days, changing media every 2-3 days. Due to tensile strength and intrinsic remodeling within matrix, the matrix will contract drastically and become reduced to a smaller, but thicker native matrix, and is ready to be utilized for invasion assay.
# 3. Invasion Assay with Tumor SCC Keratinocytes
1. Pick up the native matrix gently with blunt forceps and transfer to Nylon net. Once on the Nylon net, spread the matrix gently to lie as flat as possible using a 1ml micropipette tip and blunt forceps.
2. Prepare the sterile clonal cylinders by smearing a small amount of sterile Vaseline on one end.
3. Place the clonal cylinders on the native matrix, with the Vaseline side down. This is to ensure a tight seal between the native matrix and the clonal rings.
4. Add cSCC cells to the clonal cylinders (250,000 cells in 100ul of keratinocyte growth media).
5. Remove the clonal cylinders after 6 hr when the cSCC cells have settled down on the native matrix.
6. Lift the Nylon net with the native matrix and cSCC cells to air-liquid interface onto bent stainless steel wire mesh support.
7. Add keratinocyte growth media supplemented with ascorbic acid until the media level touches the bottom of the native matrix.
8. Change media every 2-3 days and harvest at 7 and 14 days post-seeding of cSCC cells.
# 4. Harvesting of 3D Cultures and Preparation for Histology
1. Fix in 4% paraformaldehyde overnight at room temperature.
2. Bisect the samples and embed in wax for formalin-fixed paraffin embedded blocks, with the cut surface facing outwards.
3. Alternatively, embed the bisected samples in OCT and snap-freeze immediately in liquid nitrogen for fresh frozen tissue blocks.
4. Cut 4um sections on microtome and dewax (if necessary). Stain using standard haematoxylin and eosin. To visualize the cSCC cells, mouse monoclonal antibody against keratin 14 (LL001, in-house) can be used in immunohistology staining.

protocol_hierarchical.1.1: Preparation of 200× of L-ascorbic acid 2-phosphate stock 1. Dissolve 29m g of L-ascorbic acid 2-phosphate per 5ml of Dulbecco’s Modified Eagle’s Medium (DMEM) solution and filter through 0.22um membrane filter. Store as 0.25ml sterile aliquots in -20°C . 2. Add 0.25ml aliquot of 200× L-ascorbic acid 2-phosphate stock to every 50ml of fibroblast media (DMEM with 1% L-glutamine and 10% fetal bovine serum) on the day required, for a final concentration of 0.1 m M of L-ascorbic acid 2-phosphate.
protocol_hierarchical.1.2: Preparation of keratinocyte growth media 1. Isolation and culture of primary SCC keratinocytes have been described previously21. Preparation of keratinocyte growth media is described therein as well. 2. Briefly, prepare the media as follows: 300ml of DMEM and 100m of Ham’s F-12 supplemented with 10% FBS 0.4m g/m l hydrocortisone 5mg/ml insulin 10n g/m l EGF 5mg/ml transferrin 8.4n g/m l cholera toxin 13n g/m l liothyronine 1x penicillin-streptomycin solution

2.title: In Vitro Construction of Fibroblast-derived Native Matrix in L-Ascorbic Acid 2-Phosphate Supplemented Media

protocol_hierarchical.2.1: Seed 200,000 fibroblasts per well in 6-well plates (20,000 cells/cm2) in fibroblast media supplemented with of L-ascorbic acid 2-phosphate. Refeed every 2-3 days with 2.5m of media. (Note: Refeeding frequency and volume may be adjusted to suit the individual needs of different cells. See “Discussions”).
protocol_hierarchical.2.2: A thick layer of cells embedded in extracellular matrix will form at the end of 6 weeks, visible to the naked eye (Figure 1). To release this layer from the tissue culture plate, gently scrape the circumference of matrix with a 1 ml micropipette tip and then push the edges of the matrix towards center of the well. This layer of cells and matrix (native matrix) should be easily detachable from the plate surface, and now be floating in the media. Spread the native matrix out in the media to avoid it folding up onto itself.
protocol_hierarchical.2.3: Let the native matrix float and remodel for 5 days, changing media every 2-3 days. Due to tensile strength and intrinsic remodeling within matrix, the matrix will contract drastically and become reduced to a smaller, but thicker native matrix, and is ready to be utilized for invasion assay.

3.title: Invasion Assay with Tumor SCC Keratinocytes

protocol_hierarchical.3.1: Pick up the native matrix gently with blunt forceps and transfer to Nylon net. Once on the Nylon net, spread the matrix gently to lie as flat as possible using a 1ml micropipette tip and blunt forceps.
protocol_hierarchical.3.2: Prepare the sterile clonal cylinders by smearing a small amount of sterile Vaseline on one end.
protocol_hierarchical.3.3: Place the clonal cylinders on the native matrix, with the Vaseline side down. This is to ensure a tight seal between the native matrix and the clonal rings.
protocol_hierarchical.3.4: Add cSCC cells to the clonal cylinders (250,000 cells in 100ul of keratinocyte growth media).
protocol_hierarchical.3.5: Remove the clonal cylinders after 6 hr when the cSCC cells have settled down on the native matrix.
protocol_hierarchical.3.6: Lift the Nylon net with the native matrix and cSCC cells to air-liquid interface onto bent stainless steel wire mesh support.
protocol_hierarchical.3.7: Add keratinocyte growth media supplemented with ascorbic acid until the media level touches the bottom of the native matrix.
protocol_hierarchical.3.8: Change media every 2-3 days and harvest at 7 and 14 days post-seeding of cSCC cells.

4.title: Harvesting of 3D Cultures and Preparation for Histology

protocol_hierarchical.4.1: Fix in 4% paraformaldehyde overnight at room temperature.
protocol_hierarchical.4.2: Bisect the samples and embed in wax for formalin-fixed paraffin embedded blocks, with the cut surface facing outwards.
protocol_hierarchical.4.3: Alternatively, embed the bisected samples in OCT and snap-freeze immediately in liquid nitrogen for fresh frozen tissue blocks.
protocol_hierarchical.4.4: Cut 4um sections on microtome and dewax (if necessary). Stain using standard haematoxylin and eosin. To visualize the cSCC cells, mouse monoclonal antibody against keratin 14 (LL001, in-house) can be used in immunohistology staining.

problem: Need to develop a 3D organotypic culture system that accurately models the native tumor stromal microenvironment without relying on synthetic or foreign matrices.

query: I need to solve this problem: how to effectively apply the 'Video Article Imaging the Human Immunological Synapse' protocol for Immunological Techniques research. Please help me design an experiment.

ground_truth_answer.title: Video Article Imaging the Human Immunological Synapse
ground_truth_answer.abstract: The purpose of the method is to generate an immunological synapse (IS), an example of cell-to-cell conjugation formed by an antigen-presenting cell (APC) and an effector helper T lymphocyte (Th) cell, and to record the images corresponding to the first stages of the IS formation and the subsequent trafficking events (occurring both in the APC and in the Th cell). These events will eventually lead to polarized secretion at the IS. In this protocol, Jurkat cells challenged with Staphylococcus enterotoxin E (SEE)-pulsed Raji cells as a cell synapse model was used, because of the closeness of this experimental system to the biological reality (Th cell-APC synaptic conjugates). The approach presented here involves cell-to-cell conjugation, time-lapse acquisition, wide-field fluorescence microscopy (WFFM) followed by image processing (post-acquisition deconvolution). This improves the signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) of the images, enhances the temporal resolution, allows the synchronized acquisition of several fluorochromes in emerging synaptic conjugates and decreases fluorescence bleaching. In addition, the protocol is well matched with the end point cell fixation protocols (paraformaldehyde, acetone or methanol), which would allow further immunofluorescence staining and analyses. This protocol is also compatible with laser scanning confocal microscopy (LSCM) and other state-of-the-art microscopy techniques. As a main caveat, only those T cell-APC boundaries (called IS interfaces) that were at the right 90° angle to the focus plane along the Z-axis could be properly imaged and analyzed. Other experimental models exist that simplify imaging in the Z dimension and the following image analyses, but these approaches do not emulate the complex, irregular surface of an APC, and may promote non-physiological interactions in the IS. Thus, the experimental approach used here is suitable to reproduce and to confront some biological complexities occurring at the IS.
ground_truth_answer.protocol_text:
# 1. Preparation of Slides to Adhere Raji Cells
1. Add 150uL of fibronectin (100 u/m L) per well to an 8 microwell chamber slide (plastic-bottom chamber slide) and incubate it for 30 min to 1 h at 37°C . This adhesion substrate will allow the binding of Raji cells to the well bottom (Step 2), the formation of living conjugates with Jurkat cells (Step 4) cells, and time-lapse microscopy capture (step 6), and is also compatible afterwards with optional paraformaldehyde (PFA) fixation (step 7). NOTE: For acetone fixation, use glass bottom chamber slides and poly-L-lysine (20u/mL) instead of fibronectin, since acetone dissolve plastic. The 8 microwell chamber slide ( 1c m2 well, 300uL maximum volume) or equivalent is a flexible and appropriate format.
2. Aspirate fibronectin using a 200uL automatic pipette and wash each well with 200uL of PBS for 2 min with gentle shaking. Repeat this wash one more time. The chamber slide can be stored at this stage with PBS for 1-2 weeks at 4°C .
# 2. Adhesion of Raji Cells to the Chamber Slides and 7-amino-4-chloromethylcoumarin (CMAC) Labeling
1. Transfer 10m L of a confluent ( angle1-2×106 cells/mL) pre-culture of Raji cells to a 15mL , V-bottom tube. Mix well and use 10uL to count the cells on Neubauer chamber or equivalent.
2. Centrifuge the remaining cells at 300× g for 5 min at room temperature. Aspirate and discard the supernatant.
3. Gently resuspend the cell pellet in warm complete culture medium (RPMI 1640 supplemented with 10% FCS, 2 mM glutamine, 10m M HEPES, 100U/mL penicillin, and 100 u/m L streptomycin) at a concentration of 10dot6 cells/mL. Use the equation given below: (Gamma_i n i t i a l×V_i n i t i a l=Gamma_f i n a l×V_f i n a l) , where _initial represents initial cell concentration, V_initial= initial volume of the cell suspension, _f i n a l= final cell concentration, V_f i n a l= final volume of the cell suspension. NOTE: Depending on the cell concentration of the starting culture, it is possible to collect more cells than needed but it is important to maintain the remaining cells in culture (37 °C) till the end of the experiment in order to prevent potential problems (see Step 2.6).
4. Label Raji cells to allow for their identification during the synaptic conjugate formation. In this experiment, 7-amino-4-chloromethylcoumarin (CMAC) labeling is performed in step 2.4.2.
1. Transfer the required number of Raji cells in the culture medium to a 2 mL tube. For the 8 microwell chamber slide, a total of 1.6m L of the cell suspension is needed 200uL per well).
2. Add CMAC to a final concentration of 10uM . Keep the cells in the dark by covering the tube with aluminum foil since CMAC is lightsensitive. 200uL containing 2×105 Raji cells are needed per 1c m2 well. Thus, if 8 wells need to be prepared, 1.6×106 Raji cells are required. NOTE: Labeling Raji cells with cell tracker blue (CMAC, UV excitation and blue emission) distinguishes them from Th cells when the synaptic conjugates are formed. This dye is compatible with PFA and acetone fixatives and allows further immunofluorescence procedures. Try to avoid light exposition. The labeling of Raji cells in a pool with CMAC followed by resuspension before the adhesion of the Raji cells to the fibronectin-coated chamber slides ensures the homogeneous labeling of Raji cells with CMAC among different wells.
5. Resuspend CMAC-stained cells and, after aspiration of the PBS in the chamber slide from step 1.2., transfer 200uL of the cell suspension to each well of the fibronectin-coated chamber slides prepared in step 1.1-1.2. Incubate the chamber slide at 37°C , 5% C O_2 for 30 min-1 h. NOTE: The adhesion and CMAC labeling will simultaneously occur at this step and this saves time. Please be aware that Raji cells will sediment quickly and caution needs to be taken to maintain a homogeneous concentration in the cell suspension before seeding. It is not necessary to wash CMAC at this stage by centrifugation, since CMAC washing will be more easily done at step 2.7 (when the labeled Raji cells are already adhered to the chamber slides). Since CMAC is present in the cell suspension in large excess, the blue fluorescence background is too high to distinguish the blue-stained cells. Check cell CMAC fluorescence in step 2.7 after CMAC washing.
6. Ensure that Raji cells are adhered to the bottom of the wells by gentle shaking of the chamber slides on the microscope. Ensure that the cells display gaps among each other and are not confluent (Figure 1, middle panel). 50-60% of cell confluence is appropriate. 1. If most of the cells efficiently adhere to the chamber slide and no cell gaps are observed, wash each well with warm complete medium and resuspend the medium with a 200uL automatic pipet to detach cell excess. Check confluence after each resuspension step. 2. If the cells do not adhere, repeat the adhesion step again and increase adhesion time and/or cell number. NOTE: It is possible to stop here, incubate the chamber slide at 37°C , 5% C O_2 overnight (O/N), and continue with the protocol the next day. Please confirm the next day that Raji cells remain adhered and CMAC-labeled by using fluorescence microscopy.
7. Wash each well again carefully with warm supplemented RPMI to eliminate excess CMAC and check for blue emission with the fluorescence microscope (Figure 1). NOTE: To avoid the use of immersion oil (sticky and viscous) and high numerical aperture oil objectives, extra-long distance (i.e., 20× or 40× ) objectives can be used to quickly check CMAC labeling using the fluorescence microscope.
# 3. Pulse of CMAC — Labeled Raji Cells with Staphylococcal Enterotoxin E
1. Add Staphylococcal Enterotoxin E (SEE, 1 ug/mL) to each well. SEE can be conveniently diluted in cell culture medium (working solution at 100 ug/m L) from the SEE frozen stocks 1mg/mL in PBS). Use 2uL of the 100× working solution per 200uL microwell. CAUTION: Use gloves for this step and dispose of the used tip into the biohazard box.
2. Incubate the chamber slide at 37°C , 5% C O_2 for at least 30m i n . The SEE effect lasts for at least 3-4h . NOTE: SEE can be added to the wells at different time points when required, if distinct time-lapse setups are planned (step 5) and/or depending of the start time point for the end point experiments (step 6).
# 4. Preparation of Jurkat Cells
1. Use a previously growing culture of Jurkat cells .1-2×106. cells/mL) for this experiment. Use cells from a standard culture flask or from a previous transfection following standard electroporation protocols, as previously described23. The transfection of Jurkat cells will allow time lapse visualization of the traffic of secretory granules in living cells. For instance, when GFP-CD63 (a marker of MVB) is expressed the movement of GFP-CD63-decorated vesicles can be recorded (Video 1).
2. Observe the cells under a phase contrast microscope. If an excess of dead cells (>20-30%) ) are observed, perform Ficoll density gradient centrifugation using standard protocols24, to eliminate the excess of dead cells (dead cells exhibit higher density than living cells) prior to use (see Discussion).
3. Transfer the cells to a 15mL tube, V-bottom tube, and use 10uL for counting using hemocytometer.
4. Centrifuge the remaining cells as described in step 2.2. Discard the supernatant and resuspend the cells at the same concentration as Raji cells (1ar×106/m L) using fresh, warm culture medium. Follow steps 2.2-2.3.
5. Maintain the Jurkat cells in culture (37°C , 5% C O_2, ) while waiting for the Step 4. NOTE: In the second option (transfection), the number of living cells is going to be much lower than in the first one. Thus, consider using a higher starting cell culture volume in order to have enough cells for the experiment. From 10×106 Jurkat cells per electroporation cuvette and transfection, only 2-4×106 Jurkat cells will survive after 48h of transfection and some of these cells will be lost during the Ficoll step. Thus, one electroporation cuvette is generally sufficient to challenge the adhered SEE-pulsed Raji cells from 8 micro wells ( 1.6×106 transfected Jurkat cells needed).
# 5. Co-seeding of Raji and Jurkat Cells
1. Take the chamber slides containing the CMAC-labeled, SEE-pulsed, adhered Raji cells out of the incubator from step 3.2. It is not necessary to wash the CMAC at this stage since this was previously done in Step 2.7.
2. Aspirate carefully the culture medium of each well, one by one, from one corner of the well using an automatic 200uL pipette. Do not let the medium in the well dry out completely.
3. Immediately replace the medium with 200uL of resuspended Jurkat cells in cell culture medium angle1×106/m L prepared in step 4.5. If time lapse imaging is performed, go to step 6 immediately after this step, since Jurkat cells tend to sediment and form synaptic conjugates very quickly. For convenience, the microwells containing SEE-pulsed, adhered Raji cells that do not receive seeding with Jurkat cells at this stage should be maintained with cell culture medium until subsequent challenge with Jurkat cells. This will flexibly allow subsequent challenge with Jurkat cells for additional time lapse or reverse kinetic, end point experimental approaches. 4. If a time lapse is going to be performed, quickly proceed to step 6. This involves the co-culture for 12 h on the microscope stage incubator or equivalent at 37°C , 5% C O_2 to allow the synaptic conjugate formation and simultaneous image acquisition. If end point analysis, but no time lapse, is foreseen check conjugate formation after the co-culture period using the microscope (as in Figure 1) before fixing the cells (step 7).
# 6. Time Lapse Imaging of Emerging Synaptic Conjugates
1. Prepare the microscope and incubation chamber prior to imaging. For the example shown in Video 1, the detailed microscopy settings are shown in Figure 2. be prepared before adding the Jurkat suspension to the chamber slide with adhered Raji cells. The following steps are described for a commercial microscope (Table of Materials). However, any inverted fluorescence microscope equipped with a cell culture incubator can be used. 1. Use a microscope with a 60× oil-immersion, high numerical aperture when imaging polarized traffic. 2. Ensure that the automatic focus system is switched on and adjust the offset to focus the Raji cells bound to the bottom surface. Please refer to Figure 1, Video 1 and Video 2.
2. After Jurkat addition to each well containing the adhered Raji cells in step 5.3, quickly locate the microwell chamber slide on the pre-heated (1-2 h) microscope stage incubator (i.e., OKOlab) and select some XY positions with the microscope, fields in which it is likely to record an emerging IS formation made by, for instance, a Jurkat-transfected cell falling into the microscope focus.
3. Use a pre-heated microscope stage incubator since it was observed that a temperature-stabilized stage maintains stable X,Y,Z positions. Criteria for a convenient XY field are: well-focused and non-confluent Raji cells (i.e., displaying gaps among cells) and the presence of transfected Jurkat cells (this can be checked by combining transmittance and UV or GFP channels). Jurkat cells will sediment very quickly (few minutes) on the chamber slide, and the chances to image emerging synapses will decrease with time (Figure 1). It is possible either to finish the experiment after the defined time lapse or proceed to the Step 7 and fix the conjugates for subsequent immunofluorescence and analyses.
# 7. End Point Formation of Synaptic Conjugates and Fixation
1. If only an endpoint experiment is planned (1-2 hours incubation to allow synaptic conjugate formation is appropriate), incubate the chamber slide at 37°C , 5% C O_2 for 12h . Check conjugate formation after the culture period (as in Figure 1) and, subsequently, fix the conjugates with acetone or PFA (fixation will depend on the antigens and antibodies to be used in the subsequent immunofluorescence). In this case the incubation does not require a microscope stage incubator. Please refer to Video 3 for an example.
2. To fix the cells, wash the well by gentle shaking with warm RPMI (37 °C) medium without FCS (albumin from serum may precipitate with acetone fixation). Aspirate and add 200uL of PFA or pre-chilled acetone to each well. Incubate the chamber slide at room temperature (RT) or on ice, respectively, for 20m i n .
3. Wash each well twice with PBS and add 200uL of quenching solution (PBS, 50m MN H_4C I_. ). Incubate the chamber slide at 4°C . NOTE: At this stage the chamber slide can stay for at least one month at 4°C before performing the immunofluorescence protocol, as described8. The lid prevents evaporation.
# 8. Image Processing
1. Perform post-acquisition image deconvolution (i.e. Huygens deconvolution or equivalent, Table of Materials) of time-lapse series and/or still photos of fixed cells. Deconvolute by employing an appropriate software (i.e., using the "wide field" optical option in Huygens) and the correct optical parameters. Deconvolution necessitates for the image processing of the measured point spread function (PSF) of the microscope.
2. Alternatively, use the software to calculate the idealized PSF by automatic loading of the optical parameters included among the metadata from the microscope files. These optical parameters comprise fluorochrome wavelength, refraction index, numerical aperture of the objective, and the imaging technique (confocal, wide-field, etc.).
3. Subsequently, the imaging software uses the PSF and diverse deconvolution algorithms (i.e., QMLE and CMLE in Huygens software) in a step-by-step accumulative, calculation process, whose results than can be continuously visualized and stopped (or resumed) when required by the user. At this stage the user can change the number of convolutions and/or the signal to noise ratio and resume deconvolution. The deconvolution software works well with time lapse series (X,Y,T) (Video 2) and Z-stacks (X,Y,Z) (Video 3). The deconvoluted channels were subsequently merged to the CMAC, raw channel, since cytosolic, diffuse fluorochromes do not improve by deconvolution4,9.

Ensure that the automatic focus system is switched on and adjust the offset to focus the Raji cells bound to the bottom surface. Please refer to Figure 1, Video 1 and Video 2.
protocol_hierarchical.6.2: After Jurkat addition to each well containing the adhered Raji cells in step 5.3, quickly locate the microwell chamber slide on the pre-heated (1-2 h) microscope stage incubator (i.e., OKOlab) and select some XY positions with the microscope, fields in which it is likely to record an emerging IS formation made by, for instance, a Jurkat-transfected cell falling into the microscope focus.
protocol_hierarchical.6.3: Use a pre-heated microscope stage incubator since it was observed that a temperature-stabilized stage maintains stable X,Y,Z positions. Criteria for a convenient XY field are: well-focused and non-confluent Raji cells (i.e., displaying gaps among cells) and the presence of transfected Jurkat cells (this can be checked by combining transmittance and UV or GFP channels). Jurkat cells will sediment very quickly (few minutes) on the chamber slide, and the chances to image emerging synapses will decrease with time (Figure 1). It is possible either to finish the experiment after the defined time lapse or proceed to the Step 7 and fix the conjugates for subsequent immunofluorescence and analyses.

7.title: End Point Formation of Synaptic Conjugates and Fixation

protocol_hierarchical.7.1: If only an endpoint experiment is planned (1-2 hours incubation to allow synaptic conjugate formation is appropriate), incubate the chamber slide at 37°C , 5% C O_2 for 12h . Check conjugate formation after the culture period (as in Figure 1) and, subsequently, fix the conjugates with acetone or PFA (fixation will depend on the antigens and antibodies to be used in the subsequent immunofluorescence). In this case the incubation does not require a microscope stage incubator. Please refer to Video 3 for an example.
protocol_hierarchical.7.2: To fix the cells, wash the well by gentle shaking with warm RPMI (37 °C) medium without FCS (albumin from serum may precipitate with acetone fixation). Aspirate and add 200uL of PFA or pre-chilled acetone to each well. Incubate the chamber slide at room temperature (RT) or on ice, respectively, for 20m i n .
protocol_hierarchical.7.3: Wash each well twice with PBS and add 200uL of quenching solution (PBS, 50m MN H_4C I_. ). Incubate the chamber slide at 4°C . NOTE: At this stage the chamber slide can stay for at least one month at 4°C before performing the immunofluorescence protocol, as described8. The lid prevents evaporation.
protocol_hierarchical.8.1: Perform post-acquisition image deconvolution (i.e. Huygens deconvolution or equivalent, Table of Materials) of time-lapse series and/or still photos of fixed cells. Deconvolute by employing an appropriate software (i.e., using the "wide field" optical option in Huygens) and the correct optical parameters. Deconvolution necessitates for the image processing of the measured point spread function (PSF) of the microscope.
protocol_hierarchical.8.2: Alternatively, use the software to calculate the idealized PSF by automatic loading of the optical parameters included among the metadata from the microscope files. These optical parameters comprise fluorochrome wavelength, refraction index, numerical aperture of the objective, and the imaging technique (confocal, wide-field, etc.).
protocol_hierarchical.8.3: Subsequently, the imaging software uses the PSF and diverse deconvolution algorithms (i.e., QMLE and CMLE in Huygens software) in a step-by-step accumulative, calculation process, whose results than can be continuously visualized and stopped (or resumed) when required by the user. At this stage the user can change the number of convolutions and/or the signal to noise ratio and resume deconvolution. The deconvolution software works well with time lapse series (X,Y,T) (Video 2) and Z-stacks (X,Y,Z) (Video 3). The deconvoluted channels were subsequently merged to the CMAC, raw channel, since cytosolic, diffuse fluorochromes do not improve by deconvolution4,9.

problem: Need to effectively image and analyze the formation and dynamics of the human immunological synapse using a biologically relevant cell model with high temporal and spatial resolution.

query: I need a Standard Operating Procedure (SOP) for the Plant Science & Technology method, as detailed as possible.

ground_truth_answer.title: Determination of Root Exudate Concentration in the Rhizosphere Using ^13C Labeling
ground_truth_answer.abstract: One of the most remarkable metabolic features of plant roots is their ability to secrete a wide range of compounds into the rhizosphere, defined as the volume of soil around living roots. Around 5% - 21% of total photosynthetically fixed carbon is transferred into the rhizosphere through root exudates. Until recently, studies on the quantity and quality of root exudates were conducted mostly under axenic or monoxenic in vitro conditions. Today, in situ assays are required to provide a better understanding of root exudates dynamics and role in plant-microbe interactions. By incubating plants with ^13CO_2 in situ for one week and quantifying ^13C enrichment from the root-adhering soil using mass spectrometry, we were able to determine root exudate levels. Indeed, labeled substrate ^13CO_2 is converted into organic carbon via plant photosynthesis and transferred into the soil through root exudation. We assume that all ^13C increases above natural abundance are mainly derived from exudates produced by ^13C -labeled plants.
ground_truth_answer.protocol_text: 1.  Sieve the soil (1 mm mesh size) and adjust it to 0.17 g of water per gram of dry weight. Afterward, place 170 g of dry weight into pots. Place one seed per pot and do six replicates per plant species and six replicates for unplanted-soil (bulk soil).2.  Place the pots (six replicates per plant and six replicates for bulk soil) in a growth chamber and set up the day-night period, light intensity and daily temperature according to the studied plant (Guyonnet et al., 2018b). For Brasica napus, Arabidopsis thaliana, Triticum aestivum, Medicago truncatula and Poaceae plants, for example, the day-night period is set at 8h/16h , respectively; light intensity is 13.5 klux; maximum daily temperatures ranges from 20 to 22~^°C . Control and adjust soil moisture by weighing pots using a laboratory balance and adding distilled water every2 days (Figure 1).3.  Three weeks after sowing seeds, inject pure ^13CO_2 to three replicates per plant and bulk soil during active photosynthesis, according to Haichar et al. (2008 and 2012), in order to maintain a ^13CO_2 level  at 350~uvertvert^-1 for one week. Control ^12CO_2 (soil  respiration)  and ^13CO_2 concentrations in the growth chamber. The isotope excess of ^13C atoms in the chamber must be maintained at >80% during the first 10 days and >90% thereafter.4.  Cultivate the rest of the plants (three replicates per plant) and bulk soil (three replicates for unplanted soil) without ^13C -labeling in order to determine ^13C natural abundance in soil and plants.5.  At the end of labeling, separate the root system from the root-adhering soil (RAS) by shaking each plant and collect the root-adhering soil. Carefully separate the remaining fine roots from the RAS and freeze the RAS immediately in liquid N_2 before storing at -80~^°C (Figure 1).6.  Lyophilise the RAS during 48~h~ and grind to a fine powder 100~um particle size; visual verification is sufficient) using a mixer mill to homogenize before measuring ^13C enrichment (Figure 2A). The duration of the grinding step should be adapted to each type of soil (approximately 1 min at 30H z ; repeat if needed until a fine powder is obtained).()roots issued from unlabelled plants# Figure 1. Plant growth, labeling and harvesting in order to measure root exudation rate7.  Weigh 8 mg (adjust the target mass to obtain a sufficient amount of carbon, commonly >10u of C depending on the C concentration of the sample and IRMS sensitivity) of lyophilized RAS samples into 5×8m m “Ultra Clean” tin capsules to the nearest 0.01 mg (Figure 2B). Note: In calcareous soils, inorganic carbon (carbonates) can distort the ^13C measurement of organic carbon. In this case, remove inorganic c by acid fumigation. Weigh soil samples into silver capsules instead of tin. Add a small amount (50-150mu) of distilled water in each capsule to moisten the soil. Place each open capsule in a desiccator with a beaker of fuming (37%) hydrochloric acid (HCl) during 6 to 8 h. Dry at 60~^°C . Crimp the capsule and place it in a new tin capsule.8.  Carefully seal the tin capsules using forceps into a cubical or spherical shape (Figure 2C).9.  Measure the ^13C/^12C ratio and C concentration of each sample by EA-IRMS (Figure 2D) after optimizing the source parameters and calibration according to good practice (e.g., Dunn and Carter, 2018). Intersperse reference materials with the samples to allow for the normalization of the data and quality control.()A. Ground soil sampleRoot-adhering soil (RAS) ground using mixer mil# B. Weighing()Soil sample weighing in tin(Sn) capsule at the nearest 0.001 mg using a microbalance# D. ^13mathbbC measurement()C. Capsule sealingTin capsule containing the soil sample sealed with forceps into a cubic shape()Isotope ratio mass spectrometer (left) coupled in continuous flow to an elemental analyser (right) used for the determination of ^13 C/^12 C and C concentration of soil samplesFigure 2. Scheme of soil sample preparation for the ^13C/^12C ratio measure using Elemental Analyser-Isotope Ratio Mass Spectrometer (EA-IRMS)

query: Can you generate an experimental protocol for 'Staphylococcus  aureus  30S  Ribosomal Subunit Purification and Its Biochemical and Cryo-EM Analysis' for me?

ground_truth_answer.title: Staphylococcus  aureus  30S  Ribosomal Subunit Purification and Its Biochemical and Cryo-EM Analysis
ground_truth_answer.abstract: The ribosome is a complex cellular machinery whose solved structure allowed for an incredible leap in structural biology research. Different ions bind to the ribosome, stabilizing inter-subunit interfaces and structurally linking rRNAs, proteins, and ligands. Besides cations such as K^+ and Mg^2+ , polyamines are known to stabilize the folding of RNA and overall structure. The bacterial ribosome is composed of a small (30S) subunit containing the decoding center and a large (50S) subunit devoted to peptide bond formation. We have previously shown that the small ribosomal subunit of Staphylococcus aureus is sensitive to changes in ionic conditions and polyamines concentration. In particular, its decoding center, where mRNA codons and tRNA anticodons interact, is prone to structural deformations in the absence of spermidine. Here, we report a detailed protocol for the purification of the intact and functional 30S, achieved through specific ionic conditions and the addition of spermidine. Using this protocol, we obtained the cryo-electron microscopy (cryo-EM) structure of the 30S–mRNA complex from S. aureus at 3.6textupAA resolution. The 30S–mRNA complex formation was verified by a toeprinting assay. In this article, we also include a description of toeprinting and cryo-EM protocols. The described protocols can be further used to study the process of translation regulation.
ground_truth_answer.protocol_text: # A. 70S ribosome purification1.  Streak Staphylococcus aureus’ RN6390 cells on a blood agar plate and grow at 37 ^°C for ^20h .2.  Inoculate a single colony in 50mL of sterile BHI medium in a 250~mL flask and grow for 17~h at 37~^°C and 180rpm .3.  Add 5mL of overnight culture to four 2L flasks, each filled with 500mL of sterile BHI medium, and grow for ^3~h~ at 37 ^°C and 180rpm until OD_600=1 .4.  Harvest cells by centrifugation at 4,400× g for 15min at 4^°C .5. Resuspend the pellet in 50mL of buffer A.6.  Collect the pellet by centrifugation at 4,400× g for 15min .7. Repeat the previous steps (5–6) two times.8. Weigh cells and keep at -80~^°C . Note: The typical yield is 2.varthetag of cells from 2L of culture.9.  Thaw the cells _(2.8~g~) on ice for 15-20 min and resuspend in 6.25~mL of buffer A supplemented with 0.7 mg lysostaphin, 1× protease inhibitor cocktail, 1~mM EDTA pH7.5 , and 6~ul DNase I per 1 gram of cell weight. Incubate for 1h at 37 ^°C .10. Spin down the lysate at 21,500× g (JA-25.50 rotor) for 90min at 4^°C .11. Adjust the PEG20K concentration in the collected supernatant to 2.8% and leave the reaction on ice under agitation for 5:min .12. Collect the supernatant by centrifugation at 20,000× g for 5 min (JA-25.50 rotor).13. Increase the PEG20K concentration to 4.2% and leave the reaction on ice under agitation for an additional 10min .14. Collect the supernatant by centrifugation at 20,000× g for 10min (JA-25.50 rotor).15. Resuspend each pellet in 17.5mL buffer A with 1~mM EDTA, lay on a 25mL sucrose cushion (buffer B), and spin at 235,000×g for 17h at 4^°C (Type 45Ti rotor).16. Rinse the walls of each tube with 5mL of buffer E to remove excess sucrose and resuspend the pellet in 1 mL of buffer E.17. Lay the obtained sample on twelve 7%-30% sucrose gradients and spin at 83L,000× g for 16.5h (SW28 rotor).18. Fractionate sucrose gradients (Figure 1A-I), pool fraction of 70S, and adjust the magnesium acetate to 25 mM and PEG20K to 4.5% . Leave on ice under agitation for 10min .19. Pellet down the ribosomes by centrifugation at 20,000×g for 13min (JA-25.50 rotor) (Khusainov et al., 2016).20. Wash the tube walls with the dissociation buffer to remove the excess of sucrose and PEG. Resuspend the pellet in 1.5mL of dissociation buffer. Note: The usual yield is 14 mg of 70S with at least 95% purity.21. Check the purity of 70S by 15% polyacrylamide gel (Figure 1A-II) and by mass-spectrometry analysis (Figure 1A-III).# B.  30S subunit purification1.  Load 14~mg of 70S in the dissociation buffer directly on 0%-30% sucrose gradients equilibrated in the same buffer and spin at 109,000×g for 14.5h (SW28 rotor).2.  Fractionate the sucrose gradients (Figure 1B-I) and adjust the magnesium acetate of pooled fractions to 10 mM and spermidine to 2.5mM .3.  Concentrate the 30S sample to 2-3~mg/mL in the Amicon ultra centrifugal filter. Note: By using two filters in parallel, it takes 1 h to concentrate the sample.4.  Make aliquots, flash freeze them in liquid nitrogen, and store at -80~^°C . Note: The volume of the aliquots should not be less than 3 μL to prevent evaporation of the sample. Moreover, the size of the aliquots should be small (3-5mu L) , as a large amount of sample is not required for subsequent experiments.()# Figure 1. The 30S ribosomal subunits purification.The 30S (B) were obtained from 70S (A) by running through sucrose gradients twice (I and IV, 35~mL is the total volume of sucrose gradients). The bold blue line represents the fraction used for further experiments. Both 70S and 30S fractions were run on SDS-PAGE to check the purity of the samples (II and V). 70S ribosome was sent to mass spectrometry analysis to assess the sample purity (III). 16S was obtained from 30S by phenol/chloroform extraction and run on agarose gel in denaturing conditions to represent its integrity (VI).# C. spa mRNA purificationThe plasmid used in this study was previously described in Benito et al. (2000) and Huntzinger et al. (2005). The pUT7 plasmid contains a T7 promoter for T7 in vitro transcription and ampicillin resistance. spa mRNA sequence with a strong Shine–Dalgarno AGGGGG (underlined) and UUG start codon (bold) is shown below: ACAAAUACAUACAGGGGGUAUUAAUUUGAAAAAGAAAAACAUUUAUUCAAUUCGUAAACUA GGUGUAGGUAUUGCAUCUGUAACUUUAGGUACAUUACUUAUAUCUGGUGGCGUAACACCUG CUGCAAAUGCUGCGCAACACGAUGAAGCUCAACAAAAUGCUUUUUAUCAAGUCUUAAAUAUGCCUAACUUAAACGCUGGGAUCCUCUAGAGUCGACCUGCAGGNote: Before starting to work with the mRNA, wipe the bench and pipettes with a liquid that removes RNase contamination (e.g., RNaseZap).1.  Transform XL1 Escherichia coli cells with the pUT7-spa plasmid.2.  Grow cells in 250mL of LB medium with 100~ug/mL ampicillin at 37 ^°C for 16h .3.  Purify the plasmid using a NucleoSpin Plasmid mini kit and linearize it (up to 50~ug of plasmid per reaction) with BamHI enzyme in a 1× CutSmart buffer for 2h at 37 ^°C . Note: The usual yield is 10 μg of plasmid per one reaction. One linearization reaction is enough to proceed with the experiments.4. Extract the linearized plasmid DNA from 1% agarose gel using a NucleoSpin Gel and PCR Clean-up mini kit.5.  Extract the DNA with phenol/chloroform: •  Add an equal volume of phenol, vortex well, and spin down at 17,000×g for 1 min. Keep the upper phase. • Add an equal volume of chloroform, vortex well, and spin down at 17,000×g for 1 min. Keep the upper phase.6.  Precipitate the DNA with 2.5 volumes of 100% cold ethanol and 1/16 volume of 3M sodium acetate.7.  Wash the pellet with 500~uL of 80% cold ethanol.8.  In vitro transcribe the mRNA for ^3~h~ at 37 ^°C using a MEGAscript kit.9.  Stop the reaction with 2~U/uL DNase turbo and incubate for 15min at 37 ^°C .10. Extract the mRNA with phenol/chloroform and precipitate it with 100% cold ethanol (similar to steps 5 and 6).11. Wash the pellet with 500~uL of 80% cold ethanol.12. Add 50% formamide to the sample and heat for 3min at 90 ^°C .13. Separate the mRNA by 6% polyacrylamide midi-size gel and elute with the buffer ELU for 16h at 4^°C .14. Extract the mRNA with phenol/chloroform and precipitate it with 100% cold ethanol (similar to steps 5 and 6).15. Wash the pellet with 200~uL of 80% cold ethanol.16. Resuspend in 10-30 uLmQ water.17. Store the aliquots at -20~^°C .# E. 30S and mRNA quality analysisThe absence of high-molecular-weight non-ribosomal proteins and the presence of all ribosomal proteins are detected on a 15% polyacrylamide gel (Figure 1B-V). The positions of all 70S ribosomal proteins on the SDSPAGE are identified in Khusainov et al. (2016) (Figure 1A-II). The ribosomal RNA integrity is checked by 0.8% agarose gel in denaturing conditions (Figure 1B-VI).1.  Protein polyacrylamide gel a.  Mix all buffer components for the 15% resolving gel and leave to polymerase for 15-20 min . b. Repeat the same procedure for 4% stacking gel. c. Add 1× blue buffer to the 30S samples (0.15 OD per lane) and heat for 3min at 95~^°C . d. Load 5~uL of protein ladder and 5~uL of 30S samples in the blue buffer. e. Run the gel in a 1× running buffer at 120V for 30min and then at 150V for 1h . f. Stain the gel in InstantBlue® coomassie for 20min and destain in water for 1h .2 .  Extracting the ribosomal RNA a.  Add an equal volume of phenol to the 0.15 OD 30S, mix well by vortexing, and spin down at 17,000 ×g for 1 min. b. Add an equal volume of chloroform to the upper phase, mix well by vortexing, and spin down at 17,000×g for 1 min . c.  Repeat the two last steps. d.  Precipitate the ribosomal RNA with 1/10 volume 3M sodium acetate pH5.3 and 2.5 volumes of 100% cold ethanol.e. Keep the sample overnight at -20~^°C or for 1~h~ at -80~^°C and spin down at 17,000×g for 15min at 4^°C .f. Wash the pellet with 70% cold ethanol and dry it for 5min in a speed vac.g.  Resuspend the obtained sample in 10~uL of mQ water.RNA agarose gela.  Melt 0.8% agarose in 1×TBE buffer in the microwave and then cool down to 60 ^°C .b.  Add 100mM potassium thiocyanate and ethidium bromide to the mixture.c.  Leave the gel for 40min to polymerase.d.  Load 0.015 OD of the sample on the gel together with a high-range RNA ladder.e.  Run the gel in a 1× TBE buffer at 120V for 45min .RNA polyacrylamide gelThe mRNA purity and integrity are checked by 6% polyacrylamide gel stained in ethidium bromide (Figure 2C). The sequence of spa mRNA is shown in Figure 2D.a.  Mix all components for 6% polyacrylamide gel and leave to polymerize for 15-20 min .b.  Load 4~uL of low-range RNA ladder in 1× RNA loading dye close to the 100~ng of mRNA in 50% formamide preheated at 90 ^°C for 2min .c.  Run the gel at 200V for 30min .d.  Stain the gel in ethidium bromide for 10min .# F.  Toeprinting assay1.  Mix 1~uM Toe primer in PNK buffer, 10 U/uL T4 PNK, and 5~uL of gamma-^32P -ATP, incubate at 37~^°C for 1 h, and then pass through the micro bio-spin column pre-equilibrated in mQ water.2.  Incubate 0.5uM of spa mRNA with 169,916cpm/uL Toe primer in a toeprinting buffer at 90^°C for 1 min , put on ice for 1min , and then add MgCl_2 to a 10mM final concentration. Leave for refolding/annealing at 20~^°C for 15min . The final volume of the reaction mixture is 20~uL .3.  Mix 33,983~cpm/uL of mRNA-Toe primer with 0.025~uM_; 0.05~uM , and 0.2 uM 30S small ribosomal subunits. Incubate at 37 ^°C for 10 min and with 0.1 uM itRNA for an additional 5~min (final MgCl_2 concentration should be 8mM ). The final volume of the reaction mixture is 13~uL .4.  Incubate obtained samples with 0.3U/uL of AMV reverse transcriptase and 0.3mM dNTPs in AMV buffer for 30min at 37~^°C , extract with phenol/chloroform, and resuspend in urea blue buffer.5.  For sequencing lanes, incubate 0.33uM mRNA with 135,932cpm/uL Toe primer, 1.7uM nucleotides, and 0.27U/uL AMV reverse transcriptase in AMV buffer at 37~^°C for 30min .6. Add 237~mM KOH, 26~mM tris-HCl pH 8 , 0.26~% SDS, and 3.9mM EDTA to the samples for the sequencing lanes and incubate at 90~^°C for 3 min and at 37 ^°C for 1~h~ . Precipitate RNA in 0.3~M~ sodium acetate and 100% cold ethanol and resuspend in urea blue buffer.7.  Migrate both samples on a 10%. , 40cm , 0.5mm denaturing polyacrylamide gel.8.  Obtain the data by autoradiography (Figure 2A and 2B).()5'ACAAAUACAUACAGGGGGUAUUAAUUUGAAAAAGAAAAACAUUUAUUCAAUUCGUAAACUAGGUGUA GGUAUUGCAUCUGUAACUUUAGGUACAUUACUUAUAUCUGGUGGCGUAACACCUGCUGCAAAUGCUGC GCAACACGAUGAAGCUCAACAAAAUGCUUUUUAUCAAGUCUUAAAUAUGCCUAACUUAAACGCUGGGAU CCUCUAGAGUCGACCUGCAGG s^# Figure 2. Toeprinting assay of the 30S–mRNA–itRNA complex.(A) Scheme of the toeprinting assay. (B) Toeprint analysis of the 30S–mRNA–itRNA complex formation with increasing 30S concentration. (C) The intactness of mRNA was checked by polyacrylamide gel in denaturing conditions. (D) Sequence of spa mRNA with the start codon in bold and Shine–Dalgarno sequence underlined; the +16 nucleotide is shown by the black arrow.# F. 30S–mRNA complex preparation for cryo-EM1.  Combine 450nM of spa mRNA with 90nM of 30S in a storage buffer, apply immediately on the Quantifoil R2/2300-mesh holey carbon cryo-EM grids, and plunge-freeze in liquid ethane using a Vitrobot Mark IV device ( 4^°C and 100% humidity) under the following conditions: 4uL of complex on the grid, 3 s blotting time, blot force of 5, and waiting time of 30s .2.  Clip the grids into the cartridges if you intend to use an Autoloader equipped microscope.3.  Keep in liquid nitrogen until data acquisition.# G. Data acquisition and analysis1.  Collect the data on a 200kV Talos Arctica instrument with a Falcon III direct electron detector under the focus range of -0.5 to -2.7 um with a 0.2 step. Note: For our purposes, the I20,000× magnification was used, yielding a pixel size of oversetI.24mathringA .2.  Transfer the obtained data to the workstation or cluster for data processing.3.  Use the RELION 3.0 software for data processing (Zivanov et al., 2018).4.  Perform drift and gain correction and dose weighting with MotionCor2 (Zheng et al., 2017).5. Use a dose-weighted average image of the whole stack to determine the contrast transfer function with the software Gctf (Zhang et al., 2016).6.  Pick the particles using a Laplacian of Gaussian function (min diameter 180textupAA , max diameter 290textupAA ).7.  Extract the particles with a box size of 270 pixels and divide into several classes for 3D classification.8.  Merge classes (if necessary), extract the original size, and continue with 3D refinement. The obtained 30S–mRNA structure has been refined to an overall 3.6AA resolution with individual focused refinement of the head and the body leading to the resolution of 3.6 and 3.4AA , respectively (Figure 3B and3C).9.  Estimate the local resolution in RELION 3.0 (Zivanov et al., 2018).10. Analyze structures in Chimera (Pettersen et al., 2004) or ChimeraX (Goddard et al., 2018) (Figure 3A).()# Figure 3. Scheme of the cryo-EM data processing.Electron movies were obtained from sample collection on the Talos microscope. After motion correction and contrast transfer function (CTF) estimation, the particles were picked and extracted with a minimum diameter of 180textupAA and maximum diameter of 290textupAA . The next steps were 2D and 3D classification, followed by 3D refinement and post-processing. The final 30S–mRNA structure was obtained at 3.6textupAA with focus refinement on the head and body at 3.4 and 3.6textupAA , respectively.# Recipes# 70S ribosome purification1. Buffer A 100mMNH_4Cl 21 mM Mg acetate 20 mM HEPES-KOH, pH 7.5 1 mM dithiothreitol (DTT)2. Buffer B 1.05 M sucrose 0.5 M KCl 10.5 mM Mg acetate 0.5 mM EDTA 20 mM HEPES-KOH, pH 7.5 1 mM DTT3. 5× buffer E 500 mM KCl 50 mM Mg acetate 2.5 mM EDTACite as: Belinite, M. et al. (2022). protocol_hierarchical.1.1.1.1: Streak Staphylococcus aureus' RN6390 cells on a blood agar plate and grow at 37°C for 20h.
protocol_hierarchical.1.1.1.2: Inoculate a single colony in 50mL of sterile BHI medium in a 250mL flask and grow for 17h at 37°C and 180rpm.
protocol_hierarchical.1.1.1.3: Add 5mL of overnight culture to four 2L flasks, each filled with 500mL of sterile BHI medium, and grow for 3h at 37°C and 180rpm until OD_600=1.
protocol_hierarchical.1.1.2.1: Harvest cells by centrifugation at 4,400×g for 15min at 4°C.
protocol_hierarchical.1.1.2.2: Resuspend the pellet in 50mL of buffer A.
protocol_hierarchical.1.1.2.3: Collect the pellet by centrifugation at 4,400×g for 15min.
protocol_hierarchical.1.1.2.4: Repeat the previous steps (5–6) two times.
protocol_hierarchical.1.1.3.2: Thaw the cells (2.8g) on ice for 15-20 min and resuspend in 6.25mL of buffer A supplemented with 0.7 mg lysostaphin, 1× protease inhibitor cocktail, 1mM EDTA pH7.5, and 6ul DNase I per 1 gram of cell weight.
protocol_hierarchical.1.1.4.1: Spin down the lysate at 21,500×g (JA-25.50 rotor) for 90min at 4°C.
protocol_hierarchical.1.1.4.2: Adjust the PEG20K concentration in the collected supernatant to 2.8% and leave the reaction on ice under agitation for 5min.
protocol_hierarchical.1.1.5.1: Collect the supernatant by centrifugation at 20,000×g for 5min (JA-25.50 rotor).
protocol_hierarchical.1.1.5.2: Increase the PEG20K concentration to 4.2% and leave the reaction on ice under agitation for an additional 10min.
protocol_hierarchical.1.1.5.3: Collect the supernatant by centrifugation at 20,000×g for 10min (JA-25.50 rotor).
protocol_hierarchical.1.1.6.1: Resuspend each pellet in 17.5mL buffer A with 1mM EDTA, lay on a 25mL sucrose cushion (buffer B), and spin at 235,000×g for 17h at 4°C (Type 45Ti rotor).
protocol_hierarchical.1.1.6.2: Rinse the walls of each tube with 5mL of buffer E to remove excess sucrose and resuspend the pellet in 1 mL of buffer E.
protocol_hierarchical.1.1.7.1: Lay the obtained sample on twelve 7%-30% sucrose gradients and spin at 83,000×g for 16.5h (SW28 rotor).
protocol_hierarchical.1.1.7.2: Fractionate sucrose gradients, pool fraction of 70S, and adjust the magnesium acetate to 25mM and PEG20K to 4.5%.
protocol_hierarchical.1.1.8.1: Pellet down the ribosomes by centrifugation at 20,000×g for 13min (JA-25.50 rotor).
protocol_hierarchical.1.1.8.2: Wash the tube walls with the dissociation buffer to remove the excess of sucrose and PEG.
protocol_hierarchical.1.1.8.3: Resuspend the pellet in 1.5mL of dissociation buffer.
protocol_hierarchical.1.1.9.1: Check the purity of 70S by 15% polyacrylamide gel.
protocol_hierarchical.1.1.9.2: Check the purity of 70S by mass-spectrometry analysis.
protocol_hierarchical.1.2.1.1: Load 14mg of 70S in the dissociation buffer directly on 0%-30% sucrose gradients equilibrated in the same buffer and spin at 109,000×g for 14.5h (SW28 rotor).
protocol_hierarchical.1.2.2.2: Adjust the magnesium acetate of pooled fractions to 10mM and spermidine to 2.5mM.
protocol_hierarchical.1.2.3.1: Concentrate the 30S sample to 2-3mg/mL in the Amicon ultra centrifugal filter.
protocol_hierarchical.1.2.4.1: Make aliquots, flash freeze them in liquid nitrogen, and store at -80°C.
protocol_hierarchical.1.3.1.1: Transform XL1 Escherichia coli cells with the pUT7-spa plasmid.
protocol_hierarchical.1.3.2.1: Grow cells in 250mL of LB medium with 100μg/mL ampicillin at 37°C for 16h.
protocol_hierarchical.1.3.2.2: Purify the plasmid using a NucleoSpin Plasmid mini kit and linearize it with BamHI enzyme.
protocol_hierarchical.1.3.3.1: Extract the linearized plasmid DNA from 1% agarose gel using a NucleoSpin Gel and PCR Clean-up mini kit.
protocol_hierarchical.1.3.4.1: Precipitate the DNA with 2.5 volumes of 100% cold ethanol and 1/16 volume of 3M sodium acetate.
protocol_hierarchical.1.3.4.2: Wash the pellet with 500μL of 80% cold ethanol.
protocol_hierarchical.1.3.5.1: In vitro transcribe the mRNA for 3h at 37°C using a MEGAscript kit.
protocol_hierarchical.1.3.6.1: Extract the mRNA with phenol/chloroform and precipitate it with 100% cold ethanol.
protocol_hierarchical.1.3.6.2: Wash the pellet with 500μL of 80% cold ethanol.
protocol_hierarchical.1.3.7.1: Add 50% formamide to the sample and heat for 3min at 90°C.
protocol_hierarchical.1.3.7.2: Separate the mRNA by 6% polyacrylamide midi-size gel and elute with the buffer ELU for 16h at 4°C.
protocol_hierarchical.1.3.8.1: Extract the mRNA with phenol/chloroform and precipitate it with 100% cold ethanol.
protocol_hierarchical.1.3.8.2: Wash the pellet with 200μL of 80% cold ethanol.
protocol_hierarchical.1.4.1.1: Mix all buffer components for the 15% resolving gel and leave to polymerase for 15-20min.
protocol_hierarchical.1.4.1.2: Load 5μL of protein ladder and 5μL of 30S samples in the blue buffer.
protocol_hierarchical.1.4.1.3: Run the gel in a 1× running buffer at 120V for 30min and then at 150V for 1h.
protocol_hierarchical.1.4.2.1: Add an equal volume of phenol to the 0.15 OD 30S, mix well by vortexing, and spin down at 17,000×g for 1min.
protocol_hierarchical.1.4.2.2: Precipitate the ribosomal RNA with 1/10 volume 3M sodium acetate pH5.3 and 2.5 volumes of 100% cold ethanol.
protocol_hierarchical.1.4.3.1: Melt 0.8% agarose in 1×TBE buffer in the microwave and then cool down to 60°C.
protocol_hierarchical.1.4.3.2: Load 0.015 OD of the sample on the gel together with a high-range RNA ladder.
protocol_hierarchical.1.4.4.1: Mix all components for 6% polyacrylamide gel and leave to polymerize for 15-20min.
protocol_hierarchical.1.4.4.2: Load 4μL of low-range RNA ladder in 1× RNA loading dye close to the 100ng of mRNA in 50% formamide preheated at 90°C for 2min.
protocol_hierarchical.1.5.1.1: Mix 1μM Toe primer in PNK buffer, 10U/μL T4 PNK, and 5μL of gamma-32P-ATP, incubate at 37°C for 1h.
protocol_hierarchical.1.5.2.1: Incubate 0.5μM of spa mRNA with 169,916cpm/μL Toe primer in a toeprinting buffer at 90°C for 1min.
protocol_hierarchical.1.5.2.2: Put on ice for 1min, and then add MgCl2 to a 10mM final concentration.
protocol_hierarchical.1.5.3.1: Mix 33,983cpm/μL of mRNA-Toe primer with 0.025μM; 0.05μM, and 0.2μM 30S small ribosomal subunits.
protocol_hierarchical.1.5.3.2: Incubate at 37°C for 10min and with 0.1μM itRNA for an additional 5min.
protocol_hierarchical.1.5.4.1: Incubate obtained samples with 0.3U/μL of AMV reverse transcriptase and 0.3mM dNTPs in AMV buffer for 30min at 37°C.
protocol_hierarchical.1.5.5.1: Incubate 0.33μM mRNA with 135,932cpm/μL Toe primer, 1.7μM nucleotides, and 0.27U/μL AMV reverse transcriptase in AMV buffer at 37°C for 30min.
protocol_hierarchical.1.5.6.1: Add 237mM KOH, 26mM tris-HCl pH 8, 0.26% SDS, and 3.9mM EDTA to the samples for the sequencing lanes.
protocol_hierarchical.1.5.6.2: Incubate at 90°C for 3min and at 37°C for 1h.
protocol_hierarchical.1.5.7.1: Migrate both samples on a 10%, 40cm, 0.5mm denaturing polyacrylamide gel.
protocol_hierarchical.1.6.1.1: Combine 450nM of spa mRNA with 90nM of 30S in a storage buffer.
protocol_hierarchical.1.6.2.1: Apply immediately on the Quantifoil R2/2,300-mesh holey carbon cryo-EM grids.
protocol_hierarchical.1.6.3.1: Plunge-freeze in liquid ethane using a Vitrobot Mark IV device.
protocol_hierarchical.1.7.1.1: Collect the data on a 200kV Talos Arctica instrument with a Falcon III direct electron detector.
protocol_hierarchical.1.7.2.1: Transfer the obtained data to the workstation or cluster for data processing.
protocol_hierarchical.1.7.2.2: Use the RELION 3.0 software for data processing.
protocol_hierarchical.1.7.3.1: Perform drift and gain correction and dose weighting with MotionCor2.
protocol_hierarchical.1.7.4.1: Use a dose-weighted average image of the whole stack to determine the contrast transfer function with the software Gctf.
protocol_hierarchical.1.7.5.1: Pick the particles using a Laplacian of Gaussian function.
protocol_hierarchical.1.7.6.1: Extract the particles with a box size of 270 pixels and divide into several classes for 3D classification.
protocol_hierarchical.1.7.7.1: Merge classes, extract the original size, and continue with 3D refinement.
protocol_hierarchical.1.7.8.1: Estimate the local resolution in RELION 3.0.
protocol_hierarchical.1.7.9.1: Analyze structures in Chimera or ChimeraX.

problem: The protocol addresses the challenge of purifying and analyzing the intact and functional 30S ribosomal subunit of *Staphylococcus aureus* under specific ionic conditions, including the role of polyamines, to enable high-resolution cryo-EM and biochemical studies.

query: I plan to use the Neuroscience Methods method to investigate how to effectively apply the 'Common Carotid Arteries Occlusion Surgery in Adult Rats as a' protocol for Neuroscience Methods research. Please provide a complete protocol.

ground_truth_answer.title: Common Carotid Arteries Occlusion Surgery in Adult Rats as a
ground_truth_answer.abstract: Chronic cerebral hypoperfusion (CCH) is an important risk factor of vascular dementia (VaD) and Alzheimer’s disease (AD). Hypoxia/ischemia in the whole brain induced by CCH causes serious damage to brain structure and function, which can lead to cognitive impairment. Two-vessel occlusion (2-VO), also known as permanent, bilateral common carotid artery occlusion, is one of the most widely used animal models (e.g., rat) of CCH to investigate the mechanisms of neurodegenerative processes. In this protocol, we present the surgical procedure for 2-VO in rats.
ground_truth_answer.protocol_text: Ethical statement: Adult male Sprague-Dawley (SD)/Wistar rats (10-12 weeks) were used in this protocol. All procedures involving animals follow the local animal ethics protocols and standards.1.  Fast the rats for 12 h before the experiment.2.  Anesthetize rats with chloral hydrate (300m g/k g) by intraperitoneal injection using a 2 ml syringe with a needle. Note: Slightly press the paw of the rat, it indicates the rat is in deep anesthesia if there are no reflex actions of hind limb.3.  Fix the rat on its back on a dissection board. Shave the fur of the ventral neck using curved ophthalmic scissors. Sterilize the shaved skin first with iodine tincture and then with 75% ethanol using cotton swabs (repeat three times) (Figure 3). Note: In order to minimize the risk of infections, thoroughly sterilize the dissection board with 75% alcohol before the surgery.()# Figure 3. Rat fixed on a dissection board before the 2-VO surgery4.  Use operating scissors to make a midventral cervical incision in the middle of the neck in the upper edge of the sternum (about 1-1.5 cm long). Gently remove the submandibular gland using the ophthalmic forceps. This will expose the sternocleidomastoid muscle and sternohyoid muscle (Figure 4).()Figure 4. Image of the exposed sternocleidomastoid muscle (indicated by the thin arrow) and sternohyoid muscle (indicated by the thick arrow). Cut the skin, remove the tissues, and expose the sternocleidomastoid muscle and sternohyoid muscle.5.  Carefully separate the common carotid arteries from the adhering tissues using ophthalmic forceps. Be careful and avoid causing any damage to the vagus nerve (Figures 5 and 6, Video 1).Note: Gently pull apart the sternocleidomastoid muscle deep in the sternohyoid muscle (until the sternohyoid muscle appears), there will be the common carotid arteria wrapped by fibrous connective tissue, which contains the vagus nerve in its sheath and pulses regularly.()Figure 5. Isolation of the common carotid artery()Figure 6. Schematic diagram of the Bilateral carotid artery. The arrows indicate the common carotid artery. This figure is adapted from Eszter Farkas et al. (2001).()Video 1. Isolation of the common carotid artery6.  The bilateral common carotid arteries are doubly ligated with 4-0 silk suture, the two sutures are next to each other, then the surgical wounds are sutured back with silk suture. Return the rat back to its cage until it fully recovers from anesthesia (Figure 7, Video 2). Notes:a.  The silk suture won’t be absorbed over time. They remain in place until it is executed.b.  After surgery, feed the rat with an appropriate amount of penicillin to prevent infection. The rat is kept warm on the heating pad (37.0pm0.5 ^°C) throughout the surgery until the rat is completely awake.()Figure 7. The common carotid artery ligation and wound closure. The blue arrow indicates the common carotid artery; the black arrow indicates the vagus nerve.()Video 2. Ligation of the common carotid artery

protocol_hierarchical.1.1: Adult male Sprague-Dawley (SD)/Wistar rats (10-12 weeks) were used in this protocol.
protocol_hierarchical.1.2: All procedures involving animals follow the local animal ethics protocols and standards.
protocol_hierarchical.1.3: Fast the rats for 12 h before the experiment.
protocol_hierarchical.2.1: Anesthetize rats with chloral hydrate (300mg/kg) by intraperitoneal injection using a 2 ml syringe with a needle.
protocol_hierarchical.2.2: Check the depth of anesthesia by pressing the paw of the rat; if there are no reflex actions of the hind limb, the rat is in deep anesthesia.
protocol_hierarchical.3.1: Fix the rat on its back on a dissection board.
protocol_hierarchical.3.2: Shave the fur of the ventral neck using curved ophthalmic scissors.
protocol_hierarchical.3.3.1: Sterilize the shaved skin first with iodine tincture.
protocol_hierarchical.3.3.2: Then sterilize with 75% ethanol using cotton swabs (repeat three times).
protocol_hierarchical.3.4: Thoroughly sterilize the dissection board with 75% alcohol before the surgery to minimize the risk of infections.

4.title: Surgical Incision and Exposure of Muscles

protocol_hierarchical.4.1: Make a midventral cervical incision in the middle of the neck in the upper edge of the sternum (about 1-1.5 cm long) using operating scissors.
protocol_hierarchical.4.2: Gently remove the submandibular gland using ophthalmic forceps to expose the sternocleidomastoid muscle and sternohyoid muscle.
protocol_hierarchical.5.1: Carefully separate the common carotid arteries from the adhering tissues using ophthalmic forceps.
protocol_hierarchical.5.2: Avoid causing any damage to the vagus nerve.
protocol_hierarchical.5.3.1: Gently pull apart the sternocleidomastoid muscle deep in the sternohyoid muscle until the sternohyoid muscle appears.
protocol_hierarchical.5.3.2: The common carotid arteria wrapped by fibrous connective tissue, containing the vagus nerve in its sheath and pulsing regularly, will be found.

6.title: Ligation of Common Carotid Arteries and Wound Closure

protocol_hierarchical.6.1: Doubly ligate the bilateral common carotid arteries with 4-0 silk suture, with the two sutures next to each other.
protocol_hierarchical.6.2: Suture the surgical wounds back with silk suture.
protocol_hierarchical.6.3: Return the rat to its cage until it fully recovers from anesthesia.
protocol_hierarchical.6.4.1: Feed the rat with an appropriate amount of penicillin to prevent infection.
protocol_hierarchical.6.4.2: Keep the rat warm on a heating pad (37.0±0.5°C) throughout the surgery until the rat is completely awake.

problem: Need for a standardized surgical protocol to induce chronic cerebral hypoperfusion in adult rats for the study of neurodegenerative diseases.

query: Please generate detailed steps for the experiment 'Video Article Isolation and Quantitative Evaluation of Brush Cells from Mouse Tracheas', focusing on the Cell Biology & Culture technique.

ground_truth_answer.title: Video Article Isolation and Quantitative Evaluation of Brush Cells from Mouse Tracheas
ground_truth_answer.abstract: Tracheal brush cells are cholinergic chemosensory epithelial cells poised to transmit signals from the airway lumen to the immune and nervous systems. They are part of a family of chemosensory epithelial cells which include tuft cells in the intestinal mucosa, brush cells in the trachea, and solitary chemosensory and microvillous cells in the nasal mucosa. Chemosensory cells in different epithelial compartments share key intracellular markers and a core transcriptional signature, but also display significant transcriptional heterogeneity, likely reflective of the local tissue environment. Isolation of tracheal brush cells from single cell suspensions is required to define the function of these rare epithelial cells in detail, but their isolation is challenging, potentially due to the close interaction between tracheal brush cells and nerve endings or due to airwayspecific composition of tight and adherens junctions. Here, we describe a procedure for isolation of brush cells from mouse tracheal epithelium. The method is based on an initial separation of tracheal epithelium from the submucosa, allowing for a subsequent shorter incubation of the epithelial sheet with papain. This procedure offers a rapid and convenient solution for flow cytometric sorting and functional analysis of viable tracheal brush cells.
ground_truth_answer.protocol_text:
# 1. Preparation of Reagents
1. Prepare Dispase digestion solution, which is a PBS solution containing 16cup/ m L dispase and 20 u/m L DNase I. Ensure that the dispase powder is fully dissolved before warming up the solution in a water bath at 37°C .
2. Add 5% heat-inactivated fetal bovine serum (FBS) to Dulbecco Modified Eagle Medium (DMEM) to make a stopping solution.
3. Prepare Tyrode I buffer: add 26cup/m L papain (20uL/ mL of 48cup/m g papain solution) and 10 µL/mL L-cysteine to HEPES-Tyrode's buffer without calcium.
4. Prepare Tyrode II buffer: add 2uL/ m L leupeptin (5mg/mL) to HEPES-Tyrode's buffer with calcium.
5. Prepare FACS buffer: use Hanks' Balanced Salt Solution (HBSS) without calcium, magnesium & phenol red, supplemented with 2 mM ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA) and add 2% FBS.
# 2. Dissection of Mouse Trachea
1. Euthanize the mouse with 100m g/k g pentobarbital euthanasia solution injected intraperitoneally or using standard protocols approved by the IACUC.
2. Fix the mouse on a surgical board in the supine position with 21 G needles with extended upper and lower extremities. Spray the fur with 70% EtOH to sanitize the area.
3. Using straight forceps, lift the skin and fur of the abdomen and make an incision in the center with dissecting scissors (straight scissors, 3 cm). Using the scissors, separate the skin from the subcutaneous tissue from the abdomen to the mandibula. While holding the subcutaneous tissue up with the forceps, make a small incision with the scissors in the center of the abdominal wall.
4. Open the peritoneum with a V-shaped incision. Using the forceps, gently move the small intestine to the side, locate the abdominal aorta and vena cava and make an incision with the dissecting scissors to allow for rapid exsanguination.
5. Locate the diaphragm. Using an 8 needle, make an opening in the diaphragm just below the sternum to deflate the lungs. Carefully separate the diaphragm from the rib cage using sharp-pointed straight dissecting scissors to cut along the base of the ribs.
6. Using the forceps, lift the exposed end of the sternum and cut the sternum longitudinally from the base of the rib cage to the neck. Make a central cervical incision with short straight scissors (2 cm) and separate the two lobes of the submandibular gland.
7. Carefully remove the surrounding connective tissue and the thymus overlying the carina with a pair of fine point high precision forceps.
8. Dissect the trachea free first by separating the proximal end at the level of the epiglottis and then by dissecting the distal end at the level of the bifurcation of the trachea.
9. Locate the epiglottis and cut the trachea longitudinally from the epiglottis to the carina.
# 3. Tracheal Epithelial Digestion
1. Place the trachea into a 1.5mL tube containing 750uL of pre-warmed (to 37 °C_ ) dispase digestion solution. Incubate on a shaker at 200 rpm for 40 min at room temperature. Cover the tube with aluminum foil to reduce the exposure to direct light.
2. Add 750uL of cold DMEM with 5% FBS to stop the reaction. Place on ice.
3. Transfer the trachea to a Petri dish ( 100mm×15mm) and place under a dissecting microscope. Orient the trachea with the epithelial side facing up. The longitudinally dissected trachea has a semi-cylindrical shape maintained by the cartilaginous rings. The epithelium is on the concave surface.
4. Tether the epiglottis area of the trachea with straight forceps to the Petri dish and using a size 22 disposable scalpel, scrape the epithelium off the trachea. The epithelial layer separates as a translucent sheet.
5. Mince the epithelium with the scalpel. Transfer the epithelial layer to a 2mL tube.
6. Rinse the Petri dish with 750uL of Tyrode I buffer and transfer to the 2mL tube containing the epithelial layer.
7. Incubate the tracheal epithelial layer in Tyrode I buffer for 30m i n at 37°C on a shaker at 200 rpm. Cover the tube with aluminum foil to reduce the exposure to direct light.
8. Add 750uL of cold Tyrode II buffer. Vortex the digested tissue vigorously for 20-30 s. Triturate the homogenate with a syringe attached to an 8 needle 10 times.  Switch to a 21 gauge needle and triturate 10-20 more times.
9. Filter the cells through a 100um strainer into a 50mL conical tube. Add 30:1 vol/vol of cold FACS buffer.
10. Spin at 350× g for 10 min at 4°C and discard the supernatant. Resuspend the pellet in cold FACS buffer and transfer the suspension to a 12 mm 1×75m m (5 mL) polystyrene tube. Spin again at 350× g for 10m i n at 4°C and discard the supernatant.
11. Re-suspend the pellet in 100uL of FACS buffer.
12. Add 1 uL of anti-mouse CD16/32 blocking antibody to block non-specific binding and incubate for 15m i n on ice. Do not wash.
13. Add the following antibodies and the respective isotype controls: pacific blue anti-mouse CD45 or rat IgG2a, k (0.25u/106 cells in 100uL volume) and allophycocyanin (APC) anti-mouse EpCAM or rat IgG2b, k (0.5 µg/10 cells in 100uL volume) monoclonal antibodies. Incubate for 45 min on ice protected from direct light. Add 4.5mL of cold FACS buffer, mix and spin at 350× g for 10 min at 4°C . Discard the FACS buffer and re-suspend the pellet in 300uL of cold FACS buffer.
14. Add propidium iodide (PI) (5u/mL) immediately before flow cytometric sorting. NOTE: Brush cells have an irregular shape with several processes that could potentially increase their adherence to filters (Figure 3C). We compared 30um to 70um and 100um filters and found that larger pore filters ensured better yields. In addition, thorough trituration of the cell suspension after papain digestion significantly improves the brush cell yields. We recommend using an 18G needle for initial trituration followed by a smaller bore (21 or 23G needle) for finer dissociation of cells.
# 4. Flow Cytometry Gating Strategy
1. Identify cells from debris by forward and side scatter angle. Exclude the doublets using forward scatter height and width and side scatter height and width. The doublets would be the cells that have high width values.
2. Within the single cells, identify the live cells as the population that is PI negative.
3. Within the live single cells, identify the CD45 low to negative cells based on the isotype control.
4. Within the CD45 low/negative cells, identify the EpCAM positive cells that are also eGFP positive (in the FITC channel). This is the population of brush cells (Figure 2A).
