query: Please generate detailed steps for the experiment 'Video Article Probing the Roles of Physical Forces in Early Chick Embryonic Morphogenesis', focusing on the Cell Biology & Culture technique.

ground_truth_answer.title: Video Article Probing the Roles of Physical Forces in Early Chick Embryonic Morphogenesis
ground_truth_answer.abstract: Embryonic development is traditionally studied from the perspective of biomolecular genetics, but the fundamental importance of mechanics in morphogenesis is becoming increasingly recognized. In particular, the embryonic chick heart and brain tube, which undergo drastic morphological changes as they develop, are among the prime candidates to study the role of physical forces in morphogenesis. Progressive ventral bending and rightward torsion of the tubular embryonic chick brain happen at the earliest stage of organ-level left-right asymmetry in chick embryonic development. The vitelline membrane (VM) constrains the dorsal side of the embryo and has been implicated in providing the force necessary to induce torsion of the developing brain. Here we present a combination of new ex-ovo experiments and physical modeling to identify the mechanics of brain torsion. At Hamburger-Hamilton stage 11, embryos are harvested and cultured ex ovo (in media). The VM is subsequently removed using a pulled capillary tube. By controlling the level of the fluid and subjecting the embryo to a fluid-air interface, the fluid surface tension of the media can be used to replace the mechanical role of the VM. Microsurgery experiments were also performed to alter the position of the heart to find the resultant change in the chirality of brain torsion. Results from this protocol illustrate the fundamental roles of mechanics in driving morphogenesis.
ground_truth_answer.protocol_text:
# 1. Preparation of Tissue Culture Media
1. Use a 0.5L bottle of Dulbecco's Modified Eagle's Medium (DMEM) with 4.5/L glucose, sodium bicarbonate, and L-glutamine as the base for the culture media.
2. In a sterile laminar flow hood, add 10mL of antibiotics to the 0.5L of DMEM.
3. Using a sterile pipette, transfer 50mL of the DMEM antibiotics solution to a sterile 50mL conical tube.
4. Add 50mL of chick serum to the remaining DMEM antibiotics solution in the 0.5L bottle in the sterile hood.
5. Store the final solution (referred to hereafter as chick culture media CCM) in 50mL conical tube aliquots at -20°C .
# 2. Egg Incubation
1. Use delicate wipes with 70% ethanol to clean fertilized specific pathogen-free White Leghorn chicken eggs. Arrange eggs in a longitudinal orientation on holders.
2. Turn on an egg incubator to set a target temperature of 37.5°C and maintain the humidity at 48-55% . The humidity is controlled by adding an appropriate amount of water to the incubator.
3. Incubate the eggs to HH11-13, approximately 40-44h .
4. Let the eggs cool down at room temperature for approximately 15 - 30 min prior to spraying/cleaning with 70% ethanol.
# 3. Pull Glass Capillaries
1. Mount a 10 cm-long glass capillary tubes with an outer diameter of 1.0m m and an inner diameter of 0.5m m on a micropipette puller.
2. Set the heat and pull parameters to 750 and 400, respectively. Press the pull button to pull the capillary tube into thin needles.
# 4. Filter Paper Carrier Method
1. Cut circles of approximately 3 cm in diameter from filter paper.
2. Cut a rectangle roughly 1 cm by 2 cm from the circle using a hole puncher. Make sure to remove any protruding or sharp corners.
# 5. Embryo Harvesting and Preparation
1. Crack eggs from the bottom, pull apart the shell gently and carefully deposit contents into a 150mm×15mm petri dish. To ensure the embryo side is up, keep the eggs in the same orientation they were incubated while cracking them.
2. Remove the thin albumin using a disposable Pasteur pipette.
3. Separate the thick albumin from the yolk using blunt ended forceps. Ensure that the thick albumin has been removed by lightly scraping the top of the yolk with the blunt ended forceps.
4. Use fine-tipped forceps to center and place a filter paper ring over the embryo, matching the long axis of the ring with the long axis of the embryo.
5. Cut the yolk surrounding the filter paper ring with scissors.
6. Pull the ring and embryo off the yolk in an oblique direction towards the site where the yolk was first cut.
7. Rinse the embryos in two sequential 100-mm dishes with room temperature 1x phosphate buffered saline (PBS).
8. Place a filter paper ring into a 35-mm petri dish first. Then place the embryo dorsal side up onto the filter paper already in 35-mm petri dish.
9. Place a stainless-steel ring on top of the filter paper sandwich. Make sure not to damage the embryo.
10. Add 3 mL of the previously prepared CCM to each petri dish.
11. Remove the VM of each embryo by lightly skimming the pulled capillary needle across the top of the embryo and peeling the VM away, starting from the anterior end (right above the forebrain) and proceeding to the notochord (Figure 1A).
12. Place eight 35-mm Petri dishes into one 150m m dish that was lined with water saturated delicate task wipes (to maintain humidity).
13. Place the 150-mm petri dish into a sealable plastic bag then fill the bag with a gas mixture comprised of 95% 0_2 and 5% C O_2 .
14. Seal the bag and place it in a 37.5°C incubator.
15. Incubate the embryos for an additional 27h until HH15-HH16 (Figure 1B).
# 6. Inducing Surface Tension
1. Remove the embryos from the incubator and use an optical coherence tomography (OCT) system to image them. Use OCT to determine the torsional angle of the neural tube (NT) (Figure 2).
2. Transfer the embryos to a light microscope and visualize at 10X magnification. Use a 200 microliter pipette to incrementally remove 0.2m L of media from the petri dish.
3. Take brightfield images at each interval to observe the effects of the media-air interface on the embryo.
4. Remove media until the surface tension across the embryo induces torsion (Figure 1C).
5. Image the embryo using the OCT system once again to establish a final torsional angle for comparison to control embryos.  NOTE: The Bright-field images were acquired by using a dissecting microscope. An optical coherence tomography system with an attached microscope was used to acquire cross-sectional image stacks of live embryos. Images were obtained in a 3×10mm×3mm3 scanning domain, then processed in an imaging software. Lastly, the physical model images were taken with a digital single-lens reflex camera.
# 7. Physical Modeling of Surface Tension/VM Forces
1. Develop a simplified 3D geometry that resembles an embryo between HH14-17 in commercial modeling software (Figure 3A).
2. Design the negative mold of the 3D geometry in commercial 3D computer graphics software.
3. Use a 3D printer loaded with 1.75:mm acrylonitrile butadiene styrene filament to 3D print the designed mold, in stereolithographic (.stl) format.
4. To cast the mold, mix silicone rubber elastomer components A and B in equal parts and pour the mix into the mold promptly; set the casting mold to cure at room temperature for 12h (Figure 3B).
5. Mark the physical model of the embryo along the NT on the dorsal side to visualize torsion.
6. Use a coverslip to replicate the force applied to the 3D physical model which mimics that of the VM or surface tension (Figure 3D).
7. Insert a series of rigid wires of equal length to the side of the physical model. After the coverslip exerts an external force on the brain model, the wires become tilted at an angle that depends on the location. Determine the rotational angle by arctan of the projected length over the original length of each wire (Figure 3E).
# 8. Altering the Direction of the Heart Loop
1. Follow the steps in 3.1 and 3.2 to obtain a pulled capillary tube.
2. Follow the steps in 5.1 through 5.10 to prepare the embryo.
3. Use a pair of forceps to flip the filter paper so that the embryo becomes ventral-side-up.
4. Use the pulled capillary tube to cut open a slit in the splanchnopleure (SPL) membrane.
5. Use the capillary tube to exert a mechanical force to push the heart from the right-hand side to the left-hand side.
6. Follow the steps in 5.12 through 5.15 to observe the change in torsion.

protocol_hierarchical.7.6: Use a coverslip to replicate the force applied to the 3D physical model which mimics that of the VM or surface tension (Figure 3D).
protocol_hierarchical.7.7: Insert a series of rigid wires of equal length to the side of the physical model. After the coverslip exerts an external force on the brain model, the wires become tilted at an angle that depends on the location. Determine the rotational angle by arctan of the projected length over the original length of each wire (Figure 3E).

8.title: Altering the Direction of the Heart Loop

protocol_hierarchical.8.1: Follow the steps in 3.1 and 3.2 to obtain a pulled capillary tube.
protocol_hierarchical.8.2: Follow the steps in 5.1 through 5.10 to prepare the embryo.
protocol_hierarchical.8.3: Use a pair of forceps to flip the filter paper so that the embryo becomes ventral-side-up.
protocol_hierarchical.8.4: Use the pulled capillary tube to cut open a slit in the splanchnopleure (SPL) membrane.
protocol_hierarchical.8.5: Use the capillary tube to exert a mechanical force to push the heart from the right-hand side to the left-hand side.
protocol_hierarchical.8.6: Follow the steps in 5.12 through 5.15 to observe the change in torsion.

problem: Understanding the mechanical forces driving left-right asymmetry in early chick embryonic brain development.

query: Please review the attached document containing our protocol for 'Bromodeoxyuridine (BrdU) labeling and immunohistochemical detection in adult zebrafish brain'. I need you to check it for errors and generate a machine-readable version.

ground_truth_answer.title: Bromodeoxyuridine (BrdU) labeling and immunohistochemical detection in adult zebrafish brain
ground_truth_answer.abstract: Zebrafish is an ideal model organism to study adult neurogenesis. New neurons are born in various parts of zebrafish brain all through its lifespan. Analyzing neuron renewal in adult brain provides a valuable insight into neurodegenerative diseases. Bromodeoxyuridine (BrdU) is a thymidine analog and it incorporates into the DNA only if the cells are at S-phase. BrdU labeling is a reliable method to locate the cells that are actively dividing. Here we describe the steps required for BrdU labeling in adult zebrafish brain starting from BrdU injection to the final analysis in the microscope. We utilize a paraformaldehyde fixation, vibratome slicing, fluorescent labeling of incorporated BrdU and finally confocal imaging.For a thorough coverage on the topic zebrafish neurogenesis, readers are referred to 1 from which this protocol is adapted. A protocol on diverse applications of BrdU labeling can be found in protocol series by Jackson and Cook 2 3 4.# Reagentso 7-AAD nucleus stain (A1310, Invitrogen)o Agaroseo Anti-BrdU antibody, mouse monoclonal (#5292S, Cell Signaling Technologies or B8434, Sigma)o Anti-mouse Alexafluor - 488 secondary antibody, raised in goat (A-11029 Invitrogen or #4408 Cell Signaling Technologies)o Bromodeoxyuridine powder, Sigma, B5002o Fixation Buffer, FB001, Invitrogeno Goat serum, Sigmao Hydrochloric acid, 2No Methanolo Prolong Gold Antifade Reagent, P36934, Invitrogeno Phosphate buffered saline (PBS), p H=7.4 o Tween o Borate BufferRECIPESo PBS-T  (0.5%) : to 1 L PBS, add 5mL Tweeno Blocking solution: to 9.7m L PBS-T, add 300 uL natural goat serumo 7-AAD working solution: add 787 μL PBS to 1 mg of 7-AAD to make 1 mM stock solution. Then to 10 uL of stock solution, add 990 μL PBS to make working solution.o 3% Agarose: weigh 3 g agarose into ^700mL PBSo 2N HCl: to 76.8mL water add 3.2mL HClo Borate Buffer: weigh 1.9 g borax into 50mL PBS# Equipmento Confocal microscope (Zeiss LSM 510)o Dissecting microscope (Zeiss Stemi 1000)o Syringe, 2m L , 0.4m m×5m m o Syringe, bent at the tipo 48-well plateso Glass microscope slideo Glass coverslips, 24 ×60mm o LED light o Paint brush, no:0 and no:2o Parafilmo Pasteur pipette, glass, 145m m o Plastic dish, 10 cmo Rocker-Shakero Tweezers, dumont #5, item: 14099o Vibratome (Leica, VT1200S)# ProcedureINJECTION1. Prepare 10m g/m L stock BrdU solution in water. Aliquots can be stored at -20^°C and can be thawed in 37^°C water bath before use. 2. Equilibrate the syringe with sterile water. arrow Care must be taken not to introduce air into the syringe. 3. Put 0.2m g BrdU solution for 1 g of body weight onto a parafilm and slowly take up by the syringe. arrow Amount of BrdU injected was described in 5.4. Transfer the fish from aquarium into an insulator ice bucket filled with aquarium water to half-length.5. Start adding ice cubes that were made from the aquarium water until the temperature drops to 12^° . arrow At thistemperature, the fish ceases its movement but the continues breathing. 6. Cool your fingertips in the 12^° water and gently handle the fish. Inject the BrdU solution into the abdominal cavity. arrow Fish can be immobilized onto a sponge which has been soaked in 12^° water. 7. Release the fish into 25^°C system water and observe for 10 minutes.8. Leave the fish in this tank for 4 to 24 hours. FIXATION9. Prepare icewater mixture: In an ice bucket, add 4 parts of ice and 1 part of water Put the fish into the ice water, fish should be in the 4^°C water completely. arrow As suggested by NIH Guidelines 6. arrow Fish can stay in the icewater for up to 1h.10. After 10 minutes, when all the movement ceases, fish can be killed by cutting the head with a scalpel.11. Put the head into the fixation buffer and incubate at 4^°C, overnight.DISSECTION 12. Fill a 10 cm dish with PBS and add the fish or the fish head into the PBS. Perform the dissection by using the dumont tweezers under the dissecting microscope. arrow The brain has to be dissected as a whole by cleaning all the gills and fat tissue. The pigments on the dorsal side of the brain can be removed by peeling the thin membrane. 13. Add the dissected brain in PBS into a 2ml tube. 14. Wash the samples 3 times with PBS-T for 5 min each by carefully aspirating the medium on top and slowly adding the new medium. Put the tubes on rotator in between washes.15. Perform dehydration series by incubating the brains 4 min in each of the following:a) 30% methanol in PBS-Tb) 50% methanol in PBS-Tc) 70% methanol in PBS-Td) 90% methanol in PBS-Te) 700% methanol16. Add 100% methanol and incubate at -20^°C, overnight. arrow Samples can be left at this stage for long term storage. EMBEDDING AND SLICING 17. Take the brains from -20^°C, equilibrate with room temperature methanol by washing once.18. Perform rehydration series by incubating the brains 4 min in each of the following:a) 90% methanol in PBS-Tb) 70% methanol in PBS-Tc) 50% methanol in PBS-Td) 30% methanol in PBS-Te) PBS-T19. Wash with PBS-T again and leave on the rotator for 15 min.20. Prepare 3% agarose in PBS, allow to cool down to a temperature which the bottle can be hold in hand but still not solidifed.21. Transfer agarose into the moulds.22. Pick each brain with the tweezers and carefully drop into the agarose. 23. With the help of bent syringe adjust the orientation of the brains quickly. Brains tend to sink to the bottom, try to prevent it by again touching the agarose with the syringe. Do not touch the brains.24. When the agarose has set, release the agarose from the mould.25. Cut agarose into a small block in which the brain is at the center. Brain shouldn’t be close the edges. 26. Attach agarose blocks to vibratome plate by using a strong, water-based adhesive. 27. Fill the vibratome tank with PBS, mount the plate into the tank. 28. Start cutting slices of 50uM thickness. 29. Transfer each slice into a 48-well plate filled with PBS after each cutting. Two slices can be put into one well.30. When all slices are obtained, wash twice with PBS-T by aspirating the medium with a glass pasteur pipette, carefully. Do not touch the slices.31. Add 180 μL 2N HCl to each well and incubate at 37^°C for 30 min without shaking.32. Aspirate HCl and wash the wells with borate buffer, once.33. Wash 3 times with PBS-T, 5 min each, on a rotator. 34. Incubate in blocking solution: 3% goat serum in PBS-T for 1 hour, at RT, on the rotator. Add 180μL to each well.35. Prepare the anti-BrdU antibody in blocking solution at a 1:500 dilution. Mix well.36. Add 180μL antibody to each well except for the no antibody control. To no-primary-antibody control wells, add 180μL of blocking solution. 37. Leave plates at 4^°, on a rotator, for 24 to 72 hours. Cover the plates with parafilm to prevent evaporation.MOUNTING38. Take the plates out of fridge and discard the antibodies. 39. Wash 3 times with PBS-T, 5 min each.40. Wash 2 times with PBS-T, 15 min each. Put the tubes on rotator in between washes.41. Incubate in blocking solution: 3% goat serum in PBS-T, for 30 min on a rotator.42. Preparethe secondary antibody in PBS-T at 1:1000 dilution. Mix well.43. Incubate at room temperature, for 2 hours, on the rotator.44. Discard the antibodies. 45. Wash 3 times with PBS-T, 5 min each.46. Wash 2 times with PBS-T, 15 min each. Put the tubes on rotator in between washes.47. Add 180μL of 7-AAD working solution onto the wells. Incubate for 10 min on a rotator. arrow Applied as recommended in 748. Wash with PBS-T, twice.49. Under a bright light with a black background, fill a 15c m dish with PBS. Dip one microscope slide into it, wash with PBS.50. Incline the slide to the edge of the plate, one end dipped in PBS. Take two slices at a time from the experiment plate with the help of a brush and drop into the PBS in the dish.51. With a no:0 brush, align the slices onto the microscope slide. Do not allow slices to wrap or dry. Align up to 8 slices on a slide.52. Wipe dry excess water, let the slices air dry but do not let them dry out completely.53. Drop minute amounts of Prolong antifade reagent to each slice and also to the bottom of the slide.54. Touch the coverslip to the edge of the slide and lower slowly onto the slices.55. Leave the slides overnight at room temperature, in the dark.56. Seal the edges with nail polish. arrow Slides can now be observed under microscope.# TimingInjection: 1 hourFixation: 1 hourDissection: 1 hourEmbedding and Slicing: 4 hoursMounting: 4 hours# TroubleshootingProblem: Agarose surrounding the slices disappears after HCl treatment.Solution: Use a basic agarose instead of a low-melting or reducta.Problem: High background staining or non-specific staining.Solution: Increase number of PBS-T washes or the percentage of tween. Increase blocking time before the secondary antibody.Problem: No nuclear staining.Solution: HCl treatment must be the problem. Prepare a fresh batch.# Anticipated ResultsAdvantage of this technique comes from utilization of the vibratome. Vibratome is a versatile equipment to obtain brain slices eliminating the need of freezing. It can take slices of variable widths (we tried 20um to 200um^ ) without causing a wrap in the tissue. We do not need to freeze the tissue as in cryostat but instead embed into agarose which makes the handling of the slices easier during the washing steps. HCl treatment is harsh for the slices and sometimes damages the agarose around the slices but we found that 2N HCl is compatible with 3% basic agarose. HCl treatment is essential for BrdU staining in order to separate the DNA strands. Also when we tried permeabilization buffers, we observed that the nuclear dye was not taken up into the nucleus. For anti-BrdU monoclonal antibody and for the secondary antibody, we recommend two antibodies from different suppliers. There are certainly many other but these are the ones that we used in our experiments. As a secondary antibody, we used Alexafluor-488 tagged antibodies since it does not overlap with the 7-AAD spectra. In the confocal microscopy we excite with 488n m Argon laser and collect from BP 505-550 emission filter for the BrdU staining. In a second channel, we excite with 543 nm HeNe laser and collect from LP 585 emission filter to visualize 7-AAD.Instead of injecting BrdU, we also tried to add BrdU into the aquarium water. After 24 hours we did not manage to visualize the labeling.# References1 P. Chapouton and L. Godinho, “Neurogenesis,” Methods Cell Biol., vol. 100, pp. 73–126, 2010.2 D. Jackson and P. R. Cook, “Analyzing DNA Replication I: Labeling Animals, Tissues, and Cells with Bromodeoxyuridine (BrdU),” CSH Protoc, vol. 2008, p. pdb.prot5031, 2008.3 D. Jackson and P. R. Cook, “Analyzing DNA Replication II: Fixation and Processing of Tissues and Cells Labeled with Bromodeoxyuridine (BrdU),” CSH Protoc, vol. 2008, p. pdb.prot5032, 2008.4 D. Jackson and P. R. Cook, “Analyzing DNA Replication III: Antibody Labeling of Incorporated Bromodeoxyuridine (BrdU) in Tissues and Cells,” CSH Protoc, vol. 2008, p. pdb.prot5033, 2008.5 K. Ampatzis, P. Makantasi, and C. R. Dermon, “Cell proliferation pattern in adult zebrafish forebrain is sexually dimorphic,” Neuroscience, vol. 226, pp. 367–381, Dec. 2012.6 National Institutes of Health, “Guidelines for use of zebrafish in the NIH intramural research program,” Animal Research and Care Guidelines, Office of Animal Care and Use, 2009. 7 A. Can, O. Semiz, and O. Cinar, “Two convenient methods for nuclear labeling in confocal microscopy using visible-lasers,” The Journal of Histotechnology, vol. 26, no. 3, pp. 147–151, 2003.# AcknowledgementsThis work was supported by a 1002 project (112S649) from The Scientific And Technological Research Council Of Turkey (TUBITAK). We would like to thank Laure Bally-Cuif and Jacob von Trotha for their guidance in immunohistochemical staining procedure and Zeynep Ergul Ulger for her help in confocal microscopy.# Figures()Figure 1Visualization of the BrdU labeled cells in zebrafish brain cross sections. In a 30 month old male zebrafish, 4-hour BrdU exposure was detected with BrdU monoclonal antibody and Alexafluor-488 linked secondary antibody. Nuclear staining was performed with 7-AAD dye. Cross sections were obtained at 50um thickness. A) 63× imaging of an area. B,C) 20× imaging of a cross section from telencephalon. C shows the merged image. D) 20~× imaging of a negative control slice. Same protocol was applied except blocking solution was added instead of primary antibody. Z stacks are obtained from 12 (A) or 9 (B,C,D) sections . Scale bars represent 50um in A, 100um in B, C, and D. B’ and C^ are the magnified images of the yellow rectangles in B and C, representatively. BrdU is shown in white, 7-AAD in red color. Images were obtained in Zeiss LSM510. Composite images were created in ImageJ.

protocol_hierarchical.1.1: Prepare 10mg/mL stock BrdU solution in water.
protocol_hierarchical.1.2: Equilibrate the syringe with sterile water.
protocol_hierarchical.1.3: Put 0.2mg BrdU solution for 1g of body weight onto a parafilm and slowly take up by the syringe.
protocol_hierarchical.1.4: Transfer the fish from aquarium into an insulator ice bucket filled with aquarium water to half-length.
protocol_hierarchical.1.5: Start adding ice cubes that were made from the aquarium water until the temperature drops to 12°C.
protocol_hierarchical.1.6: Cool your fingertips in the 12°C water and gently handle the fish.
protocol_hierarchical.1.7: Inject the BrdU solution into the abdominal cavity.
protocol_hierarchical.1.8: Release the fish into 25°C system water and observe for 10 minutes.
protocol_hierarchical.1.9: Leave the fish in this tank for 4 to 24 hours.
protocol_hierarchical.2.1: Prepare icewater mixture: In an ice bucket, add 4 parts of ice and 1 part of water
protocol_hierarchical.2.2: Put the fish into the ice water, fish should be in the 4°C water completely.
protocol_hierarchical.2.3: After 10 minutes, when all the movement ceases, fish can be killed by cutting the head with a scalpel.
protocol_hierarchical.2.4: Put the head into the fixation buffer and incubate at 4°C, overnight.
protocol_hierarchical.3.1: Fill a 10cm dish with PBS and add the fish or the fish head into the PBS.
protocol_hierarchical.3.2: Perform the dissection by using the dumont tweezers under the dissecting microscope.
protocol_hierarchical.3.3: Add the dissected brain in PBS into a 2ml tube.
protocol_hierarchical.3.4: Wash the samples 3 times with PBS-T for 5 min each by carefully aspirating the medium on top and slowly adding the new medium.
protocol_hierarchical.3.5.1: Incubate the brains 4 min in 30% methanol in PBS-T
protocol_hierarchical.3.5.2: Incubate the brains 4 min in 50% methanol in PBS-T
protocol_hierarchical.3.5.3: Incubate the brains 4 min in 70% methanol in PBS-T
protocol_hierarchical.3.5.4: Incubate the brains 4 min in 90% methanol in PBS-T
protocol_hierarchical.3.5.5: Incubate the brains 4 min in 100% methanol
protocol_hierarchical.3.6: Add 100% methanol and incubate at -20°C, overnight.
protocol_hierarchical.4.1: Take the brains from -20°C, equilibrate with room temperature methanol by washing once.
protocol_hierarchical.4.2.1: Incubate the brains 4 min in 90% methanol in PBS-T
protocol_hierarchical.4.2.2: Incubate the brains 4 min in 70% methanol in PBS-T
protocol_hierarchical.4.2.3: Incubate the brains 4 min in 50% methanol in PBS-T
protocol_hierarchical.4.2.4: Incubate the brains 4 min in 30% methanol in PBS-T
protocol_hierarchical.4.2.5: Incubate the brains 4 min in PBS-T
protocol_hierarchical.4.3: Wash with PBS-T again and leave on the rotator for 15 min.
protocol_hierarchical.4.4: Prepare 3% agarose in PBS, allow to cool down to a temperature which the bottle can be hold in hand but still not solidified.
protocol_hierarchical.4.5: Transfer agarose into the moulds.
protocol_hierarchical.4.6: Pick each brain with the tweezers and carefully drop into the agarose.
protocol_hierarchical.4.7: With the help of bent syringe adjust the orientation of the brains quickly.
protocol_hierarchical.4.8: When the agarose has set, release the agarose from the mould.
protocol_hierarchical.4.9: Cut agarose into a small block in which the brain is at the center.
protocol_hierarchical.4.10: Attach agarose blocks to vibratome plate by using a strong, water-based adhesive.
protocol_hierarchical.4.11: Fill the vibratome tank with PBS, mount the plate into the tank.
protocol_hierarchical.4.13: Transfer each slice into a 48-well plate filled with PBS after each cutting.
protocol_hierarchical.5.1: Wash twice with PBS-T by aspirating the medium with a glass pasteur pipette, carefully.
protocol_hierarchical.5.2: Add 180 μL 2N HCl to each well and incubate at 37°C for 30 min without shaking.
protocol_hierarchical.5.3: Aspirate HCl and wash the wells with borate buffer, once.
protocol_hierarchical.5.4: Wash 3 times with PBS-T, 5 min each, on a rotator.
protocol_hierarchical.5.5: Incubate in blocking solution: 3% goat serum in PBS-T for 1 hour, at RT, on the rotator.
protocol_hierarchical.5.6: Prepare the anti-BrdU antibody in blocking solution at a 1:500 dilution.
protocol_hierarchical.5.7: Add 180μL antibody to each well except for the no antibody control.
protocol_hierarchical.5.8: Leave plates at 4°C, on a rotator, for 24 to 72 hours.
protocol_hierarchical.6.1: Take the plates out of fridge and discard the antibodies.
protocol_hierarchical.6.2: Wash 3 times with PBS-T, 5 min each.
protocol_hierarchical.6.3: Wash 2 times with PBS-T, 15 min each.
protocol_hierarchical.6.4: Incubate in blocking solution: 3% goat serum in PBS-T, for 30 min on a rotator.
protocol_hierarchical.6.5: Prepare the secondary antibody in PBS-T at 1:1000 dilution.
protocol_hierarchical.6.6: Incubate at room temperature, for 2 hours, on the rotator.
protocol_hierarchical.6.7: Discard the antibodies.
protocol_hierarchical.6.8: Wash 3 times with PBS-T, 5 min each.
protocol_hierarchical.6.9: Wash 2 times with PBS-T, 15 min each.
protocol_hierarchical.6.10: Add 180μL of 7-AAD working solution onto the wells.
protocol_hierarchical.6.11: Incubate for 10 min on a rotator.
protocol_hierarchical.6.13: Under a bright light with a black background, fill a 15cm dish with PBS.
protocol_hierarchical.6.14: Dip one microscope slide into it, wash with PBS.
protocol_hierarchical.6.15: Incline the slide to the edge of the plate, one end dipped in PBS.
protocol_hierarchical.6.16: Take two slices at a time from the experiment plate with the help of a brush and drop into the PBS in the dish.
protocol_hierarchical.6.17: With a no:0 brush, align the slices onto the microscope slide.
protocol_hierarchical.6.18: Wipe dry excess water, let the slices air dry but do not let them dry out completely.
protocol_hierarchical.6.19: Drop minute amounts of Prolong antifade reagent to each slice and also to the bottom of the slide.
protocol_hierarchical.6.20: Touch the coverslip to the edge of the slide and lower slowly onto the slices.
protocol_hierarchical.6.21: Leave the slides overnight at room temperature, in the dark.
protocol_hierarchical.6.22: Seal the edges with nail polish.

attached_document_content.3.2: Perform the dissection by using the dumont tweezers under the dissecting microscope.
attached_document_content.3.3: Add the dissected brain in PBS into a 2ml tube.
attached_document_content.3.4: Wash the samples 3 times with PBS-T for 5 min each by carefully aspirating the medium on top and slowly adding the new medium.
attached_document_content.3.5.1: Incubate the brains 4 min in 30% methanol in PBS-T
attached_document_content.3.5.2: Incubate the brains 4 min in 50% methanol in PBS-T
attached_document_content.3.5.3: Incubate the brains 4 min in 70% methanol in PBS-T
attached_document_content.3.5.4: Incubate the brains 4 min in 90% methanol in PBS-T
attached_document_content.3.5.5: Incubate the brains 4 min in 100% methanol
attached_document_content.3.6: Add 100% methanol and incubate at -20°C, overnight.
attached_document_content.4.1: Take the brains from -20°C, equilibrate with room temperature methanol by washing once.
attached_document_content.4.2.1: Incubate the brains 4 min in 90% methanol in PBS-T
attached_document_content.4.2.2: Incubate the brains 4 min in 70% methanol in PBS-T
attached_document_content.4.2.3: Incubate the brains 4 min in 50% methanol in PBS-T
attached_document_content.4.2.4: Incubate the brains 4 min in 30% methanol in PBS-T
attached_document_content.4.2.5: Incubate the brains 4 min in PBS-T
attached_document_content.4.3: Wash with PBS-T again and leave on the rotator for 15 min.
attached_document_content.4.4: Prepare 3% agarose in PBS, allow to cool down to a temperature which the bottle can be hold in hand but still not solidified.
attached_document_content.4.6: Pick each brain with the tweezers and carefully drop into the agarose.
attached_document_content.4.7: With the help of bent syringe adjust the orientation of the brains quickly.
attached_document_content.4.8: When the agarose has set, release the agarose from the mould.
attached_document_content.4.9: Cut agarose into a small block in which the brain is at the center.
attached_document_content.4.10: Attach agarose blocks to vibratome plate by using a strong, water-based adhesive.
attached_document_content.4.11: Fill the vibratome tank with PBS, mount the plate into the tank.
attached_document_content.4.13: Transfer each slice into a 48-well plate filled with PBS after each cutting.
attached_document_content.5.1: Wash twice with PBS-T by aspirating the medium with a glass pasteur pipette, carefully.
attached_document_content.5.2: Add 180 μL 2N HCl to each well and incubate at 37°C for 30 min without shaking.
attached_document_content.5.3: Aspirate HCl and wash the wells with borate buffer, once.
attached_document_content.5.4: Wash 3 times with PBS-T, 5 min each, on a rotator.
attached_document_content.5.5: Incubate in blocking solution: 3% goat serum in PBS-T for 1 hour, at RT, on the rotator.
attached_document_content.5.6: Prepare the anti-BrdU antibody in blocking solution at a 1:500 dilution.
attached_document_content.5.7: Add 180μL antibody to each well except for the no antibody control.
attached_document_content.5.8: Leave plates at 4°C, on a rotator, for 24 to 72 hours.
attached_document_content.6.1: Take the plates out of fridge and discard the antibodies.
attached_document_content.6.2: Wash 3 times with PBS-T, 5 min each.
attached_document_content.6.3: Wash 2 times with PBS-T, 15 min each.
attached_document_content.6.4: Incubate in blocking solution: 3% goat serum in PBS-T, for 30 min on a rotator.
attached_document_content.6.5: Prepare the secondary antibody in PBS-T at 1:1000 dilution.
attached_document_content.6.6: Incubate at room temperature, for 2 hours, on the rotator.
attached_document_content.6.8: Wash 3 times with PBS-T, 5 min each.
attached_document_content.6.9: Wash 2 times with PBS-T, 15 min each.
attached_document_content.6.10: Add 180μL of 7-AAD working solution onto the wells.
attached_document_content.6.11: Incubate for 10 min on a rotator.
attached_document_content.6.13: Under a bright light with a black background, fill a 15cm dish with PBS.
attached_document_content.6.14: Dip one microscope slide into it, wash with PBS.
attached_document_content.6.15: Incline the slide to the edge of the plate, one end dipped in PBS.
attached_document_content.6.16: Take two slices at a time from the experiment plate with the help of a brush and drop into the PBS in the dish.
attached_document_content.6.17: With a no:0 brush, align the slices onto the microscope slide.
attached_document_content.6.18: Wipe dry excess water, let the slices air dry but do not let them dry out completely.
attached_document_content.6.19: Drop minute amounts of Prolong antifade reagent to each slice and also to the bottom of the slide.
attached_document_content.6.20: Touch the coverslip to the edge of the slide and lower slowly onto the slices.
attached_document_content.6.21: Leave the slides overnight at room temperature, in the dark.
attached_document_content.6.22: Seal the edges with nail polish.

problem: Need for a reliable method to detect and analyze adult neurogenesis in zebrafish brain through BrdU labeling and immunohistochemical detection.
